visits.text: Discover Freiburg im Breisgau, the vibrant university city known for its sunny climate and historic Old Town in Germany's Black Forest region. The magnificent Gothic Münster cathedral dominates the Marktplatz, its 116-meter spire a masterpiece of medieval architecture. The intricate stonework and stunning stained glass windows have survived centuries largely intact.

Explore the unique Bächle, small water-filled runnels that flow through the streets and alleys of the Old Town. These medieval water channels, originally used for fire-fighting and cooling, now charm visitors as they trace their path through the historic center.

The Freiburger Münster (Freiburg Cathedral) is a Gothic architectural masterpiece whose construction spanned from 1200 to 1513—one of the rare examples of a cathedral entirely completed in the Middle Ages. Its openwork tower of 116 meters was described in 1869 by Swiss art historian Jacob Burckhardt as 'the most beautiful tower on earth.' The interior houses original medieval stained glass commissioned by the city's guilds, an altarpiece by Hans Baldung Grien, and an Annunciation by Hans Holbein the Younger. The cathedral is the only religious building in Germany to house five organs. Each morning, the Münsterplatz market sets up directly at the foot of the facade.

The Bächle are small open-air channels that wind through the old town streets since Freiburg's founding in 1120. Their total network reaches 15.5 km, of which 6.4 km are underground. Originally dug for water supply and firefighting, they run between the sidewalks and give the old town a unique atmosphere. According to local tradition, accidentally falling into a Bächle means you'll marry a Freiburger. The old town also preserves its two original medieval gates: the Martinstor (early 13th century) and the Schwabentor.

Ascent to Schlossberg, a wooded hill east of the Altstadt, accessible on foot or by funicular. Once the site of a medieval fortress since demolished, it offers from its panoramic tower a 360-degree view over the old town, the Rhine plain, the Vosges, and the foothills of the Black Forest.

activities.text: As the gateway to the Black Forest, Freiburg offers countless nearby hiking and cycling paths. The surrounding hills, covered in dark evergreen forests, gave the region its name and provide endless opportunities for outdoor exploration.

Wine tasting of Breisgau wines at the Münsterplatz market or in an old town cellar. The Freiburg region enjoys Germany's sunniest microclimate and produces renowned Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) and Gutedel.

days_plan.itin_title: Three-Country Alpine Tour: Basel, Black Forest, Alsace & Swiss Jura
Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: Experience Europa-Park in Rust, Germany's largest theme park and the second most visited in Europe after Disneyland Paris. With over 100 attractions and shows spread across 18 themed areas representing European countries, the park offers something for all ages. From high-speed roller coasters to fairy-tale worlds, each zone recreates the atmosphere of its respective nation.

The park features 13 roller coasters, some operating since 1984, demonstrating decades of innovation in theme park thrills. The Silver Star stands as one of Europe's tallest and fastest coasters, while family-friendly attractions ensure entertainment for younger visitors.

activities.text: With capacity for 60,000 guests daily, Europa-Park offers a full day of entertainment. The convenient RailCoaster direct train from Basel makes the journey easy, with shuttle buses connecting Ringsheim station to both Europa-Park and the Rulantica water world.

days_plan.itin_title: Three-Country Alpine Tour: Basel, Black Forest, Alsace & Swiss Jura

visits.text: Explore Schluchsee, the largest lake in the Baden-Württemberg region, impressing visitors with its exceptionally clear waters and surrounding natural beauty. Originally a natural lake, it was dammed in 1932 and now offers a pristine environment for water sports and lakeside relaxation. The forested shores remain largely undeveloped, preserving the wilderness character.

Discover the well-maintained hiking and cycling paths that circle the lake and extend into the surrounding Black Forest. The terrain offers routes for all abilities, from gentle lakeside strolls to challenging mountain trails with rewarding views.

activities.text: Enjoy swimming, sailing, and fishing in the clean waters, or rent a boat to explore the lake's quiet coves. This idyllic area attracts nature lovers and active holidaymakers throughout the year, with winter bringing cross-country skiing and skating.

days_plan.itin_title: Three-Country Alpine Tour: Basel, Black Forest, Alsace & Swiss Jura

visits.text: Visit Titisee-Neustadt in the heart of the Black Forest, famous for its crystal-clear lake and picturesque surroundings. The glacial lake, formed during the last ice age, stretches nearly two kilometers amid forested mountains. The charming lakeside promenade offers stunning views and access to boat rentals.

Discover the region's famous cuckoo clock tradition at one of the local clock factories. The Black Forest has been crafting these intricate timepieces since the 18th century, and you can watch master craftsmen create these iconic souvenirs using traditional techniques.

Relax at Titisee, one of the Black Forest's most beloved lakes nestled in a picture-perfect mountain setting. This natural glacial lake offers crystal-clear waters perfect for swimming, pedal boating or simply strolling along the scenic lakeside promenade. The surrounding forested hills and traditional Black Forest villages create postcard views in every direction.

Browse the shops along the lakefront for authentic Black Forest souvenirs, including the region's famous cuckoo clocks crafted by local clockmakers using traditional techniques. The mechanical artistry of these hand-carved timepieces represents generations of Black Forest craftsmanship passed down through families. Look for shops offering demonstrations of how these intricate clocks are assembled.

Take a boat ride across the glittering waters, either paddling yourself or aboard one of the tourist vessels that cruise the lake. The surrounding mountains are laced with hiking trails offering spectacular views down to the lake and across the Black Forest peaks. A visit to the Menzenschwand valley nearby reveals traditional villages and the famous Café zum Kuckuck.

activities.text: Enjoy a boat trip on the lake or simply relax by the shore. Numerous hiking and cycling routes wind through the surrounding forests, while wellness facilities offer relaxation. In winter, the area transforms into a winter sports destination with skiing, cross-country skiing, and ice skating.
Rent a pedal boat or rowboat to explore the lake at your own pace and enjoy the mountain scenery

days_plan.itin_title: Three-Country Alpine Tour: Basel, Black Forest, Alsace & Swiss Jura
Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Ten life-size wooden atlantes support the gallery of the Stiftsbibliothek in Waldsassen. Carved by Karl Stilp between 1724 and 1726, they embody human failings: the ignorant, the hypocrite, the mocker. The Rococo hall ranks among the finest monastic libraries in Central Europe. Admission €3.50, guided tours every 45 minutes.

The adjoining Stiftsbasilika dates from the 12th century and blends late Romanesque with Baroque. The ceiling frescoes by Karl Hofreiter trace the life of St. Bernard of Clairvaux. Free access outside service hours. Take a look at the carved choir stalls.

The carved figures supporting the Stiftsbibliothek gallery aren't saints, but allegories of vice: vanity, hypocrisy, ignorance. Karl Stilp carved them life-size in 1724-25. The rococo hall, with its painted ceilings, is one of Germany's most beautiful baroque libraries. Guided tours only, Tue-Sun.

The Stiftsbasilika houses reliquaries of skeletons dressed and adorned with jewels, among the most remarkable in Europe. An 18th-century Cistercian goldsmith, Adalbart Eder, decked these Roman bones in silk and gemstones. The Jann organ counts 103 stops.

activities.text: Wander through the monastery's botanical garden (Naturerlebnisgarten), open from May to mid-October. The Cistercian nuns cultivate medicinal plants and aromatic herbs across one hectare. Combined ticket with the library €6.

Sample the cooking at Gästehaus Sankt Joseph, attached to the monastery. The Cistercian nuns run the dining room with regional dishes that change daily. Moderate prices, monastic atmosphere. Reservations recommended on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: West Bohemia in 10 days: Spas, Castles & Beer
Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Ahlbeck's Seebrücke, with its 1882 pavilion, is the grande dame of German piers. It's a listed historic monument. Direct view of the Polish coast. At its foot, the 'Polenmarkt'—border market—offers textiles and crafts at reduced prices.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Around 1900, painter Paul Müller-Kaempff founded Ahrenshoop's artists' colony. Thatched-roof houses, narrow lanes, galleries everywhere. The Kunstmuseum Ahrenshoop, whose buildings echo fishermen's houses, is worth the visit. Warning: high-season prices climb.

activities.text: Walk the steep cliff of Hohes Ufer westward: the coast crumbles into the sea, wild landscape steps from the village. Marked trail, an hour round-trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: The Alpsee, just below Hohenschwangau, is paradoxically calm despite Neuschwanstein's crowds. Crystal-clear water, alpine setting, no motors allowed. A bench and twenty minutes of silence will do.

activities.text: Rent a Tretboot (pedal boat) on the Alpsee to see both castles from a different angle. The water is so clear you can see the bottom several meters down. Rentals at the kiosk on the north side.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: No bell tower, no town around it, yet it's definitely a Dom. Altenberg Cathedral rises from deep forest in the Dhünn valley. A Cistercian church founded in 1133, rebuilt in Gothic style between 1259 and 1379, it has served both Protestant and Catholic congregations since 1857. Free admission, closed Mondays.

activities.text: Follow the short path that runs along the Dhünn downstream from the cathedral (2 km round trip, flat). The stream flows between beeches and ferns in a silence only Cistercian monks would have known. A café-restaurant sits just opposite the portal.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Hard to resist the Stadtbrille, Amberg's emblem: this 46-meter fortified passage has straddled the Vils since 1454. Its central pillar and the water create a reflection shaped like eyeglasses. Best angle: from outside the wall, south side.

Five craft breweries still supply Amberg's Wirtshäuser, nicknamed the "Bierstadt." The Marktplatz, with its Gothic Rathaus, the four medieval gates and the nearly intact wall form one of Bavaria's finest fortified ensembles.
Climb up to the Mariahilfberg for the plunging view over the old town and its tiled roofs. The Franciscan convent and the Lorettokapelle crown the hill. Free access, 15 minutes' walk from the center.

activities.text: If the Vils is calm, try a ride in a Plätte, a flat-bottomed boat recalling the transport of iron ore in the Middle Ages. Amberg was then the "Ruhr region of the Middle Ages." Departure at the Schiffbrücke.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Ansbach's Residenz surprises through its interior: more than 50 rooms in enfilade decorated in Baroque and Rococo styles, with 2,800 Delft faïence tiles in the faïence room according to the castle's count. Residence of the Margraves of Brandenburg-Ansbach in the 17th-18th centuries, it's less known than Würzburg and can be explored without jostling.

Ansbach's Stiftskirche St. Gumbert houses the Ansbach Hohenzollern nobility's burial vault and the Gothic chapter stalls. Just opposite, the Johanniskirche and the Kaspar-Hauser-Denkmal recall the enigmatic wild child found in Ansbach in 1828 - one of the 19th century's unsolved cases.

activities.text: Stroll along the Promenade, a tree-lined avenue in the heart of town, and reach the Hofgarten behind the Residenz. On Fridays, the Marktplatz weekly market gathers regional producers. Ansbach is also the starting point of the Rangau-Radweg, a well-marked cycling path for those extending the stop.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Pass beneath the Glockenturm, a medieval keep turned baroque bell tower after two fires (1600 and 1709). It's Arnsberg's emblem. The old town coils around the Ruhr's loop, with half-timbered houses and remnants of ramparts. The Kaiserspförtchen, the last medieval gate, deserves a detour.

The Sauerland Museum in the Landsberger Hof covers regional history from prehistory to the 20th century. The building itself is worth seeing: a palace built in 1605 by the Prince-Elector for his mistress. Admission €6, closed Mondays.

The ruins of Arnsberg Castle overlook the Ruhr loop from their wooded promontory. Only wall fragments and a tower remain, but the valley view is wide. Free access, 15 minutes on foot from Alter Markt.

activities.text: Arnsberg feels very medieval, yet its classicist quarter around Neumarkt dates from 1816, designed by architect Schinkel when the Prussians made it an administrative seat. The contrast between the two atmospheres can be walked in twenty minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Make a quick stop in this small town tied to porcelain. Arzberg long produced tableware known throughout Germany. The village retains a discreet charm with its Franconian houses and hilltop church. Fifteen minutes suffices to tour the center.

activities.text: A discreet little town, Arzberg is mainly worth a stopover between Hohenberg and Waldsassen. If a porcelain factory outlet is open, prices run well below those at Selb boutiques.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Nine degrees Celsius year-round, even in August: the Atta Cave refreshes and fascinates. Discovered by chance in 1907 during a quarry blast, this grotto unfolds stalactites, stalagmites, and calcite draperies colored by iron oxide across 500 meters of developed pathways. The guided tour lasts 40 minutes. Cheese is matured in a sealed gallery.

The Biggesee lacks wild charm, but it's Westphalia's largest reservoir, and the boat ride from Attendorn to Biggedamm offers a restful panorama over the wooded hills of southern Sauerland. Embarkation at Biggedamm, sailings April to October.

activities.text: Take the Biggolino, a nostalgic glazed miniature train linking Atta Cave to Biggedamm via downtown Attendorn. The journey lasts 20 minutes, schedules sync with boat departures. Cash payment only on board.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Augsburg, founded in 15 BCE by Emperor Augustus's legions, is one of Germany's oldest cities. During the Renaissance, it became one of Europe's financial capitals: the Fugger and Welser dynasties of merchants and bankers lent money to Emperors Maximilian I and Charles V. It was in Augsburg that the Augsburg Confession, the founding charter of Lutheranism, was read in 1530, and where the Peace of Augsburg was signed in 1555, officially recognizing the coexistence of religions within the Empire. The city is also the birthplace of Leopold Mozart, the composer's father, and writer Bertolt Brecht. It's the only city in Germany with its own public holiday: on August 8th, the Peace Festival commemorates the end of Protestant oppression after the Thirty Years' War.

The Fuggerei, founded in 1521 by Jakob Fugger—considered the richest man of his time—is the world's oldest social housing complex still in operation. Behind its walls and blue-and-white-striped portal, 67 ochre-façaded houses contain 142 apartments of 60 square meters each. The annual rent, fixed in 1521 at one Rhenish guilder, has never changed: today it equals €0.88. In return, residents must be Catholic, native to Augsburg, in financial need, and recite three daily prayers for the founders. A curfew remains in effect at 10 PM. Mozart's great-great-grandfather, mason Franz Mozart, lived here from 1681 to 1694—a commemorative plaque marks the spot. A model apartment and a 1943 bunker can be visited.

The Town Hall (Rathaus), designed by architect Elias Holl and completed in 1620, is considered the most important secular Renaissance building north of the Alps. It was the world's first building with more than six stories. Its Golden Hall (Goldener Saal), finished in 1643, impresses with its 14-meter-high coffered ceiling covered in gold leaf, murals, and marble floor. Everything was destroyed by 1944 bombings but faithfully reconstructed. Right next door, the Perlach Tower (Perlachturm), standing 70 meters tall, offers panoramas across the entire city and, on clear days, to the Alps.

Stroll down Maximilianstraße, the city's finest avenue, laid out along the ancient Roman Via Claudia Augusta. Three monumental bronze fountains punctuate the promenade: the Augustus Fountain (1588-1594) by Dutch sculptor Hubert Gerhard, the Mercury Fountain (1596-1599), and the Hercules Fountain (1597-1600) by Adriaen de Vries. These fountains are part of Augsburg's water management system, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2019—an ensemble of 22 hydraulic monuments spanning more than 700 years of history. At No. 36, the Fugger Palace features a Renaissance arcaded courtyard; at No. 46, the Schaezler Palace (1770) houses the most important gallery of German Baroque art and a Rococo ballroom.

Augsburg Cathedral (Dom), whose oldest parts date back to 994, blends Romanesque and Gothic architecture. It preserves the world's oldest known figurative stained glass windows, dating from the 11th century: the Prophets—Daniel, Jonah, Hosea, Moses, and David—installed in the nave's south windows. The Romanesque bronze door, considered the cathedral's oldest work of art, is displayed in the adjoining diocesan museum.

activities.text: Settle into one of the downtown breweries to try Käsespätzle, gratinated fresh cheese noodles, a Swabian specialty from Augsburg. The city, on the border between Bavaria and Swabia, offers cuisine drawing from both traditions—complete your meal with Leberkäse (meatloaf) or Zwetschgendatschi (plum tart).

Attend a performance at the Augsburger Puppenkiste, the famous marionette theater founded in 1943. Housed in the former Holy Spirit Hospital, this string puppet theater stages fairy tales for children as well as adult productions, with an attention to detail that has made it renowned throughout Germany.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria
Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Arrive via the right bank of the Rhine—the road follows the river between Rüdesheim and Bacharach, offering a succession of terraced vineyards, perched castle ruins, and half-timbered villages characteristic of the UNESCO World Heritage Upper Middle Rhine Valley. Before reaching Bacharach, stop at Rüdesheim for the Drosselgasse, a 144-meter cobbled alley entirely lined with wine taverns and beer gardens. The wine served is mainly Rheingau Riesling.

Ride the cable car (Seilbahn) up to the Niederwald Monument, a bronze statue of Germania built between 1877 and 1883 from sculptor Johann Schilling's design, commemorating the 1870-71 victory and German Empire unification. The ensemble measures 38 meters in height and offers panoramic views over the Rhine and Rheingau vineyards.

Enter Bacharach through the ancient fortified gates. The 14th-century ramparts are among the best preserved in the entire Rhine valley. Walk along the rampart path to see the slate roofs, vineyards, and Rhine below from above. The village is first mentioned in a document dated 923; its name evokes Bacchus, god of wine, whose cult already existed in Celtic and Roman times.

The Gothic ruins of Wernerkapelle, halfway up the slope between the village and Stahleck Castle. The chapel was begun in 1289 in memory of Werner of Oberwesel, a 16-year-old boy murdered in 1287. Its construction was never completed, interrupted by pogroms against the local Jewish community, falsely accused of the murder. A plaque today commemorates this past. The partially preserved Gothic structure remains striking despite the centuries.

Stahleck Castle dominates Bacharach from a ridge 160 meters above the Rhine. Its first mention dates to 1135. Destroyed by French troops during the War of the Palatine Succession in 1689, it was reconstructed in 1925 and now houses a youth hostel. Access to the inner courtyard is free—from the terrace, the view over the Rhine and valley meanders is one of the finest in the area.

activities.text: Riesling tasting at one of the village wine estates. Bacharach has produced wine since Roman times. Legend has it that Pope Pius II had it shipped by cart all the way to Rome.

Short Rhine cruise from Bacharach pier to Kaub, to see from the water the fortress of Pfalzgrafenstein perched on an island in the middle of the river—a former medieval customs post still intact. Round trip about 1h15.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Bavaria's smallest Heilbad fits in a handkerchief. Bad Alexandersbad owes its existence to a spring discovered by a farmer in 1734. The last margrave of Ansbach built a thermal palace here. Today, the village counts barely 960 inhabitants and absolute calm.

The Markgräfliches Schloss, built in 1783 for the margrave, dominates the Kurpark with its terraces refurbished in 2019. Queen Louise of Prussia stayed here in 1805, before lending her name to the neighboring labyrinth. Free access to the park.

activities.text: If your joints are creaking, the ALEXBAD offers a modern panoramic pool grafted onto the historic 1838 Kurhaus. Sauna, local moor treatments, minimalist architecture. Open from 10 a.m., entry €15-20.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Doberan Minster, from a Cistercian monastery founded in 1171, isn't a city cathedral but a country abbey church—which makes its interior even more astonishing. The Gothic red-brick construction began around 1280 and concluded with the consecration of 1386. The 76-m-long basilica houses a gilded canopy choir from the 14th century and the tombs of Mecklenburg princes. Visit in 45 min.

A small spa town since 1793, gigantic medieval heritage: Bad Doberan is this permanent contradiction. Cistercian builders shaped Baltic Backsteingotik techniques here for over a century, and their methods spread throughout the North Sea region. The Visitor Center in the Marstall explains this influence with a model of the 1550 monastery.

activities.text: Climb aboard the Molli, a narrow-gauge steam train connecting Bad Doberan to Kühlungsborn since 1886, passing through the town's shopping streets—yes, the train literally runs between the houses. The full journey takes about 45 min and hugs the Baltic coast. Ticket at Bad Doberan station.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: The Stiftsruine of Bad Hersfeld is the world's largest Romanesque church ruin, stretching over 100 meters in length with a transept span exceeding 50 meters. The Benedictine abbey was founded in 769 by Lullus, Archbishop of Mainz, on a site where the monk Sturmius had settled as a hermit in 736. Charlemagne made it an imperial abbey that served for centuries as a pillar of royal power on the eastern frontier of the Frankish kingdom. The church was destroyed by the French in 1761 during the Seven Years' War, when it was being used as a powder magazine. The Romanesque Saint Catherine's Tower, dating from 1120, still stands and houses the Lullusglocke—a bell dated 1038, Germany's oldest dated bell still in use. Since 1951, the ruins have hosted the Bad Hersfeld Festival, one of Germany's most important theater festivals, every summer.

The historic center of Bad Hersfeld, a Hessian spa town, has preserved a fabric of 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses. The sacristan's house, dating from 1452, is the town's oldest. The Stadtkirche (town church) and the former buildings of the free imperial city form a cohesive ensemble around the pedestrian center.

activities.text: Attend the Bad Hersfeld Festival, an annual theater festival held each summer in the Stiftsruine ruins since 1951. The open-air stage, set against nine-century-old Romanesque walls, hosts large-scale productions (Shakespeare, Schiller, musicals) with up to 1,500 spectators per evening.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: The Grosser Feldberg peaks at 879 meters, the highest point in the Taunus and the state of Hesse. On clear days, the summit view extends to the Alps and Cologne Cathedral. The ascent through beech and spruce forests of the Naturpark Hochtaunus offers a dramatic shift from the Main River plain. Several marked trails lead to the summit from surrounding villages.

The Naturpark Hochtaunus covers 240,000 hectares of forests, valleys, and ridges between the Main and Lahn rivers. It's one of Hesse's largest nature parks. The trail network exceeds 3,000 km. The vegetation is typically Rhenish: oak, beech, and hornbeam groves on the slopes, with some glacial relict species on the ridges.

The historic center of Bad Homburg vor der Höhe retains its 17th-century Schloss—one of the few remaining residences of the Landgraves of Hesse-Homburg still standing—with its medieval white tower that has become the town's emblem. The Kurpark, landscaped in English style with century-old avenues, ranks among Germany's finest spa parks. In the 19th century, Bad Homburg was a fashionable resort for European nobility: Tsar Nicholas II, King Edward VII, and Emperor Wilhelm II were regular visitors. The first Monte Carlo casino is said to have been founded by businessmen who learned the trade here.

The Roman fort of Saalburg, in the Taunus a few kilometers from Bad Homburg, is the only fully reconstructed fort along the Germanic Limes. Built in the late 1st century to house a cohort of 500 soldiers, it was abandoned during the Alamannic raids of 258 AD. Serious excavations began in 1853. In 1897, Wilhelm II—passionate about Roman history—ordered its complete reconstruction on the original foundations, an unprecedented project for a military installation. The site has been part of the UNESCO Upper Germanic-Raetian Limes inscription since 2005. The archaeological museum displays weapons, tools, and everyday objects found on site.

activities.text: Spa session at the Taunus Therme in Bad Homburg, a complex featuring over 1,400 m² of pools and eight themed saunas. Bad Homburg's springs have been used therapeutically since the 17th century.

Visit the Hessenpark open-air museum in Neu-Anspach, about ten kilometers from Bad Homburg. Over 100 historic buildings—half-timbered houses, barns, mills, workshops—have been dismantled and reassembled across 70 hectares. The site offers a tangible picture of Hessian rural architecture from the 16th to 19th centuries.

Hike the Limes trail from Saalburg: portions of the fortifications (reconstructed ditch and palisade) remain visible in the Taunus landscape. A 2.4 km circular trail around the fort runs along preserved sections and marks the bases of former watchtowers.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: The Rittersaal of Schloss Iburg is one of the first baroque halls in northwestern Germany, with trompe-l'oeil frescoes considered the last of their kind north of the Alps. Sophie Charlotte, first Queen of Prussia and grandmother of Frederick the Great, was born here in 1668.

Descend to the Charlottensee for a quiet break. This lake at the castle's foot bears the princess's name. You can rent rowboats in season. The adjacent Kneipp-Erlebnispark offers water-walking basins.

activities.text: If the Baumwipfelpfad is open, the Teutoburger Wald canopy reveals itself at treetop height. Otherwise, the marked Hermannsweg trail crosses Bad Iburg with easy sections. Count on an hour for a short loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Immaculate white townhouses mirror themselves in the Baroque harbour basin — Bad Karlshafen is a planned town, drafted in one stroke and commissioned in 1699. Landgrave Charles I of Hesse wanted to shelter French Huguenots expelled by Louis XIV, and to bypass the toll at Hann. Münden. The canal intended to link it to the Rhine was never completed: only 17 kilometres were ever dug.

Founded in 1699 by Charles I of Hesse under the name Sieburg, Bad Karlshafen sits at the precise confluence of the Diemel and the Weser. The Huguenot Museum, housed in a former cigar factory, traces the exile of French Protestants from 1685 onward. Free admission on the first Sunday of the month.

activities.text: Head up to the Weser-Skywalk, a 3km walk from the centre: this platform on the Hannoversche Klippen cliffs offers a view across three German states simultaneously — Hesse, North Rhine-Westphalia, and Lower Saxony. It's also accessible by car, with a small car park nearby.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Stroll along the graduation wall of Bad Kösen, an impressive wooden structure where salt water flows over blackthorn bundles. This traditional spa installation creates iodine-enriched air with invigorating properties, perpetuating a centuries-old wellness tradition. Various viewpoints over the spa town reveal a hilly landscape typical of the Saale-Unstrut region.

Discover this charming spa town nestled in the Saale valley, at the gateway to Germany's northernmost wine-growing region. The Belle Époque villa architecture testifies to the golden age of German spa culture in the 19th century. The surrounding vine-planted hills offer bucolic panoramas over this preserved landscape.

activities.text: Walk through the spa park gardens and discover the spa architecture

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Bad Langensalza owes its medieval prosperity to woad trade - a blue dye it shared with only four other Thuringian cities - enabling burghers to build grand churches and travertine ramparts. Later, the exploitation of mineral springs made it a spa town. Today, the city is known for its ten themed parks and gardens aligned along the spa promenade. The Japanese garden «Kofuku no Niwa» («garden of happiness») combines two classical pavilions, a koi carp pond, a dry garden, a bamboo grove, and cherry trees that blossom in spring.

Bad Langensalza's rose garden is one of Thuringia's most significant. It showcases collections of antique and modern roses organized in themed beds, complemented by a small rose museum tracing this flower's cultural history.

The arboretum gathers rare or spectacular trees and shrubs, native and exotic species, planted in thematic collections. Adjacent, the botanical garden displays medicinal and officinal plants recalling the town's spa tradition.

Bad Langensalza's old town is one of Thuringia's best-preserved: an almost entirely conserved medieval wall, half-timbered houses from the 15th and 16th centuries restored since 1990, and several grand churches testifying to the past wealth of the woad trade.

activities.text: Relaxation at the Friederiken Therme, a thermal spa fed by Bad Langensalza's sulphurous and saline springs. The complex features indoor and outdoor pools and several themed saunas.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Forget the neatly trimmed French gardens: Muskau Park plays a different tune. Prince Hermann von Pückler-Muskau designed it from 1815 onward as a living painting, with distant vistas and winding paths. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2004, it sprawls across roughly 830 hectares straddling the German-Polish border. Allow at least two hours for the palace park, and arrive early in the morning: the low-angle light on the Neisse River makes the early wake-up worthwhile.

Rebuilt after the war, the Neues Schloss houses a permanent exhibition on Pückler, an unclassifiable character: writer, traveler, landscape architect, and notorious seducer. The facade regained its appearance in 2011 after a lengthy restoration. Climb the tower for a panorama over the park and town. Detail worth knowing: the tower exhibition and keep tour close occasionally, so check the dates before visiting.

activities.text: Rent a bicycle at the Schlossvorwerk or hop into a horse-drawn carriage: Pückler himself recommended touring the park by carriage, so you might as well follow the master's advice. Carriages depart from the palace forecourt. The network of paths is extensive and the park immense, so two wheels save precious time. Avoid high noon in summer—shade is scarce on the vast lawns.

If you have time, cross the Neisse via the Doppelbrücke and enter the Polish side, into Park Mużakowski. Same park, two countries: the majority of the 830 hectares lie east of the river, in Poland. This double border footbridge was erected in 2003. Bring your ID just in case, and good walking shoes, because distances stretch out quickly on the far bank.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Designed to make salt, Bad Rothenfelde's Gradierwerke now serve as an open-air thermal cure. Salt water trickles down 412 meters of blackthorn walls, creating an iodine-rich aerosol. You'd think you were on the North Sea coast, 200 km from the shore.

In the Inhalationskammer, a salt fog so dense you can't see your hands: slip on the plastic cape sold at the entrance (€1) and breathe for ten to twenty minutes. Effective against allergies, according to regulars.

activities.text: Climb the 55 steps of the Besucherplattform on the New Gradierwerk to see the reconstructed Windkunst, the wind pump that lifted the brine. The view of the Kurpark and the rosarium of 210 varieties is a bonus. Guided tours from Easter to September.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Second baroque library of the journey: the Bibliothekssaal at Schussenried Abbey rivals Wiblingen's. Painter Franz Georg Hermann and sculptor Fidelis Sporer created another rococo summit here. The great abbeys of Upper Swabia competed for prestige through their libraries.
The spa center at Bad Schussenried offers baths and a cure park. The small town provides a quiet stop between abbeys. The Bierkrugmuseum, dedicated to beer steins, is a local curiosity.

activities.text: You're at the heart of the Upper Swabian Baroque Route, a tourist trail linking some thirty baroque churches and monasteries between Ulm and Lake Constance. Map available from the tourist office.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: The painted façades of Bad Tölz's Marktstraße line up Lüftlmalereien, alpine gables, and wrought-iron signs along 500 meters. It's postcard Bavaria, in pedestrian, living form. Tölz locals call this street the "loveliest living room of the Oberland."

Gabriel von Seidl, a Munich architect, redesigned the Marktstraße façades between 1900 and 1910 in alpine style. The 15th-century Altes Rathaus and its onion-dome tower are the landmark. Thomas Mann used to vacation here.

activities.text: If you're traveling with children, the gravel banks of the Isar at Bad Tölz are a natural playground. The water is cold but crystal clear, and the ducks provide the show. The Taubenloch playground is right next door.
Tölz locals say the Isar is their municipal pool. Cross the bridge and settle on the pebbles of the right bank. Turquoise water rushes between gravel bars. Bring a towel—no changing cabins.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Take the Schwarzwaldhochstrasse (B500) to reach Baden-Baden via the heights—60 kilometers of forests and ridges, this is the panoramic route through the northern Black Forest. Take it from the south when arriving from Triberg.

Descend to the old town to see the Roman bath ruins: beneath the market square, excavations discovered in 1847 unearthed baths built between 213 and 217 AD for Emperor Caracalla. Baden-Baden's springs gush from 2,000 meters deep at temperatures between 56 and 68 degrees—800,000 liters per day emerge from twelve artesian springs aged 12,000 to 17,000 years. The Romans called the town Aquae Aureliae.

Book a session at the Friedrichsbad, built between 1869 and 1877 by architect Karl Dernfeld. Inspired by Roman baths and Irish baths, it offers 17 thermal stations under a 17.5-meter dome. Mark Twain visited and wrote: "After 10 minutes you forget time; after 20 minutes, the world." The bathing tradition here is mixed and clothing-free.

End the day with a walk along the Lichtentaler Allee, which follows the Oos River for 2.3 kilometers between the town center and the Cistercian abbey of Lichtenthal founded in 1245. This tree-lined avenue of centuries-old trees was in the 19th century the open-air salon of Europe—Napoleon III, Queen Victoria, Dostoevsky and Brahms all stayed here.

activities.text: The modern Caracalla Therme, opened in 1985 over 5,000 square meters, allows you to enjoy the thermal waters with swimwear. Several pools at temperatures between 18 and 38 degrees, outdoor pool open year-round, saunas and steam baths. More accessible than the Friedrichsbad, with or without reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Marvel at Bamberg, one of Germany's most beautiful cities, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This millennial city miraculously spared by bombing has more than 2,300 buildings classified as historic monuments. Built on seven hills like Rome, it harmoniously blends medieval, Baroque and Rococo architecture in an ensemble of exceptional coherence.

Photograph the Altes Rathaus, the old town hall built on an artificial island in the middle of the Regnitz River, Bavaria's most iconic monument. Its trompe-l'oeil frescoes adorning the half-timbered facades create the illusion of three-dimensional sculptures. According to legend, when the bishop refused to cede land, the burghers created this island to build their town hall.

Visit the four-towered Imperial Cathedral, a Romanesque masterpiece housing the tomb of Emperor Henry II and the famous Bamberg Horseman, the first life-size equestrian statue of the Middle Ages. Little Venice, the former fishermen's quarter with colorful houses reflected in the river, completes this enchanting picture.

activities.text: Tasting of Rauchbier, the smoked beer that is a unique local specialty in the world

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Bastei is Saxon Switzerland's iconic rock formation—sandstone columns sculpted by erosion over a million years, dominating the Elbe by 194 meters from a plateau at 305 meters altitude. The Bastei Bridge, built in sandstone in 1851 to replace a first wooden bridge from 1826, links several of these rocky pinnacles over 76.5 meters with its seven arches. Caspar David Friedrich painted the rocks as early as 1822. In season, crowds are dense from 10 a.m.—arrive early for the best views over the Elbe's meanders.

The ruins of Neurathen Castle, on the very site of the Bastei rocks, are the vestiges of Saxon Switzerland's largest rock fortress. Built by the Sorbs at the top of the ridge over 700 meters, it was conquered and burned in 1469. Metal walkways now allow circulation between the rock crevices and ruins.

activities.text: Hike from the village of Rathen to the Bastei Bridge and surrounding rocks. The departure is from the right bank of the Elbe after crossing by ferry (a few minutes), then a climb of about 40 minutes through the forest. Several trails allow for varied returns.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: On its granite spur overlooking the Spree, the Ortenburg has watched over Bautzen for centuries, the former fortress of the Slavic Milzener tribe. Today it houses the Sorbisches Museum, dedicated to the Sorbian minority, and the regional administrative court. Climb the Matthiasturm for the view, accessible on guided tour. Start the day here, before afternoon crowds arrive.

Built in 1558, the Alte Wasserkunst supplied Bautzen with water from the Spree. This tall round tower of 47 meters, now a technical museum, retains its old pumping machinery. For the price of 184 steps, the platform offers the finest panorama over the old town and river. Open year-round, with reduced hours in winter: check before climbing.

Far from Pisa, Bautzen hides its own leaning tower: the Reichenturm leans roughly 1.4 meters to the northwest, standing 56 meters tall. From its platform, the gaze plunges onto the old town's roofs and the famous tower skyline. It opens April through October, during the day. Combine it with a loop of towers and bastions that summarizes a thousand years of history.

Push open the door of Dom St. Petri, one of Germany's oldest and largest 'simultaneous' churches: Catholics and Protestants share the same building, separated by a grille. It's the only church in Saxony to function this way, with two organs, one per denomination. The calm here contrasts with the bustle of the lanes. Free entry, but respect services—the church remains an active place of worship.

activities.text: Bautzen mustard deserves the gourmet detour: at the Senfmuseum, a small shop-museum, you taste local varieties far spicier than the industrial version. It's the perfect edible souvenir to slip into your bag. Tasting takes a few minutes, perfect between tower visits. Think about a jar or two for home—they travel well.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Wagner refused this hall. Too gilded, too Baroque, too worldly for his dramas. Yet the Markgräfliches Opernhaus is one of Europe's best-preserved theater halls - carved, lacquered, and gilded wood across three tiers of boxes, designed by Giuseppe Galli Bibiena and his son Carlo between 1744 and 1748. Listed as UNESCO World Heritage in 2012 after restoration. The Friday organ performance at 10:30 a.m. is the most affordable entry.

The Festspielhaus built in 1872 on the Grüner Hügel, north of the center, was designed exclusively for Wagner's works: stage equipped with three levels of machinery beneath the platform, acoustics conceived for grand orchestrations, no decoration in the hall to avoid distraction. The laying of the first stone was marked by Wagner himself conducting Beethoven's Ninth Symphony in the Markgräfliches Opernhaus.

Bayreuth's Eremitage, the hideaway of Margravine Wilhelmine - the sister of Frederick the Great who gave the town its opera house and Baroque character - is a 50-hectare park 4 km from the center. Fountains, artificial grottos, the 17th-century Altes Schloss, and the Neues Schloss with its round temple: the ensemble is listed with the Markgräfliches Opernhaus within the same UNESCO perimeter.

activities.text: Stop by Villa Wahnfried and its Richard Wagner Museum: the composer's birthplace home, transformed into a biographical museum after post-1945 reconstruction, is the starting point of the Wagner Walk linking the town's key sites related to his stay. Wagner's and Cosima's graves lie in the villa's garden.

If you have seven years' lead time, try registering for the Bayreuther Festspiele (Wagner festival, every summer since 1876) - the waiting list is long but not impossible via direct registration at bayreuther-festspiele.de. Otherwise, the Festspielhaus offers daily guided tours outside the festival period.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Behold the Königssee, the absolute jewel of German lakes, set between 1,800-meter vertical cliffs in the heart of Berchtesgaden National Park. This fjord-like lake with exceptionally pure emerald-green waters can only be explored by silent electric boats, perpetuating a century-old ecological tradition.

Navigate to St. Bartholomew's pilgrimage church, a Baroque gem with red onion domes perched on a peninsula at the foot of the vertiginous Watzmann wall. The legendary echo of the captain's trumpet resonating seven times against the cliffs is a magical moment of the crossing. This site of surreal beauty ranks among Germany's most photographed.

Hike in Berchtesgaden National Park, Germany's only Alpine national park, classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve. This natural sanctuary is home to chamois, marmots and golden eagles in a setting of jagged peaks, tumultuous waterfalls and flowering alpine meadows. The town of Berchtesgaden itself charms with its preserved Bavarian authenticity.

activities.text: Electric boat cruise on Königssee

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Brandenburg Gate was built between 1788 and 1791 on the orders of King Frederick William II of Prussia, designed by Carl Gotthard Langhans and inspired by the Propylaea of the Athens Acropolis. During Berlin's division, it stood trapped in the border strip, inaccessible to Berliners on both sides. It was here, in front of this gate, that Reagan issued his 1987 challenge to Gorbachev. Visit in the late afternoon when the floodlights come on—the way light catches the columns completely transforms the atmosphere.

The Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe occupies 19,000 square meters between Brandenburg Gate and Potsdamer Platz, on what was once the death strip of the Wall. New York architect Peter Eisenman placed 2,711 concrete stelae of varying heights on gently undulating ground—as you walk toward the center, the blocks rise above your shoulders and the ground drops away, creating a deliberate sense of oppression. Inaugurated on May 10, 2005, the memorial includes an 800-square-meter underground information center: reading aloud all the victims' names would take 6 years, 7 months, and 27 days.

Head to Friedrichshain to walk along the East Side Gallery, the longest remaining section of the Berlin Wall: 1,316 meters along Mühlenstraße, between Ostbahnhof and the Oberbaumbrücke bridge. Starting in January 1990, 118 artists from 21 countries came to paint the eastern side of the Wall. The most famous murals: the fraternal kiss between Brezhnev and Honecker by Dmitri Vrubel, inscribed with "My God, help me survive this deadly love"; and the blue Trabant breaking through concrete, painted by Birgit Kinder. The entire gallery was restored in 2009 for the 20th anniversary of the Wall's fall.

Karl-Marx-Allee, a 2-kilometer Stalinist boulevard laid out between 1952 and 1960, offers a concrete glimpse into East German monumental urbanism. The buildings lining it—in the so-called "Soviet wedding cake" style—are now protected as historic monuments and represent one of the best-preserved Communist-era architectural ensembles in Europe.

On the way back to Berlin, stop in Waren an der Müritz, gateway to Müritz National Park. Lake Müritz, at 117 square kilometers, is the largest lake entirely within Germany—its shores are lined with wet meadows where ospreys and gray cranes nest.

Neustrelitz deserves a brief stop for its baroque gardens laid out in the 18th century around the summer residence of the Dukes of Mecklenburg-Strelitz. The town was designed according to a star-shaped plan radiating from the castle—now vanished—with eight avenues intersecting at a central point.

activities.text: Tiergarten park stretches across 210 hectares in the heart of Berlin—it was the royal hunting ground before being converted into a public park in the 19th century. Rent a bike to cross it from end to end, or push on to the former Tempelhof Airport, now converted into a vast public space where Berliners picnic on the old runways.

On Sundays, Mauerpark in Prenzlauer Berg hosts one of Berlin's largest flea markets, with a giant open-air karaoke session in the afternoon. More compact, the Arkonaplatz market is perfect for hunting down GDR-era objects and vintage furniture.

Final evening in Berlin: climb the Television Tower at Alexanderplatz (368 meters, built in 1969 under the GDR) for a panoramic view of the city at night, then explore the square and its surroundings before heading to the airport the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast
Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast

visits.text: Attend Sunday mass at Beuron Archabbey and the monks' Gregorian chant transports you to another century. This archabbey, founded in 1077, remains an active center of monastic life. The Beuron Art School, born here in the 19th century, influenced European sacred art with a geometric style inspired by ancient Egypt.
The Danube Valley cliffs around Beuron form a spectacular canyon. The trail following the Danube toward Sigmaringen or Tuttlingen ranks among Baden-Württemberg's finest hiking routes.

activities.text: Paddle the Danube by canoe between Beuron and Sigmaringen: about 2 hours of relaxed paddling between limestone walls. Several Beuron outfitters offer equipment and return shuttle.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Binz Pier, inaugurated in 1902 and rebuilt in 1994 at 370 m (versus 560 m originally), has a heavy history: a section collapsed in 1912, killing several people, which led to the creation of the DLRG (German lifesaving society). The current pier ends with a pontoon above the waves—in rough seas, you really feel the movement.

The white villas of Binz's Strandpromenade were built between 1880 and 1930 in Bäderarchitektur style: turrets, carved wooden verandas, bay windows. Binz is recognized as the largest coherent ensemble of this style on the island. Binz was officially declared a seaside resort in 1885. The promenade runs along 4 km of fine sand.

Jagdschloss Granitz is worth the detour from Binz: this mid-19th-century hunting lodge sits on a wooded hill 4 km away. Its central 38-m tower offers views on clear days to neighboring islands. Accessible by car or on foot from Binz in 1 hour.

activities.text: Board the Rasender Roland, the narrow-gauge steam locomotive connecting Binz to Putbus since 1895. The journey crosses forests and marshes, with stops at Sellin and Baabe. The wooden period carriages are original or very close—the steam breath and panting rhythm are part of the voyage.

Islanders call it the Riviera of the East—and the comparison holds better than it seems: blue sea, generous summer sun, white architecture, and waterfront restaurants. Outside July-August, Binz recovers its provincial calm. The best plan remains waking at 7 AM, being on the beach before cruise groups, and swimming in 18-20°C water.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Hohenzollern Castle commands its 855-meter Zeugenberg, an outlying hill of the Swabian Alb. Ancestral seat of the dynasty that gave Prussia its kings and Germany its emperors, the present castle was rebuilt between 1850 and 1867 by Friedrich August Stüler in romantic neo-Gothic style. The Schatzkammer displays Wilhelm II's crown, encrusted with diamonds.

In clear weather, views reach 100 km from the ramparts. The 15th-century Gothic Michaelskapelle is the only surviving medieval element. Around 350,000 visitors annually. Adult admission 26 euros, shuttle from parking lot available.

activities.text: The castle's Biergarten, in the inner courtyard, serves regional specialties with Zollerberg views. In winter, the castle's Weihnachtsmarkt ranks among the region's most popular.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: The Blautopf ('blue pot') is Germany's second-largest karst spring, located at the edge of the Swabian Jura 16 km west of Ulm. The funnel-shaped basin reaches 21 meters deep; its intense turquoise-blue color results from light scattering by limestone particles suspended in extremely clear water. During heavy rainfall, the water 'boils' as it surges under pressure from the underground karst network of the Blauhöhlen caves, explored for over 18 km by cave divers since 1985—several divers have lost their lives here, prohibiting access to non-professionals. Swabian legend tells of a goblin pouring a bucket of ink each morning to maintain the color; another claims a water nymph steals the lead ropes that curious visitors attempt to lower to measure the depth—hence the nearby rock nicknamed Klötzle Blei ('little bit of lead').

The Benedictine monastery of Blaubeuren, founded in 1085 on the heights above the Blautopf, is one of Baden-Württemberg's best-preserved Romanesque monastic complexes. The abbey church houses a 15th-century Gothic altarpiece and remarkable carved choir stalls. The Hammerschmiede (hammer forge), powered by waters from the Blautopf, is visible at the site entrance; a film there documents the exploration dives into the caves.

The Museum of Prehistory in Blaubeuren (Urgeschichtliches Museum) preserves pieces discovered in the surrounding Swabian Jura caves—including replicas of the Venus of Hohle Fels, the ivory mammoth figurine discovered in 2008 by archaeologist Nicholas Conard in the Hohle Fels cave at Schelklingen (7 km away). Standing 6 cm tall and dating between 35,000 and 40,000 years ago, it's the oldest known figurative sculpture in the world. The same cave also yielded flutes made from vulture bone and ivory dating to approximately 35,000 years ago—among the oldest known musical instruments.

activities.text: Guided tour of Hohle Fels cave at Schelklingen (7 km from Blaubeuren). The cave is open for guided tours Wednesday through Sunday from May to October. The site sits at 616 meters altitude in the Swabian Jura; excavations begun in 1870 have uncovered Upper Paleolithic artifacts across several meters of sediment.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Locals still call him the Lügenbaron — the lying baron. Hieronymus Carl Friedrich von Münchhausen was born here on 11 May 1720, in what is now Bodenwerder's town hall. The Münchhausen-Museum next door tells both stories: the real military man who served the Russian tsarina, and the fictional fabulist whose tall tales were published without his knowledge or consent.

The Münchhausen-Museum, housed in the medieval Schulenburg (c. 1300) of the family estate, exhibits first editions of the baron's adventures. The fountain outside the town hall depicts the famous scene of Münchhausen and his half-horse emerging from the swamp — an image so absurd it somehow captures the spirit of the whole town.

activities.text: On any Sunday from May to October, catch the Münchhausen-Spiel on the steps of the town hall: local amateur actors have been performing the baron's adventures here on the first Sunday of the month for over twenty years. Free entry, and reliably good fun.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Two pyramids in the middle of a lake: nobody expects that in the Brandenburg plain. Hermann Fürst von Pückler-Muskau had Branitzer Park laid out between 1846 and 1871 on initially flat, arid terrain. The water pyramid is his tomb, covered in vines. The other, on dry land, was intended for his wife Lucie—she was never buried there. Park open year-round, schloss April through October.

Inside, Schloss Branitz's Orientzimmer still smell of travel. Pückler, a great traveler, brought back from Egypt and the Near East enough to furnish several rooms in an orientalizing style unique in Brandenburg. The Carl Blechen collection in the restored rooms is, along with Berlin's Alte Nationalgalerie, one of the two largest holdings of this Cottbus-born artist.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Bremen's Marktplatz is one of the best-preserved medieval ensembles in northern Germany. The town hall (Rathaus), built in the 15th century in Gothic style then remodeled in Weser Renaissance in the 17th century, and the Roland statue erected in 1404—symbol of municipal freedoms—have been UNESCO-listed since 2004. Bremen had joined the Hanseatic League in 1358; much of its prosperity came from the 17th century onward through coffee and cotton trade.

St. Peter's Cathedral (Dom St. Petri) was founded in 789 by Willehad, Bremen's first bishop—who died that same year. The current sandstone edifice was built starting in 1042 and remodeled several times. Its two green-roofed towers dominate the Marktplatz. In the crypt, eight naturally preserved mummies from the 17th and 18th centuries, maintained by particularly dry conditions.

The Schnoor quarter is the old fishermen's district, whose 15th- and 16th-century half-timbered houses have remained virtually intact. Its alleyways so narrow you can touch both facades by stretching your arms now house galleries, artisans, and cafes.

Böttcherstrasse is a 100-meter street rebuilt in the 1920s in expressionist style, mixing carved red bricks and decorative art—commissioned by a coffee merchant enriched in the Brazilian trade. At the entrance, a gold relief depicting the archangel Michael slaying a dragon.

The Kunsthalle Bremen, founded in 1849, is one of Germany's oldest art museums. Its collection covers Flemish and Dutch painting, German Romanticism, French Impressionism—Monet, Pissarro—and modern art. It holds over 200,000 drawings and prints, one of the most important collections of this type in Europe.

activities.text: Stroll along the Schlachte, the riverside promenade along the Weser that runs the entire length of the old town. Terraces and moored boats—very lively on summer evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles
Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Five continents in a day: the Klimahaus Bremerhaven follows the 8th meridian East from Niger to Alaska, taking in the Swiss Alps and Samoa along the way. The temperature changes with each room — you go from 35°C desert heat to Antarctica in twenty minutes. A concept unique in the world, open since the late 2000s.

The Deutsches Auswandererhaus (German Emigration Center) hits harder than a conventional museum: located on the quay from which more than seven million Europeans departed between 1830 and 1974, it hands each visitor a 'boarding pass' bearing the real biography of an emigrant. Winner of the European Museum of the Year Award in 2007.

activities.text: Stroll the Havenwelten quays and watch container ships pass within arm's reach of the embankment. Bremerhaven is one of Europe's top container ports — the yellow cranes are visible for kilometres. In the evening, a handful of fresh fish restaurants line the basin.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Conquer the Brocken, Saxony-Anhalt's highest point and the highest mountain in northern Germany at 1,141 meters elevation. This mythical summit, shrouded in mist and legend, is the gathering place of witches during Walpurgis Night according to Germanic folklore. On clear days, panoramas from the summit extend up to 200 kilometers, embracing the northern plains and neighboring massifs.

Board the Harzer Brockenbahn steam train, one of Germany's last narrow-gauge railways still in regular service. This historic line climbs the slopes of the Brocken through fir forests and highland peat bog landscapes in a nostalgic atmosphere. The 90-minute train journey constitutes an experience as memorable as the destination itself.

Discover Heinrich Heine Rock, named in honor of the famous poet who climbed the Brocken in 1820 and immortalized this experience in his travel narrative. Hiking trails wind through a subarctic landscape unique in central Germany, where stunted vegetation testifies to the extreme climatic conditions. The delicious Brockensplitter chocolates are the gourmet reward for this ascent.

activities.text: Hiking or steam train journey to the Brocken summit

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The Sauerland locals simply call them 'die Steine.' Four blocks of volcanic porphyry, 45 to 92 meters high, pierce the forest on the Istenberg's flank at 727 meters elevation. Formed 390 million years ago, designated National Natural Monument in 2017. Peregrine falcons nest in the rock faces. Admission €6.50.

activities.text: Climb up to the Feldstein, the only one accessible to the public. Its summit peaks at 756 meters and offers a circular panorama across the Hochsauerland. The ascent takes 20 minutes from the information center. Closed Mondays.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Built from 1915 to 1917 with 1.5 million clinker bricks, Burg's Bismarckturm rises 27 meters on the Schlossberg. During the Second World War the Wehrmacht used it as a radio center; in 1951 it was renamed 'Youth Tower'; it recovered its name on October 3, 1990, during a public celebration. Open April through November; closed in late May for the Spreewälder Sagennacht.

You'll cross the Kur- und Sagenpark to reach the other end of Burg. This 60,000-square-meter park stages Sorbian mythology with themed sculptures—water spirits, forest geniuses. More instructive than a simple garden, though the bilingual panels are in German and Lower Sorbian only.

activities.text: Two hours on a kahn from Burg is considerably quieter than Lübbenau. The Spreehafen Burg is one of the Spreewald's oldest harbors; the Kolonistentour goes up to villages founded by Frederick the Great. Budget around €20 per adult.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Celle's old town (Altstadt) boasts over 500 half-timbered houses designated as historical monuments, the oldest dating back to the 15th century. The town's name derives from the Latin cella—a small monastic chamber—recalling an ancient monastery established on the site. First mentioned in 985 as Kellu, Celle served as the residence of the Dukes of Brunswick-Lüneburg from 1378 to 1705. The carved beams above doorways once indicated the occupants' trades.

The Hoppener Haus, built in 1532, stands among Germany's most lavishly decorated half-timbered houses. Its six-level facade is covered with carvings depicting devils, jesters, planetary gods, mythical creatures, and reptiles. Across the street, two lampposts equipped with speakers recount local anecdotes in both standard German and Low Saxon dialect—they activate automatically as you pass.

Celle Castle (Schloss Celle) dominates the banks of the Aller River from a gentle hill surrounded by moats and a sixteen-acre park. Built in the 13th century and serving as the residence of the Dukes of Brunswick-Lüneburg for over three centuries, it houses Baroque state apartments adorned with Italian stuccowork and a Renaissance chapel. The Schlosstheater, founded in 1675, is considered Germany's oldest baroque theater still in operation. Queen Caroline Mathilde of Denmark was exiled here from 1772 until her death in 1775.

The Stadtkirche St. Marien ranks among the town's oldest buildings. Its tower offers views across the old town's rooftops and the castle. Access to the tower depends on opening hours posted on the facade.

Celle's synagogue, built in 1740, is the oldest synagogue in northern Germany. This baroque half-timbered building, located at Im Kreise 24, survived Kristallnacht in 1938 because authorities feared fire would spread to neighboring houses. Inside, a collection of objects that belonged to Holocaust survivors.

The French Garden (Französischer Garten) was designed in the 17th century by French landscape architects, though over time it has taken on a more English appearance. Linden tree avenue, flower beds, and a pond—freely accessible.

activities.text: Walk a loop along the Aller and the historic bridges—the best angle to see the castle reflected in the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: The Biertor, an ocher brick gate with twin towers, is the most striking remnant of Cham's medieval wall. The Marktplatz just behind lines up a Rathaus with carillon and the Bürgerspital from 1300. Cham can be walked in an hour.

The promenade along the Regen, which wraps the old town in a broad loop, justifies the nickname "Stadt am Regenbogen." Centuries-old trees, wooden walkways, ducks: bucolic atmosphere ten minutes from the center.

activities.text: Take the bike path along the Regen toward Chammünster to see the Pfarrkirche Mariä Himmelfahrt, one of the Palatinate's oldest parishes. 6-km round trip, flat, family-friendly.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Chemnitz was renamed Karl-Marx-Stadt in 1953 by the GDR and recovered its historic name in 1990 after reunification. The Karl Marx Monument, inaugurated in 1971, is a colossal 40-ton bust carved from Siberian granite, placed on a 13-meter pedestal. The locals call it the "Nischel," a local dialect term for "skull." In 2025, Chemnitz is European Capital of Culture under the slogan "C the Unseen"—150 projects and 1,000 events distributed throughout the city and 38 surrounding municipalities, in converted former industrial buildings.

The Kaßberg district is one of Germany's largest continuous Gründerzeit and Art Nouveau ensembles—a hundred blocks from the late 19th century with their sandstone facades and sculpted ornaments. It had largely escaped the 1945 bombings that devastated the center.

The Municipal Art Collections (Kunstsammlungen Chemnitz) present notably Germany's largest collection of works by Karl Schmidt-Rottluff—the Expressionist painter was a native of the city. The museum also includes an important collection of 19th-century paintings and contemporary art.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: An open-air museum in the countryside: the Museumsdorf Cloppenburg assembles some sixty rural buildings from the Middle Ages to the 1980s. Founded in 1934, it's one of Germany's oldest Freilichtmuseen. Count on a good two hours.

The creak of wood beneath the hooves of Heidschnucken, the black-headed sheep grazing between the windmills: the Museumsdorf isn't a frozen museum. Three windmills, a church, a forge and even a rural 1980s disco. Adult admission: around 12 euros.

activities.text: If the Dorfkirmes happens during your visit (autumn), the museum village comes alive with historical fair stalls and wood-fired bread. Check dates on museumsdorf.de.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: The Veste Coburg was Luther's hideout for six months in 1530, while the Diet of Augsburg redefined religious Europe. Luther's apartment is still visitable in the fortress, one of Germany's largest. The Kunstsammlungen preserve works by Cranach, Dürer, and Grünewald - less crowded than in the major museums, so at leisure.

Coburg's Altstadt is more human and more lively than those along the Romantic Road: no souvenir shops at every corner, residents doing their shopping, the Marktplatz with the statue of Prince Albert (born here in 1819). The tourist surplus hasn't settled in yet.

Schloss Ehrenburg, former town palace of the Dukes of Saxe-Coburg, rebuilt after a 17th-century fire, houses what is reputed to be the first flush toilet in German history - installed for Queen Victoria during her 1845 visit. The guided tour lasts one hour.

activities.text: Walk up from the Schlossplatz to the Veste via the Carl-Escher-Weg: about 30 minutes of steep climb. The effort is rewarded by the view from the ramparts over the town and the hills of Upper Franconia. Bus 3 is available for the descent.

If you're here in July, the Coburg market (second weekend of July) is one of the region's largest open-air markets. The rest of the year, the Hofgarten behind the palace is the quietest spot for a break.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Arrive in Cochem via the road that follows the Moselle: steep vineyards frame the river's meanders, and the pointed silhouette of Reichsburg appears from afar, perched 100 meters above the village. Stroll along the Moselle quays—promenades lined with terraces, pleasure boats, and slate-roofed half-timbered houses.

The Marktplatz of Cochem and St. Martin's Church, built on the ruins of an 11th-century chapel, form the old town's heart. The square is framed by restored half-timbered houses and a former baroque town hall. From the square, stepped streets climb toward the castle.

Walk up via Schlossstrasse to Reichsburg (20 minutes from Marktplatz), the largest hillside castle on the Moselle. Built around the year 1000, first mentioned in 1051, it was destroyed in 1689 by troops of Louis XIV—then purchased in 1868 by Berlin merchant Louis Ravené, who had it completely rebuilt in the neo-Gothic romantic style then in vogue. The castle has 50 rooms, 6 of which are visitable by guided tour: the Gothic-style knights' hall, the Romanesque hall, the Renaissance dining room with its woodwork, and a room housing a 1 km secret passage to the Capuchin monastery in town. In the courtyard, a 50-meter-deep well once supplied water to all inhabitants.

Then ascend to Pinnerkreuz by chairlift (Sesselbahn), a viewpoint at 290 meters altitude dominating the Moselle loop. The panorama over terraced vineyards, river meanders, and wooded Eifel hills is the most complete in the entire Cochem region.

Burg Eltz is one of the rare European castles never destroyed and still owned by the same family for 33 generations—the first written trace dates to 1157, when Rudolph 'of Eltz Castle' was cited as witness in an act of Emperor Frederick Barbarossa. The castle rises from the depths of a forested valley, perched on a 70-meter rocky spur surrounded on three sides by the Elzbach stream. In 1268, three brothers divided the property according to a Ganerbschaft system (medieval co-ownership): three family branches cohabited in their own quarters around a narrow inner courtyard, hence the mixed styles—Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance—visible in the facades. The guided tour (~35 min) covers the knights' hall, the armory (with the world's oldest cannon arrows), the Rübenach Hall furnished in 1311—housing a Virgin and Child by Lucas Cranach the Elder—and the reception hall.

The Schatzkammer (Treasury), accessible with the same ticket, is the only place in the castle where photography is allowed. It houses over 500 pieces accumulated by the family over eight centuries: goldsmithery, jewelry, weapons, chalices, armor. It's one of Germany's most important private collections.

activities.text: One-hour panoramic cruise on the Moselle from Cochem marina. The circuit heads upriver toward the wine villages of Beilstein or Ediger-Eller, skirting steep terraced vineyards and medieval castle ruins.

Photo walk on the Moselle bridge in Cochem late afternoon: from the bridge, the view axis aligns quays, steeples, and castle silhouette—Cochem's classic composition, particularly flattering in golden evening light.

Approach hike from the village of Moselkern (on the Moselle bank) to Burg Eltz: a 2.5 km forest trail (35 minutes) along the Elzbach, progressively descending into the wooded valley. The castle appears suddenly at the turn of the last meander. This path is the best way to arrive—and avoid parking lot lines.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Built in 1908 by Bernhard Sehring in just sixteen months, the Staatstheater Cottbus is Lusatia's only Jugendstil theater and Brandenburg's sole state theater. The façade reads like an Art Nouveau manifesto: panther on the roof, nine muses in a frieze. Architectural guided tours take place on Sundays.

Cottbus's Spremberger Turm has dominated the Altmarkt since the thirteenth century—31 meters of medieval red brick at the end of the shopping street. The Altmarkt itself is ringed with Saxon Baroque and Classicist gabled houses: cafés are open from 8 a.m., terraces packed in summer.

activities.text: You'll stroll around the Altmarkt, surrounded by colorful façades, then head up Burgstraße to the Oberkirche St. Nikolai, the Niederlausitz's largest Gothic church. Cottbus's center can be walked in under an hour; stay on the Burgstraße / Mühlenstraße side for the concentration of cafés and shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Standing in wood on the Elbe embankment since the 18th century, the Kugelbake has guided ships into the river mouth for centuries — it marks the northernmost point of Lower Saxony. The current structure dates from 1945 (the previous one was destroyed during the war), stands nearly 30 metres tall, and has featured on Cuxhaven's coat of arms since 1913.

The Wattenmeer mudflats around Cuxhaven stretch as far as the eye can see at low tide: a flat tidal plain churned by worm burrows, scattered with shells and lit by shifting colours. Listed as a UNESCO World Natural Heritage Site, it hosts millions of migratory birds.

activities.text: Set off on a Wattwanderung at falling tide from Duhnen beach — twice a day the sea gives up kilometres of mudflats to be crossed barefoot. Grey shrimps and crabs survive in rock pools. Get back before the tide turns: it comes in fast.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Explore the mysterious Burg Berwartstein, a fascinating castle perched dramatically atop one of the rocky sandstone hills that characterize this border region. Once seized by notorious robber barons during the Middle Ages, this fortress is steeped in amazing myths and legends that the guided tours bring thrillingly to life. The hand-carved, candlelit tunnels beneath the castle create an atmospheric adventure unlike any other.

Discover the castle's remarkable architecture, including a small chapel adorned with medieval paintings that survived centuries of conflict and neglect. The robber baron history adds a thrilling dimension to the visit, with tales of ambushes, ransoms and dramatic escapes echoing through the stone corridors. This hidden gem remains surprisingly undiscovered by mass tourism, offering an authentic castle experience.

activities.text: Take the guided tour to hear the fascinating legends and explore the candle-lit underground passages

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: The Mathildenhöhe is Germany's most remarkable Art Nouveau site, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in July 2021. On this hill overlooking Darmstadt, Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig founded an artists' colony in 1899, bringing together architects, painters, and sculptors—including Joseph Maria Olbrich and Peter Behrens—under the motto: "Let my Hesse flourish, and with it art." Four international exhibitions (1901, 1904, 1908, 1914) produced an ensemble of 23 architectural elements: artists' houses, studios, the Swan Pavilion, a lily pond, and a grove of plane trees. These buildings became a laboratory of emerging modernism, inspired by both the British Arts & Crafts movement and the Vienna Secession.

The Hochzeitsturm (Wedding Tower), standing 48.5 meters tall, is the iconic building of the Mathildenhöhe. Olbrich designed it for the third exhibition in 1908 as a gift from the city to Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig for his remarriage to Princess Eleonore. Locals call it the "Five Finger Tower" because of its five ascending arches. Since 1993, an annex of the registry office has been housed here—weddings celebrated at the top grant access to the panoramic platform.

The Russian Chapel of Saint Mary Magdalene is integrated into the UNESCO site but predates the artists' colony. It was built between 1897 and 1899 by architect Louis Benois from Saint Petersburg, commissioned by Tsar Nicholas II—who had married Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig's sister Alix of Hesse (who became Empress Alexandra) in 1894. Its five golden domes stand out against the park's greenery and served as a place of worship during visits by the Russian imperial family.

The Hessisches Landesmuseum Darmstadt is one of Germany's most comprehensive encyclopedic museums. It brings together art (including the world's largest collection of works by Joseph Beuys), archaeology, geology, and natural history. One gallery is devoted to fossils from the Messel Pit.

The Messel Pit (Grube Messel), 9 km northeast of Darmstadt, is Germany's first natural site inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1995). This former oil shale quarry preserves the remains of over 40,000 specimens from a 47-million-year-old ecosystem—the Eocene epoch, when the first modern mammals were developing in a tropical forest. Discoveries include primitive horses the size of a dog (Eurohippus messelensis), primates, crocodiles, snakes, and insects with colors still visible. Access to the pit itself is by guided tour only. The finest specimens are displayed at the Senckenberg in Frankfurt and the Landesmuseum in Darmstadt. Fun fact: the pit nearly became a municipal landfill in the 1970s—strong citizen and scientific mobilization prevented it.

activities.text: Guided tour into the Messel Pit: the descent into the quarry allows you to observe the layers of oil shale in which fossils are still extracted. Working preparation replicas are visible. The tour lasts approximately two hours.
