Explore Darmstadt's scientific quarter, nicknamed "Science City": the Technical University, GSI (heavy ion research center), ESA/ESOC (European Space Operations Centre), and EUMETSAT are all based here. The facade of the ESOC control center—from which European Space Agency satellites are operated—is visible from the street.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the Bauhaus universe in Dessau, where this revolutionary art school was founded in 1919 and relocated in 1925. The main building designed by Walter Gropius, with its iconic glass facade, embodies the founding principle of form follows function. Over 300 Bauhaus buildings dot the city, constituting the world's largest architectural ensemble of this movement.

Visit the Bauhaus Museum which houses the world's most important collection dedicated to this major 20th-century artistic movement. Works by legendary masters Walter Gropius, Marcel Breuer, and Wassily Kandinsky are displayed in a contemporary architectural setting. The Masters' Houses, residences of Bauhaus professors, offer a moving testimony to their avant-garde lifestyle.

Discover the rebuilt city center of Dessau, more than 85% destroyed during World War II. The Residential Palace, Town Hall, and mausoleum testify to the princely history of the town before the Bauhaus era. St. Peter and Paul Church and Georgium Palace, surrounded by its English-style park, complete this diverse architectural heritage.

activities.text: Visit Luisium Park, an 18th-century landscape garden with its small neoclassical palace

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The LWL-Freilichtmuseum in Detmold is Germany's largest open-air museum: 90 hectares, more than 120 buildings dismantled from their original locations and rebuilt here, spanning 500 years of Westphalian architecture from peasant farmsteads to urban workshops. A 1960s petrol station turned museum piece sits alongside 16th-century farmhouses. Open Easter to October, Tuesday to Sunday.

Smoke rises from the blacksmith's forge inside a half-timbered building — at the LWL-Freilichtmuseum, smiths, bakers, and potters work for real in the historic workshops. Daily demonstrations run on no fixed schedule: come in the morning to catch an artisan at work. Senner-Pferde, a rare horse breed, graze freely within the museum grounds.

The Residenzschloss in Detmold, built in the Weser Renaissance style, is still the residence of the princely zu Lippe family. Its façade and clock tower dominate the Altstadt skyline. The state apartments can be visited on guided tours by arrangement — the interior is well worth the effort.

activities.text: Start early and set aside two to four hours at the LWL-Freilichtmuseum — a full circuit of the site covers 9 kilometres. Horse-drawn carriages run between the entrance and the Paderborner Dorf. Three historic on-site restaurants mean you can have lunch without leaving the museum.

If the Saturday market is on, Detmold hosts a weekly market on the Altmarkt with regional produce from the Lippe area. The nearby Strate's Brauhaus has been serving its local beer since 1863 — a solid choice for dinner.

days_plan.itin_title: Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Less famous than Rothenburg, and that's its fortune. Dinkelsbühl has preserved its medieval ramparts almost intact with five gates, eleven towers, and a bastide. Focus magazine named it Germany's most beautiful Altstadt - not a scientific ranking, but the evening stroller quickly understands why.

The Münster St. Georg presents a fan vault that astonishes in this late Gothic church from the 15th century. The severe façade doesn't prepare you for the interior ceiling. A curiosity: the bakers' stained-glass window, in the last bay on the right, depicts a pretzel. Free entry.

activities.text: Walk to the Wörnitztor in the morning before the shops open. The ramparts walk in Dinkelsbühl is shorter than Rothenburg's and less crowded; it's a better place to wander without a precise plan. The Weinmarkt in the center is the epicenter of local life.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Dinkelsbühl is often called Rothenburg's 'little sister'—but rightly less known, therefore less crowded. The town is surrounded by ramparts dating partly from 1370, with 16 towers and 4 gates still standing, accessible along their entire perimeter. Unlike Rothenburg, Dinkelsbühl suffered no damage during World War II—three-quarters of its houses are over 350 years old. Walk the outer rampart path (2.7 km, about 45 min) to see the towers, moats, and gardens bordering the walls.

St. George's Minster (Münster St. Georg) is Dinkelsbühl's central monument. Built between 1448 and 1499 in just 51 years, it's one of the most beautiful Gothic hall churches in southern Germany. The three naves, covered by a network vault, are of equal height, creating a rare sense of unified space in Gothic architecture. The Romanesque tower predates the nave: it dates from the 12th century.

The Marktplatz and Weinmarkt (wine market), two adjacent squares separated by the Minster, form the historic heart. The Deutsches Haus on Weinmarkt is a patrician half-timbered house in German Renaissance style, with a facade adorned with sculptures, one of the region's finest.

activities.text: Night watchman's round (Nachtwächter): every evening, a guide in medieval costume, lantern in hand, leads visitors through the illuminated old town to recount Dinkelsbühl's history.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: The Zwinger is Dresden's most iconic monument. Built from 1709 by Matthäus Daniel Pöppelmann for Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony and King of Poland, it began as an orangery and princely festival arena. Its official inauguration took place in 1719 for the marriage of Augustus's son to Archduchess Maria Josepha of Austria. Six pavilions linked by galleries enclose a vast interior courtyard with fountains and lawns—Augustus the Strong had himself depicted as Hercules above the Wallpavillon. The Kronentor, the gate crowned by a golden crown carried by four eagles, is the complex's most photographed image.

The Old Masters Picture Gallery, housed in the Semper Wing built between 1847 and 1855, contains over 700 paintings from the 15th to 18th centuries. Its absolute jewel is Raphael's Sistine Madonna, painted around 1512 and acquired by Augustus III in 1754 for the colossal sum of 25,000 ducats. Rubens, Vermeer, Rembrandt, Titian, Giorgione, and Botticelli are also represented. The paintings had been evacuated before the 1945 bombings—they were found hidden in underground mines and returned by the USSR in 1955 after ten years of detention in Moscow.

The Zwinger Porcelain Collection houses some 20,000 pieces of Chinese, Japanese, and Meissen porcelain. Augustus the Strong was so obsessed that in 1717 he traded a regiment of 600 Prussian soldiers for 151 Chinese porcelain vases—these pieces are still on display here. The collection also illustrates how the discovery of European porcelain at Meissen in 1708 arose from a royal commission for alchemy: Augustus wanted gold, his chemists invented porcelain.

The Semperoper, standing on Theaterplatz facing the Zwinger, is one of Europe's most renowned opera houses. The first building was designed by Gottfried Semper and inaugurated in 1841, then destroyed by fire in 1869. The second, also designed by Semper but completed by his son Manfred, reopened in 1878—Semper père was then banned from Saxony for participating in the 1849 insurrection. Razed by the February 13, 1945 bombings, it was rebuilt identically and reopened on February 13, 1985, exactly forty years after its destruction. Richard Strauss premiered several of his operas here, including Der Rosenkavalier in 1911.

The Residenzschloss, palace of the prince-electors and kings of Saxony, is one of Dresden's oldest buildings. Its construction dates to 1220; it was remodeled in Renaissance style between 1548 and 1556, then partially destroyed in 1945 and gradually rebuilt from 1986. It now houses several major museums. The Historic Green Vault (Historisches Grünes Gewölbe) presents 3,000 baroque goldsmith objects in their original 18th-century display cases—one of Europe's greatest treasure collections. On November 25, 2019, thieves broke a window at 5 a.m. and stole several diamond parures from the collection: the case caused a scandal throughout Germany.

The Fürstenzug stretches 102 meters along the outer wall of the Stallhof, the palace stable courtyard. This monumental mural depicts the procession of the 35 rulers of the House of Wettin, from Conrad I in 1127 to King George I in the early 20th century, accompanied by soldiers, artists, and scholars. Originally painted between 1871 and 1876 to celebrate 800 years of the dynasty, it was replaced between 1904 and 1907 by approximately 23,000 Meissen porcelain tiles to withstand the weather—it's the world's largest porcelain artwork. Despite the 1945 bombings, only 200 tiles had to be replaced during restoration.

The Frauenkirche, standing on the Neumarkt, is the symbol of Dresden's resurrection. Built between 1726 and 1743 by municipal architect Georg Bähr, its 96-meter stone dome—the largest north of the Alps—had withstood Prussian cannonballs in 1760 that bounced off it. It collapsed, however, on February 15, 1945, two days after the Allied bombings, from the accumulated heat in the sandstone. The ruins remained a memorial for 45 years under the GDR. Reconstruction, financed by donations from around the world, lasted from 1994 to 2005: 3,643 original stones blackened by fire were reintegrated into the new edifice, visible by their dark color among the light blocks. The golden cross at the summit was donated by British contributors, including the son of one of the pilots who had bombed the city.

The Neustadt, on the right bank of the Elbe, is Dresden's artistic quarter—it survived the 1945 bombings better than the Altstadt, and its Art Nouveau streets from the early 20th century have kept their character. The Kunsthofpassage, a series of five interconnected courtyards, is the best-known example of this creativity: each courtyard was entrusted to different artists. The most famous is the Court of Elements, whose blue facade is covered with gutters shaped like trumpets and funnels that transform rain into music.

The Brühlsche Terrasse, nicknamed "the balcony of Europe" by Goethe, is a 500-meter elevated promenade along the Elbe above the old ramparts. Developed in the 18th century on medieval fortifications by Count Brühl, it offers the classic view of the Altstadt silhouette with its domes and steeples. It's from this terrace that Romantic painters—notably Canaletto and Caspar David Friedrich—immortalized Dresden's panorama.

The Albertinum houses the Gallery of New Masters, covering painting and sculpture from the 19th century to the present. The collection includes works by Caspar David Friedrich, including Two Men Contemplating the Moon, as well as Impressionist and Expressionist paintings. The building itself, a 19th-century neo-Renaissance arsenal, was flooded during the 2002 Elbe floods—the works had to be urgently carried to upper floors to be saved.

activities.text: Walking or electric scooter ride along the Elbe to admire Dresden's reconstructed skyline
Observation platform at the Frauenkirche for views over the city

Ascent to the Frauenkirche observation gallery for a panoramic view of Dresden's rooftops, the Elbe, and surrounding hills. Access is via an interior staircase to the dome lantern, 67 meters above ground.
Walking or electric scooter ride along the Elbe to admire Dresden's reconstructed skyline
Observation platform at the Frauenkirche for views over the city

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Locals call it 'das kleine Meer': the Dümmer See, Lower Saxony's second-largest lake at 12.5 km², is so shallow that the average depth is 1.2 meters. Common cranes and greylag geese stop here by the thousands in autumn. Bike loop around the lake: 27 km.

activities.text: Rent a bike and circle the lake on the marked cycle path. The east side of the Dümmer offers free-access sandy beaches, ideal for a swimming break. About 90 minutes of leisurely pedaling.

If you come in autumn, bring binoculars: the Dümmer is a wetland of international importance. Observation towers are scattered around the lake. The Naturschutzstation on the west side organizes guided tours.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Düsseldorf's Altstadt is nicknamed "the longest bar in the world" due to its concentration of pubs and breweries in less than half a square kilometer. Beyond the festive reputation, the quarter preserves notable medieval remnants: St. Lambert's Basilica with its crooked spire (deformed by 14th-century drought according to legend), the 13th-century Schlossturm, the last tower of the princely castle destroyed by fire in 1872 and now a navigation museum, and the Rhine promenade laid out in 1993 when the former main road was buried in a tunnel.

The Königsallee, known as the "Kö," owes its name to an 1848 incident: King Frederick William IV, passing in a carriage on what was then called Kastanienallee, was pelted with horse manure by angry citizens. To smooth over the scandal, the city renamed the avenue "Königsallee" in 1851. Stretching 800 meters and bordered by a 30-meter-wide canal, it's one of Germany's widest avenues and has been a premier shopping street since the late 19th century.

The MedienHafen demonstrates that architectural conversion can be an attraction in itself. This former freight port decommissioned in the 1980s was converted starting in the 1990s with a simple rule: each new building had to dialogue with the harbor's history. Frank Gehry's Neuer Zollhof (1998) is the centerpiece—three buildings with undulating forms and distinct skins (white plaster, brushed steel, brick) that some interpret as a father-mother-child family. The neighboring Rheinturm rises to 240 meters and houses the world's largest decimal clock on its shaft.

activities.text: Stroll along the Rheinuferpromenade, 1.5 km of landscaped quays between Burgplatz and the MedienHafen. The promenade follows the Rhine with views of Oberkassel and its Art Nouveau façades on the opposite bank. In late afternoon, locals gather here for Altbier, the bitter brown beer typical of Düsseldorf.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: The smell of smoke and salt announces Eckernförde well before you reach the harbour: the town has been smoking sprats and eels in wood-fired kilns since the 19th century. The Alte Fischräucherei Eckernförde museum traces this craft. At the harbour, a few artisan smokers still sell directly at the quayside.

St. Nicolaikirche, plain and Gothic, surprises with the quality of its interior woodwork — 17th-century altars and pews inside a building raised in the 13th century. 200 metres from the harbour, usually empty, free entry.

activities.text: Buy smoked sprats directly from fishermen on the quay in the morning — Eckernförder Sprotten, smoked and marinated in vinegar, are the local speciality. On the first Sunday of the month, the fish market sets up along the harbour promenade.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Waldeck Castle is worth the trip for the view, not the furniture. Perched 120 meters above the Edersee, this 12th-century castle served as a prison until 1868. The 'Hinter Schloss und Riegel' museum stages torture instruments and cells. The restaurant terrace offers the most complete panorama of the lake and dam.

Like the Möhnetalsperre, the Edersee dam wall was bombed by the RAF in May 1943 (Operation Chastise). The dam was rebuilt in four months. The Sperrmauer Museum at Hemfurth documents the attack. In the evening, a light installation illuminates the stone façade.

activities.text: Take the Waldecker Bergbahn, a 1961 cable car linking the lake shore to the castle in a few minutes. The ride is short and without adrenaline, but the vintage mechanics have a quaint charm. Down below, swimming or kayaking on the Edersee.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Wartburg Castle rises from a rocky spur 411 meters above Eisenach, deep in the Thuringian Forest. Founded around 1067 by Count Ludwig the Springer, its name springs from legend: upon first seeing the hill, the count allegedly shouted «Warte, Berg - du sollst mir eine Burg tragen!» («Wait, mountain - you shall bear my castle!»). The Romanesque palace (Palas), built between 1156 and 1162, is the oldest preserved residential building of its kind in Germany. The complex was extensively restored in the 19th century under architect Hugo von Ritgen at the behest of Grand Duke Karl Alexander - a restoration whose imagery directly inspired Ludwig II of Bavaria for Neuschwanstein. The castle has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1999. In 1817, student associations gathered here to demand a German constitution: the «Wartburgfest» is considered one of the founding acts of the German liberal-national movement.

In the Lutherstube, a small vaulted chamber in the Vogtei, Martin Luther lived from May 1521 to March 1522 under the alias Junker Jörg - «Knight George». Declared an outlaw after refusing to recant at the Diet of Worms, he had been «kidnapped» by fake brigands dispatched by Elector Frederick the Wise to protect him. In ten to eleven weeks, he translated the New Testament from ancient Greek into German - a version that did as much to standardize the German language as to spread the Reformation. Luther called Wartburg his «Patmos», referring to the Greek island where the apostle John wrote the Book of Revelation. The museum also preserves works by Lucas Cranach the Elder and objects related to Saint Elizabeth of Hungary, who lived here from 1211 to 1228.

activities.text: Panoramic view over the Thuringian Forest from Wartburg's ramparts. On clear days, the vista extends to the Rennsteig - the ridgeline that once separated Saxony from Franconia, and during the Cold War, the two Germanys.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: The Kurfürstliche Burg often flies under the radar, overshadowed by the castles of the Romantic Rhine - wrongly so. Built in the 14th century on the ruins of a fortress destroyed in 1301, it served for 150 years as the residence of the Archbishops of Mainz. The Gutenberg Museum at the top of the tower recalls that the printing press inventor lived here in 1465. Admission: €3.50 adult. Note: the east flank is under renovation in 2026.

If you visit in June, the rose gardens of the Kurfürstliche Burg alone justify the detour. This flowering garden earned Eltville the title of Rosenstadt in 1988. Climb the 123 steps in the Burgturm for views across the Rheingau and Rhine as far as the Rhine plain.

activities.text: Walk down to the Rhine quays along Eltville's car-free promenade. The Rheingau vineyards begin immediately behind the town - the tourist office offers guided walks through the vines with tastings at local wineries. Reservations at +49 6123 9098-0.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Discover Emden, a historic seaport and sailing paradise on the North Sea coast. This maritime city at the mouth of the Ems River has been a center of seafaring for centuries, offering excellent conditions for sailing enthusiasts and water sports. The harbor atmosphere, maritime museums, and Dutch-influenced architecture reflect its rich trading heritage.

activities.text: Set sail from Emden's harbor to explore the stunning East Frisian coastline and nearby islands. The protected waters and steady North Sea breezes create ideal sailing conditions for both beginners and experienced sailors, with opportunities to spot seals and seabirds along the way.

Explore the unique East Frisian culture with its distinctive language, tea traditions, and windmill-dotted landscape. The flat polder lands stretching to the horizon offer superb cycling routes along historic dikes and through charming villages where time seems to stand still.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: St. Mary's Cathedral dominates Domplatz from a raised terrace, flanked by the collegiate Church of St. Severus - one of Central Europe's most impressive sacred ensembles. The current cathedral was begun around 1280 with a vast Gothic choir. Its glory is the Gloriosa: cast in 1497, it's the world's largest free-swinging medieval bell. It's rung only on grand occasions to protect the structure. The cathedral also houses the «Wolfram», a 12th-century bronze candelabra, and Gothic stained glass in the choir.

Fischmarkt is Erfurt's central square, surrounded by Renaissance merchants' houses, some dating from the 16th century. The neo-Gothic town hall, built 1870-1874, overlooks the square with its murals of local legends. Erfurt miraculously survived World War II bombing, explaining the density of its historic fabric - the city is often called «the preserved medieval metropolis».

The Krämerbrücke - «merchants' bridge» - is Central Europe's longest inhabited bridge: 120 meters long, with 32 half-timbered houses built directly on its structure. First mentioned in 1156, its current stone form dates from the 14th century. It recalls Florence's Ponte Vecchio or Venice's Rialto, except that its houses are inhabited and occupied by shops even today.

Erfurt's Old Synagogue, built around 1094, is Central Europe's best-preserved medieval synagogue. It kept its walls intact because after the pogrom of March 21, 1349 - when about 900 of the city's Jews were massacred - it was converted into a warehouse and fell into oblivion, protecting it during Kristallnacht. The synagogue-mikveh-stone house ensemble has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023. In the cellar, the Erfurt Treasure - discovered in 1998 in the wall of a neighboring cellar - has been permanently displayed since 2009: 3,141 silver coins, 14 ingots, and more than 700 Gothic goldsmith objects from the 14th century. The masterpiece is a gold ring adorned with a miniature temple bearing the Hebrew inscription «mazal tov». The treasure was exhibited at the Musée de Cluny in Paris and the Metropolitan Museum in New York before joining the permanent collection.

activities.text: Visit the medieval Jewish treasure of Erfurt in the Old Synagogue's cellar. Examining the Gothic goldsmithery - gem-studded brooches, gold rings, ceremonial vessels - one grasps what Erfurt's Jewish community was at its apex, before its annihilation in 1349.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Wander through Esslingen's remarkably preserved medieval old town, home to some of Germany's finest half-timbered buildings clustered in atmospheric lanes. The town center features extraordinarily large and beautifully decorated timber-frame houses that survived centuries of war and development pressures. This architectural treasure trove just southeast of Stuttgart rewards visitors who venture beyond the capital.

Admire the historic Altes Rathaus (Old Town Hall) and the twin-spired Stadtkirche that dominate the market square, both masterpieces of medieval civic architecture. The town's wine-growing tradition continues on the steep vineyard slopes that surround the old town, producing excellent local varieties. Canal waterways threading through the center add to Esslingen's picturesque charm.

Visit during Christmas market season to experience one of Germany's most magical medieval markets. The market transforms the old town into a fairy-tale setting with Middle Ages-era children's rides, torch-lit streets and the unique spectacle of betting on mice running into numbered wooden houses. Flaming braziers and costumed performers create an authentically medieval atmosphere unlike any other Christmas market.

activities.text: Climb to the castle ruins above town for panoramic views over the half-timbered rooftops and vineyards

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Whether you read them as mystical or purely geological, the Externsteine have captivated every traveller who has passed this way. Thirteen sandstone pillars rise 40 metres above a pond in the Wiembecke valley, and a Romanesque Descent from the Cross relief carved directly into the rock — first documented in 1129 — still stands in place. Carved stone stairways lead to a panoramic platform at the summit.

A Romanesque Descent from the Cross carved between 1115 and 1130 into the Externsteine sandstone depicts Christ at life size — one of the rare surviving examples of Romanesque open-air sculpture still in its original position in Germany. Whether the site was a Germanic place of worship, a Christian pilgrimage site, or both remains a matter of scholarly debate.

activities.text: Arrive early — crowds build significantly around midday and into the afternoon at this popular site. The circular one-hour trail around the rock formations offers the best angles. Climbing ticket €5 for adults, €2.50 for children aged 6 to 17.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Flensburg has been importing rum from the Danish West Indies since 1755, when the city's merchants supplied Saint Croix, Saint Thomas and Saint John. Of the era's 200 rum houses, two active distilleries remain: Johannsen and Braasch. The Rum Museum inside the Schifffahrtsmuseum traces this colonial trade that most Germans know nothing about.

The Museumshafen with its old sailing vessels is worth visiting even without a festival: the traditional wooden boats moored along the stone quay are accessible on certain days. The former West Indies warehouse stands directly on the basin.

In the inner courtyards of the Norderstrasse, hints of molasses and tobacco seep from the old warehouses. The Kaufmannshöfe — merchants' courtyards — conceal secret gardens behind gates that are often left open. The Marienburg is the best known.

activities.text: Follow the Rum & Zucker Meile from the Nordertor to the harbour: this marked trail links the old warehouses, merchants' courtyards and the two last active distilleries. Tastings are offered at Johannsen Rum — let them know in advance.

If you arrive on a Thursday morning, the Südermarkt market is at its liveliest: fruit, vegetables, Danish cheeses, smoked fish. Flensburg is officially bilingual German-Danish — you hear both languages at the stalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Between 1938 and 1945, the SS detained around 100,000 people at KZ Flossenbürg, exploited in the granite quarries. At least 30,000 died there. Dietrich Bonhoeffer was executed here in April 1945. The Gedenkstätte, one of Europe's most modern memorial sites, is free and open daily.

If you then climb to the Schlossberg, the Burgruine at 732 m offers a striking contrast: a peaceful view over the verdant Upper Palatinate. The ruin is accessible year-round. The proximity of the two sites, just a few hundred meters apart, leaves a mark.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: The Römerberg has been Frankfurt's historic heart since the High Middle Ages. At the center of the square stands the Fountain of Justice (Gerechtigkeitsbrunnen) with its goddess Justitia, and on the west side, the Römer—a three-gabled town hall that's served as the seat of city administration since 1405. Inside, the Kaisersaal (Emperor's Hall) preserves portraits of all 52 emperors of the Holy Roman Empire, from Charlemagne to Francis II. This is where coronation banquets were held after ceremonies in the nearby cathedral. From the Golden Bull of 1356 until 1792, the Römer was the site of imperial elections. The hall and inner courtyard are freely accessible.

Continue to the Kaiserdom (St. Bartholomew's Cathedral), whose red Gothic tower rises 95 meters. Consecrated in 1239, it was officially designated the coronation site for Holy Roman Emperors in 1356—ten ceremonies took place here between 1562 and 1792. The current building is a postwar reconstruction, but the cathedral treasury preserves authentic pieces, including a Gothic priestly vestment dated to 1350. Climb the 324 steps of the tower for panoramic views over Frankfurt's rooftops.

Just steps away, the Paulskirche (St. Paul's Church) deserves a stop. In 1848, it served as the seat of Germany's first freely elected National Assembly—the founding event of modern German parliamentarism. It was one of the first buildings reconstructed after the war, a testament to its symbolic importance. The austere interior now hosts exhibitions and official ceremonies.

Cross the Eiserner Steg, an iron footbridge built in 1869 over the Main River, to reach Sachsenhausen on the south bank. From the bridge, the view aligns half-timbered houses of the Römerberg, Gothic towers, and the financial district skyscrapers in the background—capturing Frankfurt's paradox, nicknamed 'Mainhattan' for this coexistence of medieval and modern. Water level markers engraved in the pillars recall historic floods.

The Alt-Sachsenhausen quarter preserves its cobbled lanes and half-timbered houses. This is where the city's cider taverns concentrate: Apfelwein (or Ebbelwoi in local dialect) is served in blue stoneware pitchers in diamond-shaped glasses. Wander the alleys around Frankensteiner Platz before heading to the Museumsufer along the waterfront.

The Städel Museum, founded in 1815 by banker Johann Friedrich Städel as Germany's first civic museum foundation, is the crown jewel of the Museumsufer. Its collection spans 700 years of European art: 3,100 paintings, 660 sculptures, and over 100,000 drawings and prints. In 1937, the Nazis confiscated 77 paintings and 700 prints deemed 'degenerate'—the remaining works were evacuated to Bavaria to protect them from bombing. The museum preserves masterpieces by Dürer, Botticelli, Cranach, Rembrandt, Vermeer, Monet, and Picasso. The underground extension inaugurated in 2012, lit by circular skylights at surface level, houses contemporary art.

Head back toward the center to reach the Main Tower, one of the few Frankfurt skyscrapers with a public viewing platform on the 55th floor, at 200 meters height. On clear days, views extend to the Taunus mountains to the north and the Odenwald to the south. From here you best understand the 'Mainhattan' skyline: 39 glass and steel towers forming continental Europe's densest financial district.

The Palmengarten (botanical garden), open since 1871, is one of Germany's largest with 13 hectares of greenhouses and themed gardens. The tropical greenhouses shelter rare species from the Amazon, Africa, and Asia. It's a breathing space cherished by families and strollers.

Finish with a stroll along the Museumsufer, the museum boulevard that lines the south bank of the Main for about 2 km. Over thirty museums cluster here—cinema museum, architecture museum, Jewish museum, communications museum. Every last weekend of August, the Museumsufer Festival transforms this boulevard into one of Europe's largest outdoor cultural events.

activities.text: Walking tour through the Altstadt: circuit Römerberg → Kaiserdom → Paulskirche → Goethe House (Großer Hirschgraben 23). The writer's birthplace, destroyed during the war, has been completely reconstructed identically with original furniture and objects. You can see the study where Goethe wrote The Sorrows of Young Werther at age 23.

One-hour panoramic cruise on the Main departing from the Museumsufer embankments. The circuit passes the quay's 39 museums, under the Eiserner Steg, and offers a complete reading of the city from the river.

Apfelwein tasting in a traditional Sachsenhausen tavern. The local apple cider is served in stoneware pitchers in a glass called Geripptes (ribbed). The tradition of cider taverns in this quarter dates to the 18th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks
Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: The Fraueninsel is a fishing village set on water, with a Benedictine convent active since the 8th century. You can walk around it in twenty minutes, between vegetable gardens, pottery workshops, and Tassilo's thousand-year-old linden tree. No cars, no noise.
The Abtei Frauenwörth sells its own Klosterlikör and marzipan in a small shop at the entrance. The Carolingian Torhalle, a 9th-century remnant, is the island's oldest building.

activities.text: Settle in at the Klosterwirt for a smoked Renke (whitefish), caught that morning from the Chiemsee. The terrace overlooks the lake and Herreninsel. One of the best quality-pleasure ratios on the whole trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Three centuries of construction, a cathedral that leans slightly but holds. The Freiburg Minster took over 300 years to complete. What really catches the eye: the gargoyles on the south façade, including one famous for its scatological posture—supposedly the revenge of an unpaid sculptor. The tower measures 116 m and can be climbed via 335 steps. Closed to visitors during Sunday morning services.

The Minster's gargoyles look you straight in the eye here—the sandstone originals, not the copies displayed outdoors, are installed in the nave of the former Augustinian convent at nearly 12 m high. The Augustinermuseum fully reopened in February 2026 after 22 years of renovation work. The medieval sculpture hall is the highlight. Pricing and hours at museen.freiburg.de, closed Mondays.

activities.text: Kitschy, the gutter that makes you marry a Freiburger? Maybe—but the legend is told with the seriousness of local truth. The Bächle are medieval irrigation channels running through the pedestrian streets. Follow Konviktstraße for the half-timbered houses, stop at Hausbrauerei Feierling (Gerberau 46) for a beer brewed on-site. The terrace overlooks the rooftops. No reservations accepted.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: The photo is everywhere on Instagram, but nothing prepares you for the real visual shock. Freudenberg's Alter Flecken lines up over 80 black-and-white half-timbered houses, rebuilt after the 1666 fire following the 1540 plan. Climb to the Kurpark for the full panorama. Designated a monument of international significance by the State.

Locals simply call their quarter the 'Flecken.' The 4fachwerk museum on Mittelstraße displays local clockmaking crafts in two restored half-timbered houses. Open Wednesday, Saturday, and Sunday 2pm-5pm. Free admission.

activities.text: Walk the Fachwerkweg, a 12-km loop around Freudenberg. The trail passes through the Hohenhain tunnel (former railway), the wooded Gambachtal valley, and offers several viewpoints over the black-and-white ensemble. Allow three hours at a good pace.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Drive the legendary Black Forest High Road (Schwarzwaldhochstraße) from Baden-Baden to Freudenstadt, one of Germany's most scenic mountain routes. This spectacular drive takes you along the peaks of the Black Forest, with incredible views plunging down into forested valleys at every bend. Numerous viewpoints and trailheads invite you to stop, breathe the pine-scented air and absorb the magnificent panoramas.

Pause at the Mummelsee, a mystical glacial lake surrounded by dark fir forests and wrapped in local legends about water spirits and lake nymphs. The viewing platform at Hornisgrinde, the highest peak in the Northern Black Forest, rewards visitors with 360-degree views stretching to the Vosges Mountains in France. Several hiking trails of varying difficulty branch off from the road through pristine forest landscapes.

Arrive in Freudenstadt, home to Germany's largest market square, a vast open space surrounded by arcaded houses that give the town a distinctly Mediterranean feel. This planned Renaissance town was founded in 1599 and rebuilt after World War II damage with remarkable attention to its historic character. The unique L-shaped Protestant church and covered shopping arcades make it a pleasant base for Black Forest exploration.

activities.text: Stop for short hikes along the route to experience the Black Forest's pristine nature up close

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: An airship four times larger than an Airbus, yet lighter than air: the Zeppelin Museum in Friedrichshafen tells this engineering madness across 4,000 square meters. The Bauhaus building of the former Hafenbahnhof is a perfect setting. More than 1,500 pieces on display.

Step inside the life-size reconstruction of the LZ 129 Hindenburg passenger cabins: 33 meters long, original carpeting, Art Deco dining room. You understand why the wealthy of the 1930s paid a fortune to cross the Atlantic in three days.

You don't expect to find Otto Dix in a dirigible museum, and yet. The Zeppelin Museum's art collection brings together Baumeister, Ackermann, and other artists of the "inner migration," refugees at Lake Constance during Nazism. An unexpected counterpoint.

activities.text: If you have the budget, a Zeppelin NT flight over Lake Constance is still possible: Deutsche Zeppelin-Reederei offers tours from 30 to 120 minutes departing from Friedrichshafen. Steep price, unique experience in the world. Reservation required.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: It is hard to believe: Amsterdam has an enclave in Schleswig-Holstein. Friedrichstadt was founded in 1621 by Duke Friedrich III to receive Dutch settlers — Remonstrants fleeing religious persecution. They brought their bricks, their stepped gables and their canals. The town council minutes were written in Dutch until the 18th century.

Founded in 1621 by Duke Friedrich III of Schleswig-Gottorf, the planned town is laid out around the Mittelburggraben — the central canal — on a grid of perpendicular streets. The Gevelsteen, carved and painted stone reliefs above doorways, bear the coats of arms of the original Dutch families and some date back to the town's founding.

activities.text: Take a rowing boat out on the Mittelburggraben for a tour of Friedrichstadt's grachten — departure from the Tourist-Information at Am Markt 9. Red-brick gabled houses mirror themselves in the water. One hour is enough for the main canals.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Who installs an 11-ton dragon, 16 m long and 5 m tall in the middle of a Bavarian village? Furth im Wald. The Further Drache "Tradinno" is the world's largest four-legged walking robot (Guinness). It breathes fire and walks toward you. Not a toy: a feat of engineering blending Hollywood and German robotics.

In the Drachenhöhle, the floor trembles, red smoke rises: the museum tells the story of the Drachenstich, Germany's oldest Volksschauspiel, attested since at least 1590. You also understand the robot's mechanics. Entry + demonstration: €15 adult, €10 child.

activities.text: If you're there in August, the Drachenstich Festspiele mobilize 350 extras in medieval costume on the Stadtplatz. The dragon crosses through the crowd breathing flames. Tickets on pre-sale at drachenstich.de.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: The clatter of the wheels in the ravine reaches you before the view—you hear it before you see it. The Hexenlochmühle, built in 1825 as a sawmill and in the same family since 1839, is the only mill with two hydraulic wheels in the Schwarzwald. It still operates. Local legend says witches held their rituals at night in this narrow valley. Terrace by the Heubach stream. Decent restaurant, shop selling regional schnapps to take away.

Not the supermarket cuckoo clocks: the real clock museum in Furtwangen is a serious scientific institution, not the Triberg tourist trap. The Deutsches Uhrenmuseum traces the history of timekeeping, from the first clocks of the 17th century to industrial precision pieces. Admission around €6. Allow 90 minutes. Closed Mondays.

activities.text: Take the damp trail, abandon the numbered signs: Zweribach Falls is an option for those who want two hours of complete solitude. Ferns, mosses, no crowds on weekdays. Two hours round trip from the St. Märgen area. Bring waterproof shoes: the path follows a stream and slippery sections are common after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Neuschwanstein Castle, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2025, is Germany's most visited castle with over one million entries annually. King Ludwig II of Bavaria, crowned in 1864 at age 18, commissioned its construction in 1869 on a rocky spur 200 meters high. He had 8 meters of rock dynamited to clear the foundations and, between 1879 and 1880, transported some 465 tons of Salzburg marble and 400,000 bricks. The construction site employed the most modern techniques of the era: central heating, running water on every floor, and a steel structure to support the throne hall. Ludwig II lived here only a few months in 1884 before being declared unfit to govern and dying mysteriously in Lake Starnberg on June 13, 1886. The castle was then unfinished—and never would be completed.

Walk up to the Marienbrücke, suspended 90 meters above the Pöllat Gorge and its 45-meter waterfall. This bridge, originally built in wood in the 1840s by Ludwig II's father as a birthday gift for Queen Marie, then reconstructed in iron in 1866, offers THE classic photo of Neuschwanstein: the castle stands out in its entirety against a backdrop of forests and Alpine peaks. This silhouette inspired Walt Disney for Sleeping Beauty's castle at the first Disneyland, opened in 1955 in California—it later became the Disney studios logo. The walk up from the Hohenschwangau parking lot takes about 40 minutes.

Hohenschwangau Castle, below Neuschwanstein, is the residence where Ludwig II spent his childhood. His father Maximilian II had it restored starting in 1832 in neo-Gothic style. The rooms, richly decorated with tapestries illustrating medieval legends—including that of Lohengrin, the swan knight—nourished the future king's imagination from his earliest years. Seeing Wagner's opera Lohengrin in 1861 at age 15 was Ludwig's revelation: the swan would become his emblem, omnipresent throughout Neuschwanstein. The castle overlooks the Alpsee, whose turquoise waters invite a walk on the trail circling it.

The Hohes Schloss (High Castle) of Füssen, summer residence of the prince-bishops of Augsburg since the late 15th century, is one of the largest and best-preserved Gothic castles in Swabia. Its trompe-l'oeil painted façades—false windows, false battlements, false oriels—are unique in Germany and date from the reign of Bishop Friedrich von Zollern (1486-1505). The Rittersaal (Knights' Hall) and its carved coffered ceiling merit the climb. The Bavarian State Gallery, installed in the former reception rooms, displays late Gothic paintings and sculptures from the 15th and 16th centuries.

Below the castle, St. Mang's Benedictine Monastery (Kloster St. Mang), founded in the 8th century and rebuilt in Baroque style between 1697 and 1726, houses one of Europe's finest collections of antique lutes and violins—Füssen is considered one of the cradles of European luthiery. The heritage museum also presents Jakob Hiebeler's "Dance of Death" (1602), Bavaria's oldest mural representation of a danse macabre. The monastery's splendid Baroque halls offer a glimpse of the institution's former wealth.

Wander through Füssen's pedestrianized old town along Reichenstraße, lined with painted-façade houses, which follows the route of the ancient Via Claudia Augusta, the Roman road connecting Italy to Germania. The luthier's fountain recalls the town's historic role in stringed instrument making. On Schrannenplatz, the former grain hall, historic pageants enliven the town in summer. The town, perched at 800 meters altitude at the foot of the Alps, has over 2,000 years of history—its colorful Baroque houses, alleys, and mountain views make it a spectacular terminus for the Romantic Road.

activities.text: Walk the circuit around the Alpsee, a 5-kilometer trail hugging turquoise waters framed by conifer forests and the rocky faces of the Alps. The panorama of the two royal castles from the south shore is spectacular. The trail is flat and accessible, perfect for unwinding after visiting the castles.

Walk down to the Lechfall, the Lech Falls, 10 minutes from Füssen's center. The Lech River, which originates in Austria, plunges here through a narrow gorge in a series of natural cascades. The bridge spanning the gorge offers spectacular views of the turquoise waters and erosion-sculpted rocks.

Embark on a cruise on the Forggensee, Bavaria's fifth-largest lake by surface area. Boats operate from early June to mid-October and offer panoramas of Neuschwanstein and Hohenschwangau castles, Füssen's old town perched on its hill, and the surrounding Alpine peaks. Evening cruises at sunset are particularly recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria
Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria
Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns
German Alpine Road: Lake Constance to Königssee
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Start with the Partnachklamm, a 700-meter gorge carved by the Partnach River, with walls rising up to 80 meters in places. Designated a natural monument as early as 1912, it's accessible via a path carved into the rock alongside the glacial torrent. In winter, the waterfalls transform into spectacular frozen cascades. Allow 25 minutes on foot from the Olympic stadium.

Head to Partenkirchen's Ludwigstrasse for a stroll through the historic heart. Garmisch and Partenkirchen were two separate municipalities for centuries before being forcibly merged by Hitler in 1935, ahead of the 1936 Winter Olympics. Their character remains distinct: Garmisch is more modern, while Partenkirchen retains its houses with painted trompe-l'oeil facades, a Bavarian decorative style called Lüftlmalerei.

Dedicate the afternoon to the Zugspitze, Germany's highest point at 2,962 meters, on the Austrian border. In clear weather, the panorama extends to Italy. A 90-year-old cog railway connects Garmisch to the glacier plateau at 2,600 meters, from where a 1,000-meter cable car ascends to the summit. The cable car from Lake Eibsee (opened in 2017) holds three world records: tallest pylon tower, greatest vertical rise, and longest free span.

activities.text: Walk around Lake Eibsee, with its turquoise waters surrounded by limestone rocks and pine forests, at the foot of the Zugspitze. The complete loop is about 7 kilometers. In summer, rowboats can be rented on site.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns
German Alpine Road: Lake Constance to Königssee

visits.text: Half-timbered village, true. But behind the façades: a Benedictine abbey founded by the wandering monk Pirmin around 725, which survived the Rhine wars, the Reformation, Louis XIV's troops in 1689, and the secularization of 1803. The baroque church with its 1716 bell tower still stands. The Marktplatz deserves a stop—20 minutes is enough, then head to Offenburg 10 km away.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Gera is the birthplace of Otto Dix (1891-1969), one of the most important German painters of the 20th century. Born into a working-class family in the village of Untermhaus, he studied decorative arts in Dresden before leaving for war in 1914. His paintings of the Great War—The Trench, the War Cripples—are among the rawest and most disturbing visual testimonies of that conflict. In 1937, the Nazis confiscated 170 of his works as "degenerate art." The Otto Dix House and the Orangery of the Princes of Reuss, dedicated to him, house a selection of paintings and engravings including the famous Der Krieg (The War) series from 1924.

Gera's market square is one of Thuringia's most beautiful, with its Baroque town hall with mansard roof and entrance tower built after the 1686 fire. The neighboring Salvatorkirche is a Thuringian Baroque masterpiece with its frescoed ceiling and ornate galleries.

The Höhler are a network of vaulted cellars dug under the old town in the 17th and 18th centuries to store beer. Since the late 1980s, a section has been open as a museum. In October, the cellars host the Höhler Festival, one of Thuringia's largest folk festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Balthasar Neumann designed this basilica between 1730 and 1739, and the result is a paradox: an austere twin-towered façade for an incandescent Baroque interior. Franconia's main pilgrimage site for the Trinity, it receives about a hundred pilgrimage groups annually according to the parish site. The Friday guided tour at 10:30 a.m. includes an organ concert from May to October.

The Burg Gößweinstein, dating at least to the 11th century according to the first mention in 1076, faces the basilica on the opposite outcrop. Legend says Wagner found inspiration here for the Grail castle in Parsifal - nothing proven, but the silhouette confirms the intuition. Panoramic beer garden open Easter to October.

activities.text: On the Burg terrace, the beer garden overlooks the confluence of the Wiesent, Püttlach, and Ailsbach in the valley. The Balthasar-Neumann-Rundwanderweg (3.6 km from the village) connects the basilica and fortress via a trail that multiplies viewing angles. A car-free detour possible from Gößweinstein.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Görlitz is Germany's best-preserved city—more than 4,000 listed historic buildings in the center, covering five centuries of architecture from late Gothic to Art Nouveau, because the city emerged intact from World War II. Its prosperity in the 15th century rested on cloth manufacturing and trade in woad, a highly prized blue pigment. The Untermarkt, the lower square, is the heart of the old town: its colorful Renaissance and Baroque facades, wrought-iron balconies, and vaulted passages form one of Saxony's most beautiful ensembles. The city serves so often as a film location it's called "Görliwood"—Wes Anderson's The Grand Budapest Hotel, Inglourious Basterds, and The Reader were filmed here.

The Görlitzer Warenhaus, the former Art Nouveau department store from 1913 on Demianiplatz, is the building used as the hotel lobby in The Grand Budapest Hotel. Its large glass-roofed interior courtyard and multi-level shopping galleries are striking—the building was threatened with demolition before the filming saved it.

The Schönhof, dating from 1526, is one of Germany's oldest bourgeois Renaissance houses still preserved in their original state. It houses the Silesian Museum, which documents the history and art of the Silesia region shared between Germany, Poland, and the Czech Republic.

The Church of St. Peter and Paul stands directly on the banks of the Neisse, the border with Poland. Its Baroque "sun organ," built in 1703 by Eugenio Casparini, is one of Saxony's most remarkable instruments—its monumental case has remained intact since its creation.

activities.text: Cross the Old Town Bridge (Altstadtbrücke) to Zgorzelec, the Polish side. The pedestrian bridge rebuilt in 2004 bears a European Union plaque at its center. You can stroll for a few minutes on the Zgorzelec side—the two municipal councils have jointly managed many facilities since the symbolic reunification of the two banks.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: A thousand years of ore without interruption: the Rammelsberg is, according to UNESCO, the only deposit in the world exploited continuously for over 1,000 years (until 1988), listed along with the old town in 1992. The guided tour descends into the galleries aboard the old miners' train—allow 2.5 hours, around €18. Book ahead in high season. The Feuergezähe Gewölbe from the 13th century, still visible on site, is presented by UNESCO sources as the oldest vaulted mining chamber in Europe.

At night, Goslar's alleys smell of damp timber and ancient stone—roughly 1,500 half-timbered houses compressed into one square kilometer. The Kaiserpfalz (Imperial Palace), founded by the Ottonians around 1005 and brought to its apex by the Salians in the 11th century, is the largest preserved secular building of that century in Germany. The Marktplatz with its bronze imperial eagle, and the Frankenberg church with its legends of miner ghosts. The old town is compact: everything walkable in two hours.

The Neuwerkkirche, hidden behind the market square, is one of Goslar's most austere Romanesque structures—no guided tour, no ticket. You push the door and enter. A walled garden behind the apse, stone benches, silence. A useful counterpoint after the Rammelsberg. Generally open mornings until noon.

activities.text: Discover the Upper Harz Water Management System, an ingenious network of dams, reservoirs and channels listed by UNESCO, built to power the historic mines. This 800-year-old feat of hydraulic engineering stretches across the Harz mountains, demonstrating how medieval miners harnessed water power with remarkable sophistication.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: The Georg-August University was founded in 1737 by George II, Elector of Hanover and King of Great Britain, to spread the ideals of the Enlightenment. Napoleon reportedly declared: "Göttingen belongs neither to a state nor to Germany—it is the university of Europe." Since its founding, 47 Nobel Prize laureates have studied or taught here, including Max Born, Werner Heisenberg, and Otto Hahn. One in four residents is a student. The Aula, the main classical-style building on Wilhelmsplatz, is the visible heart of the historic campus.

The Gänseliesel—Little Goose Girl Lison—is the Art Nouveau fountain installed in front of the Altes Rathaus (13th century). Every new doctoral graduate must climb the fountain, offer the statue a bouquet, and plant a kiss on her cheek. The tradition was officially forbidden but always tolerated. On some summer days, the scene repeats almost hourly.
The Old Botanical Garden, created in 1736, houses over 10,000 plant species and 19th-century historic greenhouses—one of Germany's oldest university botanical gardens, still active for research.

The Junkernschänke is a late Gothic 15th-century house with a richly carved facade, one of the most beautiful in the old town. The Stadtwall, a former medieval rampart transformed into a green promenade, circles the historic center.

activities.text: Walk a loop on the Stadtwall, the green ring that follows the route of the old medieval ramparts around Göttingen's center—about 4 km, flat and shaded path.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Friedrich called it 'my beloved ruin'—Eldena Abbey Ruins, founded in 1199 by Cistercians, provided Caspar David Friedrich with his principal motifs for three decades. He was born in Greifswald in 1774, spent his childhood there, and sketched these walls from his adolescent visits. The paintings derived from them are preserved in Berlin and in Greifswald itself at the Pommersches Landesmuseum.

Greifswald's Altstadt stayed standing thanks to a surrender without combat in 1945—the garrison commander chose to surrender rather than let fighting destroy the city. Result: the Gothic brick ensemble is intact. St. Nikolai Cathedral, the Marktplatz, and the university museum (the university dates from 1456) form a compact ensemble visitable in two hours.

activities.text: The Caspar-David-Friedrich-Bildweg follows on foot or by bike from the painter's birthplace in the Altstadt to Eldena Abbey Ruins, passing through the historic port and the fishing village of Wieck with its wooden drawbridge. The route is about 6 km and takes 2 hours on foot. The Caspar-David-Friedrich-Zentrum in the birthplace house opens onto his work and local motifs.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Three concrete cubes connected by terraces overlook the former tagebau filling with water before your eyes: Großräschen's IBA-Terrassen are the visitor center for the Lausitzer Seenland, born from the mining basin's conversion. The 'IBA meets IBA' exhibition traces 100 years of German Bauausstellungen. Check whether the renovation closure has been lifted before coming.

activities.text: If you're there on a Thursday, the iba-aktiv-tours bus tour leaves the IBA-Terrassen at 10 a.m. to show every stage of Seenland filling: active tagebau, navigable canals, floating houses. Count on about 3 hours. On the first and third Sundays of the month, a public version is also offered.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Schloss Güstrow is considered northern Germany's finest Renaissance building. The Ernst Barlach workshop-museum, showcasing the expressionist sculptor who lived here, displays his tormented bronzes in a Gothic chapel. The town is calm, far from tourist circuits.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Step back in time at the Black Forest Open-Air Museum (Vogtsbauernhof), an extraordinary village of meticulously rescued historic farm buildings spanning 400 years of rural life. Each farmhouse, barn and mill has been carefully dismantled, transported and reconstructed on this picturesque hillside site, complete with authentic furnishings and equipment. This living museum offers an unparalleled window into the traditional way of life in the Black Forest region.

Watch craftspeople demonstrate traditional skills in the working wooden toyshop, where hand-carved Black Forest toys are made using centuries-old techniques. If you're lucky, the wood-fired bread ovens will be in operation, filling the air with the irresistible aroma of fresh-baked loaves. Farm animals roam the grounds, adding to the authentic atmosphere of a working agricultural community from centuries past.

Follow the excellent audio tour through the farmhouses to understand how families lived, worked and survived the harsh mountain winters. Interactive exhibits bring agricultural techniques and domestic life vividly to life, engaging visitors of all ages. The traditional Black Forest houses with their distinctive low-sweeping roofs represent architectural solutions to the region's heavy snowfall.

activities.text: Purchase authentic Black Forest handicrafts and traditional products directly from the museum workshops

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Hainich National Park is Germany's largest contiguous deciduous forest with its 13,000 hectares. Established on December 31, 1997, it is Thuringia's only national park. Its uniqueness stems from its military past: vast sectors were restricted zones during the Cold War, leaving them to develop freely for decades. The forest has thus returned to an almost primeval state, with old-growth trees, abundant deadwood, and more than 1,600 identified fungus species. The 1,573-hectare core zone has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2011 as part of the «Ancient and Primeval Beech Forests of Europe». A 9.5 km heritage trail traverses the core zone.

The Baumkronenpfad - canopy walkway - starts at the park center in Thiemsburg. Over 540 meters, the path gradually rises to 24 meters, at the level of the beech crowns, then climbs to an observation platform at 44 meters: Germany's highest platform of its kind, with unobstructed views across all of Hainich and the Thuringian Basin. The route is fully wheelchair-accessible thanks to an elevator. Panels explain at each level the life of the canopy and species diversity.

The national park center features an interactive exhibition on Hainich's limestone geology, forest fauna, and conservation challenges. A reconstructed «root cave» illustrates subterranean life. The center also provides information on the wildcat - an emblematic species visible at the dedicated village of Hütscheroda, a few kilometers away.

activities.text: Hike the heritage trail, a 9.5 km circuit through the primeval beech forest. The path follows deadwood zones, skirts forest pools, and crosses clearings where the black woodpecker - Europe's largest woodpecker - excavates its holes in old trunks.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: The Wikinger Museum Haithabu, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2018 alongside the Danevirke, is one of Germany's most important archaeological museums. Between the 8th and 11th centuries, Haithabu was one of Northern Europe's largest trading ports — around 3,000 people are estimated to have lived here in about 950 AD. The semicircular earthwork that enclosed the town is still visible in the landscape today.

The Danevirke, a 30-km wall that cut across the Jutland peninsula from the Schlei to the North Sea, was built to defend the Viking kingdom against the Franks. It shares its UNESCO listing with Haithabu. A restored section of the earthwork wall can be visited a few kilometres to the north.

activities.text: Walk the boardwalk paths of the Haithabu open-air museum, where seven Viking longhouses have been reconstructed full-size within the ancient rampart. Craftspeople in period costume work here in season. A reconstructed longship is moored at the jetty — boat trips are offered in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Admire Halberstadt Cathedral, a Gothic masterpiece housing one of Europe's most important ecclesiastical treasures. This majestic cathedral, built on the model of French cathedrals, impresses with its harmonious proportions and medieval stained glass windows. St. Martin's Church completes this exceptional religious heritage with its preserved Romanesque architecture.

Explore the Klusfelsen near Halberstadt, impressive sandstone rock formations where medieval troglodyte chapels were carved. These sacred caves, sculpted into the rock by medieval hermits, create a mystical landscape unique in northern Germany. Hiking trails allow you to discover this site between nature and spirituality.

Visit the sandstone caves and Regenstein Castle near Blankenburg, a medieval fortress carved directly into the rock. This cliff-top citadel offers spectacular views over the Harz foothills and testifies to medieval defensive ingenuity. The cave dwellings of Langenstein, some inhabited until 1916, reveal an unusual way of life perpetuated for centuries.

activities.text: Hike to the Blankenburg sandstone caves and discover the rock castle of Regenstein

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Discover Halle-on-Saale, birthplace of the famous composer George Frideric Handel whose monument proudly adorns the market square. This cultural metropolis, the most populous in Saxony-Anhalt, houses the Martin Luther University of Halle-Wittenberg, one of Germany's oldest founded in 1502. The academic and artistic atmosphere permeates every street of this multifaceted city.

Contemplate Halle Cathedral and the Market Church of Our Lady with its four emblematic towers dominating the urban skyline. These late Gothic edifices testify to the religious and economic power of the town in the Middle Ages. Giebichenstein Castle, perched on a rocky promontory above the Saale, today houses a prestigious School of Fine Arts.

Marvel at the Nebra Sky Disc at the State Museum of Prehistory, the oldest concrete depiction of the sky ever discovered. This 3,600-year-old bronze disc, adorned with gold representing the sun, moon, and stars, revolutionized our understanding of Bronze Age astronomical knowledge. The Francke Foundations, a Baroque architectural complex dedicated to Pietist education, complete this exceptional heritage.

activities.text: Visit the Halloren Museum and taste the famous chocolate balls, specialty of Germany's oldest chocolate factory still in operation

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The smell of mud and roasted coffee rises from the canals of the Speicherstadt in the early morning. This district of red-brick warehouses was built between 1883 and 1927 on wooden pilings and designated UNESCO in 2015 alongside the Kontorhausviertel. Its 26 hectares and its fleetes (internal canals) make it one of the largest historical warehouse complexes in Northern Europe. Arrive before 9 AM—tour groups start pouring in from 10 AM.

The Speicherstadt vanishes underfoot—and there rises the Elbphilharmonie, perched atop the former Kaispeicher A warehouse from 1963 like a glass iceberg on brick. Inaugurated in 2017 following designs by Herzog & de Meuron, it soars to 110 m. The Grand Hall is considered one of the world's best-designed concert halls acoustically, with its stucco walls cut like lace. The Plaza on the 8th floor is free and open daily.

The Elbphilharmonie still divides Hamburgers—a massive budget overrun, ten years late, and yet no one regrets it anymore. The Reeperbahn a few streets away is the flip side of the city: this is where the Beatles played their first real gigs in 1960-1962 in the clubs on Große Freiheit. At night, the street becomes one of Germany's liveliest—worth seeing even if music isn't your priority.

The Kunsthalle Hamburg, founded in 1869, is one of Northern Germany's largest art museums. Seven centuries of painting coexist here, from Gothic art to contemporary works—with a dense collection of Caspar David Friedrich that echoes Greifswald later on the route. The Neo-Renaissance building adjoins a separate modern gallery. Closed Monday; allow at least 1.5 hours.

HafenCity unfolds along the Elbe on former port lands as Europe's largest intra-city urban renewal project according to developers' data—157 hectares under development until around 2030. On the Elbpromenade side, the Marco-Polo-Terrassen offer a viewpoint over the Elbe free from any construction, rare in a port city.

The Reeperbahn deserves an hour in the evening, even if clubs don't interest you: this is where the Beatles myth began in the Große Freiheit clubs between 1960 and 1962, before Liverpool. The Beatlemania Museum and commemorative panels on Star-Club Street trace this precise chapter. The street is lively, safe, and lined with theaters.

activities.text: If you climb to the Elbphilharmonie Plaza on a windless day, the panorama over the Elbe, the Speicherstadt, and the harbor cranes captures in a single image what makes Hamburg distinctive. Access is free, but concert tickets sell out months in advance—book now if you want to hear something.

Walk or bike along the Elbe from HafenCity to the Fischmarkt: on Sunday mornings from 5 AM, this century-old fish market spills herring, fruit, and vendors' shouts onto the quay for three hours. It's kitschy and sincere—a way to see Hamburg outside the polished circuits.

An Alster ferry crossing (Alsterdampfer) from Jungfernstieg takes 1 hour and loops the two inner lakes that carve through the city. Seen from the water, Hamburg changes completely: church steeples, ambassadorial villas, and gardens replace the industrial docks. Boats run year-round, more frequently April to October.

Dine in the Schanzenviertel, the alternative district north of the Reeperbahn, before heading to the airport. The restaurants on Schulterblatt are open evenings, prices reasonable, the atmosphere less formatted than HafenCity. Hamburg Airport is 15 min by U-Bahn from Hauptbahnhof.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: The Fagus Factory is a shoe-last factory built starting in 1911 by Walter Gropius—at 27, his first independent commission—in collaboration with Adolf Meyer. Gropius eliminated load-bearing corner pillars and placed the structure inside the building, creating fully glazed corners—a radical innovation for 1911. This curtain wall principle in glass, which he would perfect at the Bauhaus in Dessau, was used here for the first time. The factory still operates today and continues to produce shoe lasts. UNESCO-listed since 2011—the only World Heritage site still in production.

Hameln is built along the Weser, with an old town whose half-timbered houses and 16th- and 17th-century sandstone facades illustrate the Weser Renaissance style. In the cobblestones, thousands of small bronze rat-shaped plaques guide visitors to the main sites—a nod to the legend whose earliest written traces date back to a chronicle from 1384.

The Rattenfängerhaus (Pied Piper House), built in 1602, bears on its facade an inscription recalling the event of June 26, 1284: "130 children born in Hamelin were led away by a piper dressed in many colors to Calvary near the Koppen and disappeared there." Some historians see in this legend an allegory of eastward colonization migrations (Ostsiedlung) in the 13th century—the rats, incidentally, only appear in 16th-century versions.

The Hochzeitshaus (Wedding House), built between 1610 and 1617, is the most imposing Renaissance building on the central square. Its mechanical carillon plays the Pied Piper melody three times daily with moving figures—crowds gather to watch.

St. Boniface's Minster (Münster St. Bonifatius) is Hameln's oldest religious building, its origins tracing back to a Benedictine monastery founded in 851. The current church preserves Romanesque and Gothic elements.

activities.text: Walk along the Weser from the historic center—the river borders the old town along its entire west side.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Discover Hanover, the vibrant regional capital of Lower Saxony, where modern urban life meets rich historical heritage. The city boasts magnificent royal gardens, world-class museums, and a dynamic cultural scene that hosts the world's largest shooting competition and the spectacular International Fireworks Competition. From the elegant Herrenhausen Gardens to the bustling city center, Hanover offers endless discoveries.

Visit the enchanting Marienburg Castle, a fairy-tale Gothic Revival palace rising majestically near Hanover. Built in the 19th century for Queen Marie of Hanover, this romantic castle features ornate towers, grand halls, and stunning views across the surrounding countryside. The castle museum showcases royal treasures and offers glimpses into aristocratic life.

activities.text: Stroll through the magnificent Herrenhausen Gardens, one of Europe's most impressive baroque garden ensembles. The Great Garden features geometric precision with fountains, sculptures, and the famous Grand Fountain reaching 80 meters high, while the surrounding botanical collections offer year-round floral displays.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Alexander von Humboldt reportedly called it one of the seven most beautifully situated towns in the world — and even if that quote is apocryphal, you understand it immediately here. Hann. Münden is a half-timbered town of more than 700 buildings, ringed by forests and set at the junction of the Werra, the Fulda, and the newborn Weser.

On the Tanzwerder island, the Werra and Fulda merge into the Weser at the foot of the 1899 Weserstein — a small obelisk bearing an inscription quoted in every German geography textbook. The Welfenschloss overlooks the confluence from the bank, its Weser Renaissance structure still standing intact.

activities.text: Take the river boat towards Kassel if the timetable allows: seasonal services on the Weser connect Hann. Münden to Reinhardshagen and sometimes beyond. Otherwise, follow the Weser-Radweg south by bike as far as the wooded banks of Reinhardshagen — 12 kilometres, flat all the way.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Trek the famous Harz Witches' Trail through Germany's northernmost highland region, where stunning panoramic views reward every climb. This legendary trail winds through ancient forests steeped in folklore, offering spectacular vistas across valleys and peaks that have inspired myths for centuries. The Harz mountains combine natural beauty with mystical atmosphere like nowhere else in Germany.

activities.text: Experience the Harz National Park's 3,000 kilometers of trails perfect for mountain biking adventures through pristine wilderness. From gentle forest paths to challenging alpine routes, the varied terrain caters to all skill levels while showcasing the region's diverse landscapes of rocky outcrops, dense woodlands, and crystal-clear mountain streams.

In winter, glide through the white snowy countryside on cross-country skis across the Harz's well-maintained trails. The region transforms into a winter wonderland offering downhill skiing, snowshoeing, and toboggan runs amidst snow-dusted pine forests and frozen waterfalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Locals call it the Kornstadt: Haselünne, the oldest town in Emsland, still counts three Korn distilleries (Berentzen, Heydt, Rosche). In the 19th century, there were 26. The Berentzen Hof offers tours with tasting.
Stop by the Wacholderhain at the town's edge, a nature reserve of junipers across more than 30 hectares. Semi-wild Tarpan horses graze there. Marked trails, free access.

activities.text: The Korndiplom at Rosche is kitsch but well done: you take an exam on historic school benches at the Korn-Academie, then taste the result. Reservation required.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Locals say she thought like a man and suffered like a woman — Annette von Droste-Hülshoff was born here in 1797, in this Renaissance moated castle, and spent the first thirty years of her life within its walls. Burg Hülshoff is now a literary museum with six original rooms, a library, and family portraits. The restaurant in the vaulted cellars is open from April to November.

The seven-kilometre Lyrikweg links Burg Hülshoff to Haus Rüschhaus near Münster, tracing a path Annette von Droste-Hülshoff walked hundreds of times. Outdoor poetry stations, hedgerows, open fields — an open-air museum without gates or crowds. Best walked in the late afternoon.

activities.text: After your visit, stop in at Droste Café 1797 in the castle's vaulted cellar — regional cakes and a view over the moat. Check the Burg website for ongoing restoration works: some areas may be closed depending on the phase.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Germany's only cave navigable by boat: the Wimsener Höhle is explored by rowboat on an underground river that gushes from a limestone cliff. The journey lasts about ten minutes in total silence, broken only by the lapping of water. Open April to October.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Behold Burg Hohenzollern, one of Germany's most spectacular castles and ancestral seat of the Prussian royal family who later became German Emperors. This fairy-tale fortress, rebuilt in its current neo-Gothic splendor in 1850, crowns a dramatic conical peak rising 855 meters above the Swabian countryside. The castle rivals Bavaria's famous Neuschwanstein in beauty and surpasses it in authentic royal connection.

Tour the gloriously opulent interiors, where visitors don enormous felt slippers to protect the precious floors while children can dress in royal costumes for an immersive experience. The family still uses the castle for special events, so verify opening times before your visit to avoid disappointment. The treasury displays Prussian crown jewels and personal effects of Frederick the Great.

Hike to the neighboring Zeller Horn viewpoint for the iconic photograph of the castle floating above clouds that has made Burg Hohenzollern famous worldwide. The approach road itself offers increasingly dramatic views as the castle emerges from the forested hilltop. Hot air balloon rides over the castle at sunset provide an unforgettable perspective on this architectural marvel.

activities.text: Visit during Advent for the famous Christmas market held within the castle walls

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Marvel at Heidelberg Castle, one of Germany's most romantic ruins perched high above the red-roofed old town. This magnificent Renaissance fortress, partially destroyed by French troops in the 17th century, offers breathtaking views over the Neckar Valley and houses the world's largest wine barrel. The atmospheric ruins create an unforgettable silhouette against the sky that has inspired poets and artists for centuries.
