Explore the Kurpfälzisches Museum on the pedestrianized main street, where fascinating exhibits reveal the Celtic and Roman civilizations that inhabited this region long before the castle was built. The museum houses remarkable archaeological treasures and artwork spanning millennia of human settlement in the Neckar Valley. This cultural institution provides essential context for understanding Heidelberg's deep historical roots.

Wander through Heidelberg's enchanting old town, a maze of cobblestone streets, baroque churches and historic university buildings that make it one of Germany's most picturesque cities. The famous Alte Brücke (Old Bridge) offers postcard-perfect views of the castle reflected in the river below. Stay overnight to experience the magical atmosphere after the day-trippers have departed and the city reveals its true romantic character.

activities.text: Join a guided castle tour to discover the fascinating stories behind these evocative ruins and explore hidden chambers

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Founded in 1793 by Duke Frederick Francis I, Heiligendamm is continental Europe's oldest seaside resort. White neoclassical villas line the beach, earning it the nickname Weiße Stadt am Meer (White City by the Sea). The Grand Hotel—site of the 2007 G8 summit—can be admired from outside. 200-meter pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: View from the battlements of Starkenburg: the Rhine plain stretches to the Palatinate, the vines of the Bergstrasse tumble below, Heppenheim looks barely the size of a village. The fortress was built in 1065 to protect the estates of Lorsch Abbey; it's therefore the natural offspring of the previous stop. The observation tower (67 steps) opens in season.

Heppenheim's Altstadt is the Bergstrasse's finest surprise: a Marktplatz with fountain, half-timbered houses, the town hall, and the Stiftskirche St. Peter in a compact, preserved arrangement. On the evening of November 11, 1693, French troops set fire to the town - five buildings survived. What you see today is a meticulous 18th-century reconstruction.

activities.text: Walk up to Starkenburg via the path from the old town (about 30 minutes). The Burgschänke at the top offers Brotzeit and a view: two good reasons to linger. A youth hostel occupies part of the buildings - an original option for an overnight stay.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: The three Kaiserbäder (Heringsdorf, Ahlbeck, Bansin) line up their Belle Époque villas along 12 km of promenade—Europe's longest. Bismarck, Kaiser Wilhelm II, and Fontane took the waters here. Heringsdorf's Seebrücke, 508 meters long, is Germany's largest.

activities.text: Walk the promenade from Heringsdorf to Ahlbeck (4 km) to compare the three Kaiserbäder's styles. Each resort has its own personality. Ahlbeck possesses Germany's oldest Seebrücke (1882), the one from the film Pappa ante Portas.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Fifty-three and a half metres of triumphant Germanic warrior — the Hermannsdenkmal on the Grotenburg is Germany's tallest statue. Before its inauguration in 1875, it was also the tallest in the Western world. Ernst von Bandel devoted 40 years of his life to the project, completed the year before his death. The viewing platform costs €5 to climb.

From the top, the Teutoburg Forest stretches away in every direction — on a clear day, the hills above Bielefeld are visible on the horizon. The monument commemorates Arminius's victory over the Roman legions of Varus in 9 AD, in this very forest. The Waldbühne amphitheatre at the base of the hill hosts concerts from June to August.

activities.text: Walk back down to the car park via the Hermannsweg footpath from the monument: the dense forest, the reddish sandstone trails, and the geology information boards make this twenty-minute descent a real counterpoint to the monument above. The adjacent Teuto-Kletterpark offers treetop adventure courses for families.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Leaving the boat, a forest path leads to Schloss Herrenchiemsee in fifteen minutes (or by carriage, like Ludwig II). The park fountains spout facing the Great Hall of Mirrors, a copy of Versailles'. The king slept there just ten nights before dying in 1886.

Ludwig II bought the island in 1873 and launched construction in 1878 with architect Georg Dollmann. The castle was granted UNESCO status in 2025, along with Neuschwanstein and Linderhof. The ground-floor Ludwig II Museum displays his baptismal gown and death mask.

activities.text: Bavarians call the Chiemsee their "sea." After visiting the castle, walk to the island's southern tip. No tourists, just trees and the lake stretching to the horizon. Allow two hours on the island in total.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: No cars on Hiddensee: you move by bike or on foot. The island stretches 17 km long and 250 meters at its narrowest between Baltic and Bodden. The Dornbusch lighthouse, in the north, offers a 360° panorama. Gerhart Hauptmann, Nobel laureate, had his summer house here (museum open).

activities.text: Rent a bike at the Vitte landing and circle the island in half a day. The west beaches are deserted; the Dornbusch heathland recalls Scotland. Return by evening ferry.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: St. Michael's Church in Hildesheim was built between 1010 and 1022 by Bishop Bernward. Its symmetrical double-apse plan, characteristic of Ottonian Romanesque art, earned it the nickname "heavenly castle." The painted wooden ceiling of the central nave, created in the 13th century on 1,300 oak planks, depicts the Tree of Jesse and measures 27.6 × 8.7 meters—one of the extremely rare monumental paintings from the High Middle Ages to have survived, along with those in Zillis, Switzerland, and Dädesjö, Sweden. UNESCO-listed since 1985.

St. Mary's Cathedral (Hildesheimer Dom) dates back to 872. Its current form as a three-aisled basilica was completed in 1061. It houses the Bernward Doors, cast in bronze in 1015, and the Christ Column from 1020—the first major bronze castings since antiquity. In the cloister grows the thousand-year-old rose: planted according to tradition in the 9th century, destroyed during the 1945 bombings, it has since regrown from its roots. UNESCO-listed since 1985.

Hildesheim's Marktplatz presents a curiosity: its gabled houses were completely destroyed in 1945 and faithfully reconstructed between 1984 and 1989 from precise architectural records. Renaissance and Baroque facades that appear medieval but are less than forty years old.

The Roemer and Pelizaeus Museum houses one of Germany's most important Egyptian art collections, assembled in the 19th century by merchant Wilhelm Pelizaeus from Cairo. Funerary statues, masks, and papyri spanning over 3,000 years.

activities.text: Urban walk between Michaelisplatz and Domhof—the two UNESCO sites connected on foot in about twenty minutes through medieval alleyways.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: Corvey — Westphalia's only UNESCO World Heritage site to date — was founded in 822 on the orders of Louis the Pious. Its Carolingian Westwerk has stood intact since the 9th century and earned the UNESCO designation in 2014. In the princely library of some 75,000 volumes, Hoffmann von Fallersleben was working as librarian when he wrote the Deutschland-Lied in 1841.

The Weser shimmers behind Corvey's Carolingian towers — the site is a two-kilometre walk from the centre of Höxter along the river. Along the way, Höxter's Altstadt lines up its Weser Renaissance half-timbered houses between the stones of the old town walls.

activities.text: Walk the two kilometres from the town centre to Corvey along the Weser — flat, well-marked, following the Weser-Radweg cycle path. The return by bike takes ten minutes. On site, the medieval Remtergarten herb garden is free to enter.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Here, they say love launched the industry: Karl Magnus Hutschenreuther, son of a porcelain painter, founded the region's first factory in 1814 to stay near Johanna Reuß, the forester's daughter. The Porzellanikon Hohenberg, in his villa, displays 2,000 m² of porcelain history. Closed Mondays.
The Burg Hohenberg, mentioned as early as 1222, is considered the best-preserved castle of the Fichtelgebirge. From its towers, on clear days the view carries to the Erzgebirge. Access free and open.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Hohenschwangau Castle is Ludwig II of Bavaria's childhood castle. His father, King Maximilian II, had the dilapidated building restored from 1832 in neo-Gothic style, having medieval legend scenes depicted on the walls—including episodes of the swan knight Lohengrin, with whom Ludwig strongly identified. One room still displays the piano on which Richard Wagner played before the young king. It was here, facing the site of two former ruined castles on the opposite hill, that Ludwig II made the decision in 1868 to build Neuschwanstein there.

The Museum der Bayerischen Könige (Museum of Bavarian Kings), on the shore of the Alpsee, traces the Wittelsbach family's history from the Middle Ages to the 20th century. Collections include portraits, jewelry, and personal objects of Bavarian kings.

Walk along the Alpsee, a glacial lake with transparent waters at the foot of both castles. The complete circuit trail is about 3.5 km. In clear weather, the reflections of the Alps and castles in the water are striking.

activities.text: Late afternoon reconnaissance of the paths leading to Neuschwanstein and the Marienbrücke bridge. The ascent from the Ticket Center takes 30 to 40 minutes on foot. Identifying the route the day before allows you to depart at opening without fumbling.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

activities.text: Hike a section of the Hohenwarte Reservoir trail, a 74.4 km circuit that follows the reservoir's shores through the dense forest of the Thuringian Slate Mountains. The paths at times overlook the Saale's steep meanders from rocky cliffs, creating a landscape evoking Norwegian fjords - hence the local nickname «Thuringian Sea» for this region.

Walk in the Naturpark Thüringer Schiefergebirge/Obere Saale, which covers the Thuringian Slate Mountains and the upper Saale course. The nature park protects landscapes of heathlands, conifer forests, and deep valleys typical of the slate massif.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Five towering rock pillars surge from the forest floor to 40 meters—nothing but stone and sky. The Externsteine have no archaeological proof of Saxon worship, yet their Romanesque Relief of the Descent from the Cross (circa 1160) remains the oldest large medieval rock carving north of the Alps. Look closely at the tilted column beneath Christ's feet: some scholars read it as a defeated Irminsul—the Saxon sacred pillar—bowing to Christianity. Climbing the rocks costs around €4 (April to October). Allow 90 minutes.

activities.text: The Teutoburg Forest unfolds along the Hermannsweg, a 156-kilometer marked trail. From the Externsteine, a 90-minute loop through beech woods shows why Roman legions lost their way here in 9 AD. No technical scrambling, easy terrain. Take the path climbing behind the rock formation and loop back via Bruchsee pond—weekday silence guaranteed.

days_plan.itin_title: Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: Hörnum Odde is Sylt's very end: a sand spit that narrows towards the south and is half-swallowed by winter storms. The island loses several metres of ground here each year on the North Sea side. There is a rare feeling of impermanence — the land is literally slipping away underfoot.

Leuchtturm Hörnum, brought into service in 1907, is the only lighthouse on Sylt accessible on a guided tour — the other four do not open to visitors. Built in cast iron on a 17-metre dune, it rises nearly 50 metres above sea level. Views from the gallery take in Föhr, Amrum and the halligen.

activities.text: Walk to Hörnum Odde point along the marked dune path from the village: 2 km each way, flat terrain, but a headwind can double the effort. Seals often rest on the sandbanks visible from the tip — binoculars are useful.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: The oldest building in town, Hoyerswerda Castle began life in the 13th century as a moated water fortress before being rebuilt as a Renaissance structure in 1592. Today it houses the municipal museum, which traces local history and the Sorbian roots of the region. An anecdote: Napoleon supposedly dined there for a few hours in 1813. A combination ticket covers both castle and neighboring zoo—better value if you do both.

Stroll around Hoyerswerda's Marktplatz, past the Sorbenbrunnen (Sorbian fountain) and the old postal column. The Altes Rathaus, erected in the 15th century, closes off the square with its squat silhouette. A local curiosity: computer inventor Konrad Zuse took his high-school exams in the town's former lyceum. The old town can be covered quickly on foot; the Touristinformation at the Bürgerzentrum provides maps and guided-tour departures.

activities.text: Zoo Hoyerswerda has a history that touches local hearts: residents built it themselves starting in the 1950s, in the former castle park. Across six hectares, it houses roughly 1,100 animals, including brown bears, a Siberian tiger, and zebras. A restaurant and playground occupy the park's center. With children, plan a half-day rather than a rushed hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: The Hundingshütte is a hunting lodge built by Ludwig II in Viking style, inspired by Wagner's Valkyrie. Raw timber structure, a century-old ash tree planted in the roof's center. Thirty-minute walk from Linderhof, gentle ascent.

activities.text: Walk back down the forest trail to the Marokkanisches Haus, another exotic pavilion of Ludwig II's in Linderhof park. The contrast between the Viking chalet and the Moorish salon sums up the king's unbridled imagination.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Theodor Storm called his hometown 'the grey city by the sea' — and he was right. North Sea skies press down hard on Husum. The Theodor-Storm-Zentrum, installed in the house where the writer lived, displays manuscripts and personal objects belonging to the master of Nordic short fiction. Storm is the author of Der Schimmelreiter — read it before you come.

The Schloss vor Husum is worth visiting less for its interior rooms than for its park: in spring, more than four million violet crocuses carpet the lawn beneath the trees — the spectacle lasts two weeks in March and April. It is the only castle on the North Sea coast in Schleswig-Holstein, built between 1577 and 1582.

At the Binnenhafen, ochre and white façades reflect in the still water of the inner basin — the very image of a Nordic postcard. This is the marina today. The real fishing harbour, with its crab cutters, lies at the Außenhafen, 500 metres further towards the sea.

activities.text: Walk through the Nordfriesland Museum to understand the history of the North Frisians — a seafaring people settled on this coast since the Middle Ages, whose language is still spoken in a handful of villages. Fishing implements, storm charts and traditional costumes sum up a thousand years of coastal life.

If it is April, the castle park erupts in crocuses for two weeks — now or never. Outside the flowering season, the Herzoginnengarten and Schlosspark take about twenty minutes to walk through. The smoked herring Brötchen stalls on the Binnenhafen quay open from 8am.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Twenty-seven euros to see flowers is steep. But Mainau Island delivers what it promises: 45 hectares of gardens, a tropical Schmetterlingshaus (Germany's second-largest butterfly house), a Bernadotte baroque palace, and lake views at every turn. Children free up to age 12.

Take the boat from Konstanz to arrive by lake: the approach is more beautiful than the road bridge. The 18th-century Deutschordensschloss stands at the center, private residence of Count Björn Bernadotte. The Palmenhaus shelters more than 20 palm species.

activities.text: In May, one million tulips carpet Mainau's slopes. In summer, roses take over in the Italienischer Rosengarten. In autumn, 12,000 dahlias. The Schlosscafé, between palms and exhibition space, is the best spot for a break.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: Rothenburg is more famous, but Iphofen has kept its Stadtmauer intact without becoming a theme park. The medieval ramparts with their three gates and towers still encircle the town; you can follow them entirely along the Herrengraben. The Rödelseer Tor, built between 1455 and 1466, is the oldest of the three and serves as the town's official symbol.

The Knauf Museum at the edge of the Marktplatz presents reliefs from the great cultures of Antiquity through modern times - five millennia, four continents, in a contemporary building constructed with stones from the Schwanberg castle destroyed in 1525. The Knauf company, based here since 1936, finances the museum.

activities.text: The vines of the Julius-Echter-Berg bake in the sun above Iphofen - the walk from town takes about twenty minutes to reach the terroir f viewpoint with its interpretation panels. More than twenty winemakers work in the town; some offer Weinproben directly in their cellars without reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Five oaks aged 600 to 1,000 years stand in Ivenack Park, Germany's first National Natural Monument. Their trunks exceed 14 meters in circumference. A Baumkronenpfad—canopy walkway—lets you approach them at crown height. Fallow deer roam free in the enclosure.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Perched atop Tempelberg hill, Jagdschloss Granitz overlooks Rügen's beech forests. Its central tower houses a freestanding spiral cast-iron staircase of 154 steps. The view from the top sweeps the island's east coast. Access on foot, by carriage, or via the Rasender Roland.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Schiller's Garden House in Jena is the modest dwelling the poet occupied from 1797, when he was teaching at the university. It's in this pastoral setting that he began his creative collaboration with Goethe - who had brought him to Jena - and started «Wallenstein». The original building is preserved with some period furniture.

The Zeiss Planetarium, inaugurated July 18, 1926, is the world's oldest continuously operating projection planetarium. It was designed by engineer Walther Bauersfeld of Carl Zeiss, who simultaneously developed the thin-shell concrete dome - an architectural invention that revolutionized 20th-century construction. The dome measures 25 meters in diameter. It's also Germany's largest planetarium. Current projections use a Zeiss Velvet full-dome laser system. The original idea dates to 1913, when the founder of the Deutsches Museum approached Zeiss for a «Himmelsschau» - a representation of the sky.

The JenTower is a 160-meter cylindrical tower built during the GDR era to serve as a Zeiss research center - hence its local nickname «Keksrolle» (cookie roll). At 128 meters high, the panoramic platform offers views over the Saale's steep valley and the surrounding limestone hills.

The Kunstsammlung Jena (Jena Art Collection) brings together more than 5,000 works ranging from the Middle Ages to the present, with a particular focus on German Expressionism. The museum is housed in several buildings in the historic city center.

activities.text: Hike a section of the SaaleHorizontale, a 91 km trail that follows the limestone ridges overlooking the Saale River around Jena. The cliffs plunge steeply to the river offering sweeping views over the valley and the city. Several trailheads accessible from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: In AD 9, three Roman legions were annihilated here by a Germanic coalition led by Arminius. The site was only identified in 1987, when a British amateur archaeologist officer found Roman coins in a field. The museum exhibits the famous iron Reitermaske and eight Roman gold coins.

From the top of the 40-meter tower, your gaze sweeps the Kalkrieser Berg and the Großes Moor. Below, the Germanic rampart has been reconstructed on the probable route. The 20-hectare park features themed pavilions and a treetop adventure course.

activities.text: Grab the audio guide and walk through the archaeological park following the battle route. The reconstructions of the Germanic palisade and the swamp give a concrete idea of the trap set for the legions. Admission: €9.50 adult.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Kandinsky and Gabriele Münter spent the summer of 1903 in this village at the confluence of the Naab and Vils. Since then, Kallmünz has never lost its painters. The colorful houses of the Vilsgasse, the old millstone, the gardens suspended in the rock: everything here is, in the literal sense, picturesque.

The Haus ohne Dach, carved into the limestone cliff above the Vilsgasse, was inhabited until 1937. You can still make out a corridor, two storage niches and a smoking chamber. Open once a year, on Tag des offenen Denkmals.

activities.text: Walk down to the confluence of the Naab and Vils for the finest view of the Burgruine. The ruin itself, reached by a steep staircase, dates to the 13th century and offers a view covering the entire Naabtal.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Climb Bad Tölz's Kalvarienberg for the panorama over the Isar valley and the Karwendel range. At the top, the Heilig-Kreuz-Kirche houses a Heilige Stiege, a copy of Rome's Scala Santa. Gentle ascent, fifteen minutes.

activities.text: From the Kalvarienberg summit, the baroque stations of the cross descend past the Leonhardikapelle. The panorama stretches from Bad Tölz's rooftops to the Karwendel massif. Descent in ten minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Kamenz's red Rathaus stands out with its Venetian palazzo airs: built around 1847–1848 in an Italian neo-Renaissance style, it marks the old town. Kamenz is the birthplace of Enlightenment writer Gotthold Ephraim Lessing, born here in 1729. Worth knowing: the Lessing Museum is closed for renovations until spring 2027, so you follow his traces around town, not in the museum. Park near the center—everything is walkable afterward.

Completed around 1510, the Klosterkirche St. Annen blends church and sacred-art museum on the northern edge of Kamenz's old town. It preserves a rare ensemble of carved late-Gothic altarpieces, among the religious treasures of Lusatia. The painting 'Law and Grace,' painted in 1542 by a pupil of Cranach, numbers among the notable pieces. Check opening hours—they're more restricted outside peak season.

Climb the 117 steps of the Roter Turm, a remnant of Kamenz's old fortifications, a former guardpost turned prison. From its 32-meter height, the view sweeps across the old town's rooftops. The key is picked up at Kamenz-Information, so stop by before the ascent. The opening slot is limited—midday is usually your best bet.

activities.text: If blooms attract you, climb the Hutberg, the hill on Kamenz's outskirts, planted with rhododendrons and azaleas that burst into color in spring. The landscaped park rises gently to the Hutbergbühne, an amphitheater-style open-air stage. At the summit, a small viewing platform overlooks the town. Spring for flowers, summer for a concert: choose according to the program.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Kampen carries the steepest price tag on Sylt — €8 breakfasts, luxury boutiques, villas tucked behind hedgerows. But the beach and the cliff are free. The Rotes Kliff owes its name to the iron-rich clay that turns blood-red in the late afternoon light — 30 metres of cliff face stretching 4 km between Kampen and Wenningstedt.

The Rotes Kliff runs 4 km along Sylt's west coast between Kampen and Wenningstedt: the 30-metre cliff crumbles steadily into the sea — keep 5 metres back from the edge. A wooden boardwalk has run along the top since 1979, designated a nature reserve. Kampen Lighthouse dates from 1855 and stands 40 metres tall.

activities.text: Climb the Uwe-Düne via 110 wooden steps from Kampen: at 52.5 metres, it is Sylt's highest natural point. The wooden platform gives a 360° view over the island, from List in the north to Hörnum in the south. Free and accessible at any time.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Three towers on a Nordic promontory—it's too many, and yet each deserves a stop. The Schinkelturm (1827, the oldest) was designed following plans by the Prussian Architecture Commission and remains the second-oldest lighthouse on the German Baltic coast. The Tall Lighthouse (1902, 35 m) is active. The Peilturm (1927) served as a radio beacon for the Sassnitz-Trelleborg ferry line.

The Schinkelturm, built in 1827 in square red brick, is the oldest lighthouse still standing on the German Baltic coast after Travemünde lighthouse. At its feet: the Burgwall of Jaromarsburg, a Slavic rampart from the 6th to 12th centuries protecting the temple of the god Svantovit. In 1168, Danish King Valdemar I conquered and razed it. Only the circular embankment remains.

activities.text: Leave the car at Putgarten (paid parking) and walk the 1.3 km to the cape's tip—no traffic, sea wind, views over the 45-m cliff. In good weather, the Danish coast of Møn is visible on the horizon, about 30 km away. The village of Vitt, 200 m below the cape, is an untouched fishing hamlet with an octagonal 19th-century chapel.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Three bell towers, three naves, three altars, three round towers: everything comes in threes in the Dreifaltigkeitskirche Kappl. Georg Dientzenhofer built it between 1685 and 1689 on a cloverleaf plan. From the Glasberg at 599 m, the view embraces the Egerland all the way to Czechia. Free admission, open 8 a.m. - 6 p.m. April to October.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe is Europe's largest hillside park (240 hectares) and was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2013. Art historian Georg Dehio described it as "perhaps the most grandiose combination the Baroque ever dared between architecture and landscape." Construction of the park began in 1689 at the initiative of Landgrave Carl of Hesse-Kassel and continued for 150 years. Reservoirs and a hydro-pneumatic system feed a 350-meter Baroque cascade, a sound-effect grotto, rapids, and the Great Fountain—a 50-meter geyser, the world's tallest when created in 1767. The water features operate every Wednesday, Sunday, and public holiday from May to October.

The Hercules monument dominates the Bergpark from an elevation of 596 meters. The hammered copper statue, standing 8.30 meters tall, reproduces the 3rd-century Farnese Hercules. It's visible from across the city and serves as Kassel's symbol. Below the statue, a pyramidal octagon houses the head of the cascade. The entire ensemble—statue, octagon, and grotto—measures 71 meters in total height.

Kassel's documenta is the world's most important contemporary art exhibition. Held since 1955 every five years for 100 days, it takes place across multiple city venues—including the Fridericianum (1779, continental Europe's first public museum), the Documenta-Halle, and urban spaces. Permanent works from past exhibitions are visible throughout the city year-round.

The Grimmwelt, opened in 2015, is the world's largest exhibition space devoted to the Brothers Grimm's work. Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm worked in Kassel in the early 19th century, where they began collecting folk tales from the region. The annotated copies of Children's and Household Tales are inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register. The museum also presents their role as linguists and their contribution to the German dictionary.

activities.text: Walk through the iconic documenta sites in the city: the Fridericianum on Friedrichsplatz, the Documenta-Halle, and the in-situ works scattered throughout Kassel's public spaces. Even outside exhibition periods, permanent installations are visible.

Bergparkleuchten evening: several times a year, the Bergpark and its monuments are illuminated by colorful light installations. The water jets and cascades are lit up at nightfall, producing a particularly impressive spectacle on the Karlsberg slopes.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks
From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: The pastel façades along Kaufbeuren's Kaiser-Max-Straße survived the war without a scratch. The town, founded as a Frankish royal court around 740, kept its medieval lanes intact. You can walk the center in an hour.

Built in 1420, the Fünfknopfturm is the town's former watchtower. Its five cupolas give it a toy-like air. 92 steps with no elevator, but the panoramic rooftop view rewards the effort. Open weekends in summer.

The Crescentiakloster isn't on the usual tourist circuit, and that's just as well. This 13th-century Franciscan convent still operates. You can visit the Gedenkstätte dedicated to Crescentia Höss, beatified in 2001.

activities.text: If you pass through in July, the Tänzelfest transforms Kaufbeuren for twelve days. It's Bavaria's oldest historical children's festival: parades in medieval costume, torchlit camps. Book accommodation well in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Kempten calls itself Germany's oldest city, and the proof lies beneath your feet. The Greek geographer Strabo mentioned Kambodounon over two thousand years ago. Today it's a lively, unpretentious town of 70,000 souls.

Descend into the underground Erasmuskapelle, a vaulted space rediscovered beneath the market square. The atmosphere is striking: medieval walls and modern scenographic lighting. Open Tuesday to Sunday.

The gilded stucco of the Fürstäbtliche Residenz hits you the moment you step into the Thronsaal. Bavaria's first baroque residence, built in the mid-17th century. The Prunkräume rank among the land's finest rococo interiors.

Beneath your feet, the Cambodunum archaeological park reconstructs a Roman quarter with baths, forum, and temples. Open March through November, Tuesday to Sunday 10 a.m.–5 p.m. Allow an hour for the visit.

activities.text: If you're traveling with children, the Alpin-Museum inside the Allgäu-Museum tells the story of mountain life at eye level. Interactive and well done. Combinable with the Residenz on the same ticket.

The people of Kempten call the Rathausplatz their living room. In the morning, cafés roll out their tables and the vibe turns Italian. Grab a Milchkaffee facing St. Mang church and its 66-meter bell tower.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Kiel was 80% destroyed in 1944, but its waterfront was rebuilt with care around the Kieler Förde — the 17-km fjord that opens onto the Baltic. The Kiellinie, a 4-km promenade along the fjord's west bank, is the meeting point of joggers, cyclists and families. In June, Kieler Woche turns it into one of the world's largest sailing regattas.

The Kiellinie runs 4 km along the Förde from the city centre to the botanical garden: Scandinavian ferries, cargo ships and racing yachts pass within metres. At the far end, the Düsternbrooker Gehölz is a waterside woodland park.

activities.text: Take the free ferry between the two banks of the Kieler Förde — the Schlepp- und Fährgesellschaft runs crossings every 15 minutes. Commercial port on the east side, marina and sailboats on the west. Five minutes on the water, panorama included.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Hard to resist life-size dinosaurs: Saurierpark Kleinwelka, Germany's largest dinosaur park, lines up more than 200 sculptures across sixteen hectares. It all started with sculptor Franz Gruß's private garden, who shaped his first concrete dinos as early as 1978. Entry now passes through the 'Mitoseum,' opened in 2017. Open mainly April through November; buy tickets online to skip the queue.

activities.text: Lose yourself for real in the Irrgarten Kleinwelka, the maze attached to the dinosaur park. Hedges, dead ends, and false trails test young and old alike, especially at visit's end when legs tire. Count on half an hour to an hour depending on your sense of direction. Separate ticket from the Saurierpark—check if a combination deal exists on the day of your visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Film buffs recognize these corridors instantly: during winter 1985-1986, Jean-Jacques Annaud shot the interiors of The Name of the Rose here with Sean Connery. Founded in 1136 by twelve Cistercian monks, Eberbach Abbey ranks among Germany's best-preserved abbeys. The basilica, the 13th-century lay brothers' hall, and the wine cellars each deserve an hour of exploration. Open daily 10 a.m. to 7 p.m.

Eberbach Abbey's basilica reveals an austere façade that contrasts with late Cistercian exuberance. Romanesque nave, Gothic choir - two centuries of construction legible in a single crossing. Its acoustics make it one of the Rheingau Music Festival's venues each summer: check the program.

activities.text: Finish with a tasting at the Cabinet-Keller or WineBar1136 in the monastery cellars. The Hessische Staatsweingüter GmbH still works the vineyard surrounding the abbey, including the Steinberg, an exclusive property since 1239. A bottle of the house Riesling feinherb costs under €10.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Thirty-two marble columns, a balustrade that ripples like a wave, and a ceiling painted by Franz Georg Hermann: the Bibliothekssaal at Wiblingen Abbey is a visual shock. This 23-meter-long rococo salon, built between 1740 and 1750, soars through two stories. The inscription above the door promises 'all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.'

The Benedictine abbey, founded in 1093, was secularized in 1806. Its books were scattered between Munich and Stuttgart. Only about sixty original volumes remain in the hall today. The small adjoining museum recounts this turbulent history. Around 90,000 visitors annually.

activities.text: Pair your visit with the adjacent Klosterkirche: its ceiling frescoes, painted in late baroque style, rank among Württemberg's most colorful. The complex forms part of the Upper Swabian Baroque Route.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Count Bothmer lived at 10 Downing Street before it became the Prime Minister's residence. A Hanoverian diplomat, he helped George I ascend the throne. His Schloss Bothmer at Klütz (1726-1732) is northern Germany's only English country house. Thirteen brick buildings, 12 hectares of parkland. Around €8.

activities.text: Walk the Festonallee, an avenue of linden trees pruned into garland shapes—a unique ceremonial axis in the north. Open-air concerts in the park in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: The Energiefabrik Knappenrode occupies a former lignite briquette factory, state-of-the-art for its era. The last crew left in 1993; today, this site of the Saxon Museum of Industry tells the rise, fall, and future of the Lusatian coal basin. The red-brick halls provide the backdrop for the visit. Closed Mondays: schedule your stop another day.

activities.text: Serious industrial museum on one side, playground on the other: at the Energiefabrik Knappenrode, the youngest race off into the slide-tunnel and labyrinth while adults marvel at the giant excavators. The enormous bucket-wheel machines convey the scale of work done to extract lignite. Factor in time for a coffee at the bistro in between. Workshops and temporary exhibitions rotate—glance at the schedule.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Climb the Herzogstand, a hiker's paradise culminating at 1,731 meters between two strikingly beautiful lakes. This iconic summit offers one of Bavaria's finest panoramas, embracing at a glance the steel-reflecting Kochelsee and the turquoise Walchensee. A cable car allows non-hikers easy access to this natural spectacle.

Discover the Walchensee, one of Germany's deepest and purest Alpine lakes. Its intensely blue waters, often compared to tropical lagoons, contrast magnificently with the surrounding snow-capped peaks. This natural dam lake served as the setting for numerous films and remains a well-kept secret among Bavarians.

Contemplate the Kochelsee, a more peaceful and accessible lake bordered by green meadows and traditional villages. It was here that painter Franz Marc, a major figure of German Expressionism, found inspiration for his famous works. The Franz Marc Museum in the nearby village pays tribute to this master of color.

activities.text: Panoramic ridge hike on the Herzogstand

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Cologne Cathedral is Germany's most visited monument. Its construction began in 1248 at the initiative of the archbishop to house the Shrine of the Three Kings, brought from Milan in 1164 by Frederick Barbarossa—the golden reliquary is still visible in the choir. Work stopped in 1560 for lack of funds and only resumed in 1842 under the impulse of Romanticism and Prussian nationalism. The building was completed in 1880, after 632 years of construction. Its twin towers reach 157 meters and made the cathedral the world's tallest building from 1880 to 1884, until the inauguration of the Washington Monument. Its west façade, over 7,100 square meters, is the largest of any cathedral in the world. The 533 steps of the south tower lead to a panoramic platform over Cologne and the Rhine.

Cross the Hohenzollern Bridge to see the thousands of padlocks attached to its railings by passing couples—a tradition launched in the early 2000s that has become one of Cologne's most iconic images. The steel bridge, built between 1907 and 1911, carries both rail traffic and pedestrians and offers the most classic view of the cathedral from the left bank.

The Romano-Germanic Museum, right next to the cathedral, is built around a 2nd-century Dionysus mosaic discovered in situ in 1941 during construction of an air-raid shelter. Cologne—Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium—was one of the most important cities of the Roman Empire north of the Alps, founded in 50 AD. The museum displays the vestiges of this Roman history.

activities.text: Walk through Cologne's old town—the Altstadt—between the cathedral and the 12 great Romanesque churches that punctuate the historic center. Cologne has the highest concentration of Romanesque churches in Germany: Groß Sankt Martin, Sankt Gereon with its 4th-century rotunda, Sankt Maria im Kapitol built on a Roman temple, and many others form a unique architectural journey.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Pure white against deep blue: the Königsstuhl is Rügen's tallest and best-known chalk cliff, at 118 m above the Baltic. The chalk dates from about 70 million years ago. The Skywalk, a suspended walkway above the void, opened in 2023 and offers a direct view of the wall face. Jasmund National Park (3,000 ha) is Germany's smallest national park.

The Königsstuhl cliff plunges 118 m into the Baltic and erosion makes it one of the coast's most unstable formations. Entire sections regularly fall into the sea—a natural process since the last glaciation. The Stubnitz beech forests bordering the cliffs have been designated UNESCO World Natural Heritage since 2011.

activities.text: Descend to the pebble beach from Sassnitz (8 km from the National Park Center) via the coastal trail—allow 2 hours round-trip. From below, the view of the chalk cliffs is more impressive than from the summit. Early morning, the site is calm and the raking light brings out the chalk's whiteness.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen

visits.text: Königstein Fortress occupies 9.5 hectares on a sandstone plateau that dominates the Elbe by 240 meters—it's one of Europe's largest mountain fortresses. Never taken by assault, it was nicknamed the "Bastille of Saxony": a state prison from 1591 to 1922, it notably housed Johann Friedrich Böttger, inventor of European porcelain, who was imprisoned there to try to recreate his secret formula. During World War II, it served as an oflag for Allied officers—General Henri Giraud escaped on April 17, 1942 by descending a rope made of knotted sheets, an action that put Hitler in a black rage. The well dug between 1563 and 1569 to supply the garrison measures 152.5 meters deep—the deepest in Saxony.

activities.text: Walk around the fortress ramparts (1.7 km) with panoramic views of the Elbe valley, Saxon Switzerland National Park, and Bohemia. A panoramic elevator provides access to the plateau from below.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

places.name: Konigswartha

visits.text: At the heart of Germany's largest pond network, the Haus der Tausend Teiche serves as gateway to the Upper Lusatian Heath and Pond Landscape UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. The multimedia exhibition recounts carp farming, practiced here since the 13th century, and the wildlife of the reed beds. A large aquarium closes the visit. The center is located in Wartha; closed Mondays, open mainly during the day.

activities.text: Ride a bike along the Seeadlerweg, the 'white-tailed eagle path,' which winds between the ponds from the reserve's center at Wartha. The dikes offer observation points over waterbirds; in the quiet, you may encounter herons and raptors. In August, the heath turns purple—it's the most beautiful time. Bring binoculars and mosquito protection near the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

map_keywords: House of a Thousand Ponds Wartha

visits.text: Explore Konstanz on the shores of the Bodensee (Lake Constance), Germany's largest lake straddling the border with Switzerland and Austria. The lovely lakeside promenade is lined with restaurants and cafes offering views across the shimmering waters to the Swiss Alps. The famous rotating Imperia statue at the harbour entrance completes one revolution every three minutes, a modern landmark with medieval satirical roots.

Visit the fascinating pile-dwelling open-air museum, a UNESCO World Heritage site that reconstructs Stone Age and Bronze Age settlements built on wooden stilts over the water. These remarkable reconstructions offer a glimpse into human life on Lake Constance over 4,000 years ago. Archaeological finds from the lake bed bring these ancient communities vividly to life through engaging exhibits.

Cross to Mainau Island, the famous 'Flower Island' where subtropical botanical gardens, a baroque palace and butterfly house create a Mediterranean paradise in the heart of Europe. The gardens burst with colour year-round, from spring tulips to summer roses and autumn dahlias. Nearby Meersburg Castle, Germany's oldest inhabited castle, perches dramatically above the lake with parts still occupied by the owning family.

activities.text: Take a boat cruise across the Bodensee to appreciate the stunning Alpine panorama from the water

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas
Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: A basalt arc so slender it seems unreal: the Rakotzbrücke, known as the 'Devil's Bridge,' reflects in Lake Rakotz to form a perfect circle. Built between the 1860s and 1880s in basalt stone, it cannot be crossed—you admire it from the shore. The reflection depends on wind and light, so aim for a calm morning. On fine days, you won't be alone in front of this backdrop.

activities.text: Reducing Kromlau to its Instagram shot would be a shame: the Rhododendron Park surrounding the bridge deserves a proper stroll. Across roughly 200 hectares, you'll encounter curious stone structures, like the 'Basaltkirche' jutting out of the water and the 'Heaven and Hell' grotto. The rhododendron bloom runs from May to July—that's the prime time. Outside peak season, check the opening hours for the village cafés; many close.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Make a stop in Wismar en route—the Hanseatic old town has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2002. The Marktplatz, one of northern Germany's largest, is surrounded by red brick Gothic facades. The town belonged to Sweden from 1648 to 1803, which gave it a rare hybrid German-Scandinavian architecture in the region.

Kühlungsborn is Mecklenburg's largest spa resort, founded in the late 19th century when three independent coastal villages merged in 1938. The promenade follows the sea for several kilometers, lined with bathing villas with wooden facades typical of Bäderarchitektur style. Descend to the beach through one of the access points between the streets—fine sand stretches as far as the eye can see.
The pier at Kühlungsborn East measures 240 meters and extends into the Baltic Sea—it's the best spot to watch ferries offshore and observe sunsets over the water.

activities.text: Rent a bike and head into the Kühlung Forest, 133 hectares of beech and pine that extend directly behind the resort. You'll find Bronze Age burial mounds, enormous glacial erratics deposited by ice sheets, and paths that emerge onto coastal panoramas. The Strandkorb—the woven wicker beach chair typical of Baltic beaches—awaits you on the sand for your afternoon break.

Bastorf Lighthouse stands on Cape Bukspitze, a few kilometers west of Kühlungsborn. At 20.8 meters tall, it's one of Germany's smallest lighthouses but one of the best positioned—it towers on a cliff at a remarkable height above the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast

visits.text: The Lech cascades down five stone steps across 7.7 meters at the Lechfall, a ten-minute walk from the old town. It's no accident: Füssen locals built these tiers in 1787 to harness hydraulic power. In spring, snowmelt makes the spectacle impressive.

activities.text: From the Maxsteg or the 2014 panoramic platform, watch the Lech's power as it rushes into the gorge. Best moment: early morning, when light hits the turquoise water. Free access.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: The weathered planks creak, the reed roofs smell of damp hay: Lehde's Freilandmuseum recreates four nineteenth-century farmhouses from different parts of the Spreewald. One of Brandenburg's oldest open-air museums. One family room where seven people slept in a single box bed—the panels present this as a fact hard to imagine today. Open April through October, 10 a.m.–6 p.m.

activities.text: You can only reach Lehde's Freilandmuseum on foot or by canoe from Lübbenau—no direct road. The path follows the Lehmgraben for 2 km from the Lübbenau-Ortsteil Lehde parking lot. Along the way, count the stufenholzbrücken, the stepped wooden bridges characteristic of the Spreewald.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Less famous than Lehde, Leipe is the real island village. Perched on an 800-by-400-meter sandbank surrounded by the Spree and the Leiper Graben, it's home to a handful of residents and wasn't connected to the road network until 1969. Before that, the doctor, the postman, children going to school—everything was done by boat. The Heimatstube displays Sorbian costumes and utensils from another era.

activities.text: If you have a canoe or kayak, the fließe are calmest from Leipe. Several Lübbenau rental operators offer return shuttles; count on about 3 hours of paddling one-way from the Großer Hafen for a peaceful arrival in the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: The Thomaskirche is Leipzig's musical heart. Johann Sebastian Bach was cantor here from 1723 until his death in 1750—he composed the St. Matthew Passion, the Christmas Oratorio, and more than 150 cantatas here. His tomb stands before the altar. The Thomanerchor, founded in 1212, is one of the world's oldest choral ensembles and still performs motets every Friday and Saturday. Martin Luther himself preached from this pulpit.

The Gewandhaus Orchestra, founded in 1743 by Leipzig's merchant bourgeoisie—a clean break from the tradition of princely or ecclesiastical orchestras—is one of the world's oldest symphony ensembles. Felix Mendelssohn was its director and it was under his direction that Bach's St. Matthew Passion was rediscovered in 1829, after a century of oblivion. Richard Wagner is a native of Leipzig, Robert Schumann lived and composed here. More than 500 composers have reportedly resided in the city.

The Nikolaikirche, a Gothic church remodeled in the 16th century with a green and white palm-themed neoclassical interior, is the church of the Peaceful Revolution of 1989. It was here that the Monday demonstrations (Montagsdemonstrationen) began, with prayers for peace organized every week since 1982. On October 9, 1989, 70,000 people marched through Leipzig's streets—it was the turning point that precipitated the fall of the Berlin Wall.

The Völkerschlachtdenkmal, inaugurated in 1913 for the centenary of the Battle of Nations, is Germany's largest monument: 91 meters high, an interior staircase of 500 steps, colossal sandstone statues of warriors in the Wilhelmine monumental tradition. The October 1813 battle, which pitted Napoleon against the Prussian, Austrian, Russian, and Swedish coalitions, left 90,000 dead in four days at Leipzig's gates—one of the Napoleonic era's deadliest.

The Spinnerei, in the Plagwitz district west of the city, is a former cotton mill built in 1884 that employed up to 3,500 workers. Since the 1990s, its 10 hectares of industrial buildings have been converted into artist studios, galleries, cafes, and cultural spaces—more than 100 artists work there. The "Neue Leipziger Schule," a figurative painting movement influential in 2000s global contemporary art, was born here.

The Mädlerpassage, one of Leipzig's historic covered passages, houses Auerbachs Keller in its cellars—a tavern whose origins date back to 1525. Goethe frequented it during his Leipzig student years and made it the setting for a scene in Faust: Mephistopheles makes wine gush from a wooden table. Two groups of statues at the entrance depict the scene.

activities.text: BMW factory tour
Concert or motet at the Thomaskirche. Friday evening and Saturday afternoon motets with the Thomanerchor are free and open to all—it's one of Germany's most authentic musical experiences.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland
Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: The Burgruine Leuchtenberg, the Palatinate's largest fortified ruin, sits atop a granite cone at 585 m. Mentioned as early as 1124, it belonged to the Landgraves whose title ended up borne by an emperor of Brazil and a king of Sweden. The chapel and Rittersaal are still legible. Entry: €3.50.

activities.text: Climb the Bergfried for a 360° panorama over Upper Palatinate and the Fichtelgebirge. In summer, the Burgfestspiele turn the courtyard into an open-air theater. Shows from May to August.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Dubbed the 'Fairy-tale Castle of Württemberg,' Lichtenstein Castle perches at 817 m above Honau village. Count Wilhelm von Württemberg commissioned it after reading Wilhelm Hauff's novel in 1826. The neo-Gothic castle, completed in 1842, remains the private property of the ducal family of Urach.

The 30-minute guided tour winds through period-furnished salons and concludes on a balcony with vertiginous views over the Echaz valley. The 2009 film Dornröschen was shot here. Closed January–February.

activities.text: Combine your visit with the nearby Nebelhöhle: 141 steps descend into this stalactite cave of the Swabian Alb. Open April to November. The Maultaschenwirt at the cave entrance serves homemade Maultaschen.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: At 80 meters up, the wind cuts through the structure and the rivets rumble: Lichterfeld's Abraumförderbrücke F60 is as much a physical experience as a historical one. Five hundred and two meters long and weighing 11,000 tons, the F60 operated for only 13 months in 1991–92 before being shut down for economic reasons. Nicknamed 'the reclining Eiffel Tower of Lusatia.' Ninety-minute guided tours starting at 11:30 a.m.

activities.text: The Bergheider See adjoins the F60: a body of water born from flooding the same tagebau, with a fine sandy beach accessible on foot from the parking lot. Twenty minutes' walk under the structure to finish the itinerary with your feet in the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

places.name: Limburg An Der Lahn

visits.text: Seven towers, a red-and-white façade, a limestone outcrop above the Lahn: Limburg Cathedral (1235) strikes first by its silhouette. The interior mixes massive Rhenish Romanesque with early luminous Gothic. No other church in Germany has seven towers.

Limburg locals claim that Römer 2-4-6 is Germany's oldest freestanding half-timbered building, dated 1289. True or not, the Fischmarkt lane around it concentrates 13th-century façades in a tiny space. The contrast with the cathedral looming overhead is striking.

Cross the old stone Lahnbrücke (replaced in 1315, rebuilt since). On the other side, the view of the cathedral rising above the slate rooftops is the city's postcard shot. The Farbmühle, an old dye mill at the foot of the rock, makes a good photo landmark.

activities.text: If you stay for dinner, restaurants on Salzgasse and Fischmarkt serve Hessian cuisine in half-timbered settings. The cathedral is illuminated after sunset. Limburg is well served by train (ICE at Limburg-Süd), convenient if you're returning the car here.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

map_keywords: Limburg an der Lahn Old Town

visits.text: Begin your journey on Lindau Island, perched on Lake Constance (Bodensee). The old town occupies a 70-hectare island that's been linked to the mainland by a railway bridge since 1853. Two guardians mark the harbor entrance: the Mangturm, a 37-meter medieval lighthouse from the 13th century that guided boats across the lake since 1230, and the newer white lighthouse built between 1853 and 1856 - Bavaria's only lighthouse. Standing watch beside them is the 6-meter Bavarian lion carved in sandstone by sculptor Johann von Halbig and unveiled in 1856.

Stroll down Maximilianstrasse, the old town's main artery lined with burgher houses spanning Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque styles - each distinct yet harmoniously aligned. On Reichsplatz stands the 15th-century Altes Rathaus with its painted fresco facade. Lindau was a Free Imperial City from 1275 to 1805, and this town hall hosted several Imperial Diets of the Holy Roman Empire.

Take the Deutsche Alpenstraße east - Germany's oldest scenic road, inaugurated in 1933, linking Lindau to Berchtesgaden across 450 km along the Alps. Your first dramatic stop is Bad Hindelang and the Oberjochpass: 106 hairpin bends climbing 300 vertical meters over 5 km, including 9 dramatic switchbacks that spiral above the village. Built in 1895, the Jochstraße was already an engineering marvel - back in 1662, 15,850 barrels of salt crossed this pass annually on the backs of 300 horses.

Halfway up the Oberjochpass, pull over at the Kanzel viewpoint for sweeping panoramas over the Ostrach valley, Bad Hindelang and Bad Oberdorf below, with the Nagelfluhkette ridge and the 2,224-meter Nebelhorn rising behind. It's one of the finest drive-to viewpoints in the Bavarian Alps.

activities.text: Take a ferry from Lindau harbor across the Bodensee toward Bregenz (Austria) or Friedrichshafen. From the water, the island town reveals its true character - colorful facades, twin lighthouses, and the full sweep of the harbor entrance framed by Alpine peaks in the distance.

Walk freely at the Aussichtspunkt Kanzel on the Oberjochpass, where a short trail from the parking area reaches the viewpoint in 2 minutes. On clear days, the panorama extends to the Austrian mountain chains.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
German Alpine Road: Lake Constance to Königssee
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: Schloss Linderhof is the only castle Ludwig II saw completed. Small in size, extravagant in décor: the bedroom drowns in gold, and the Venus Grotto recreates a Tannhäuser scene with an artificial lake and colored lighting. Online booking recommended.

Linderhof's Italian-style park unfolds its terraces, fountains, and Moorish pavilion in a valley of the Ammergauer Alpen. The grand fountain spouts every hour in summer. Allow an hour to stroll the gardens.

activities.text: Linderhof's Italian-style park unfolds its terraces, fountains, and Moorish pavilion in a valley of the Ammergauer Alpen. The grand fountain spouts every hour in summer. Allow an hour to stroll.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: At the Ellenbogen — Sylt's 'forearm', the island's northern tip — the wind cuts in from both sides at once: North Sea to the west, Wadden Sea to the east, Denmark visible 12 km away. It is private land that the owner opens for a €8 car toll; walkers and cyclists pass freely on the dune paths.

Lister Hafen, at the island's northern end, runs ferries to Rømø (Denmark) and to the halligen islands. Fishing boats sell Sylt oysters directly at the dock — Sylt is one of the very few places in Germany where oyster farming still survives.

activities.text: Take a ferry to Rømø from List — 45 minutes to the Danish island. Rømø has the widest beaches in Scandinavia at low tide: up to 2 km of dry sand. The wind drives the land yachts.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Lorsch Abbey was founded in 764 during the reign of Pepin the Short, Charlemagne's father. It became one of the most important cultural centers of the early Middle Ages, housing one of Europe's largest libraries and a renowned scriptorium. Upon the death of Louis II the German in 876, the abbey became the necropolis of the Carolingian kings of East Francia. Of this once immense complex, only the Königshalle (Torhalle) remains standing: a monumental 17-meter portico built around the mid-9th century with a polychrome facade alternating light and dark bands over arcades inspired by antiquity. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991, it's one of the rare Carolingian buildings to have retained its original appearance. Its exact function remains debated—library, reception hall, or ceremonial gateway? The Lorsch Pharmacopoeia, written around 800 in the abbey, has been inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register since 2013 as the first herbal medicine treatise.

activities.text: Walk through the Lorsch Abbey site: the footprints of disappeared buildings (basilica, cloister, monastic buildings) have been materialized in the lawns during the 2017 renovation by Topotek1. The medicinal herb garden behind the tithe barn is planted according to species mentioned in the Lorsch Pharmacopoeia.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks
From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Touristy, yes, but Lübben's Schlossinsel delivers on its promise. This six-hectare island in the town center brings together the castle museum (with its wappensaal), a water playground, and the SpreeLagune footbridge that stretches 107 meters above the water. Skip the Christmas market weekend—the queues spill onto the bridges.

The scent of old timber still lingers in the Paul-Gerhardt-Kirche, a late-Gothic three-nave church named for the local Lutheran poet. The tower platform sits at 22 meters—Vera Städter, Brandenburg and Berlin's only Türmerin since 2011, leads 115 steps of history three times a week. Schedule details at luebben.de.

activities.text: You'll find a kahnfährmann first thing in the morning at the Schlossinsel's small harbor. Spree barges carry about twenty people and leave on demand. Budget 90 minutes for a basic loop; the fährmann poles standing, the pole is called a Rudel.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Kitsch on the outside, but Lübbenau's Spreewald-Museum in the Torbogenhaus on Topfmarkt is worth the hour it asks. The museum recreates a regional town circa 1900 with a colonial grocery, furrier's workshop, and steam locomotive 99 57 03 from the Spreewaldbahn. Founded in 1899 by Paul Fahlisch, who launched tourism in the Spreewald.

Lübbenauers simply call it 'der Große Hafen' and gather there every morning as if at the café. Lübbenau's Großer Spreewaldhafen is where Theodor Fontane embarked more than 150 years ago for his walks in the Hochwald. Today the Gurkenmeile lines up pickle producers along the quayside.

activities.text: If you have a choice, take the Wotschofska-Tour: 5.5 hours round-trip via motorless fließe to the forest inn on Alder Island. Departure 10:30 a.m., €28 per adult. The 2-hour version at €17 works if the afternoon is packed.

You'll taste pickles from the Gurkenmeile: more than a dozen local producers offer different recipes with garlic, herbs, or mustard. The market runs every morning May through October. A fresh crunchy pickle is something else entirely from what you find in a jar.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Built between 1464 and 1478 under the direction of Hinrich Helmstede, the Holstentor leans slightly on its marshy foundation—and it's precisely this flaw that makes it human. Its two round red-brick towers appear on the German two-euro coin. It's one of only two of Lübeck's four medieval gates still standing. The interior museum traces Hanseatic commercial power: allow one hour, arrive early.

Lübeck's seven spires hover permanently over the city, but it's the Marienkirche that induces vertigo. Its two towers reach 125 m—it was for a long time the city's tallest structure. Its melted bells, which fell during the 1942 bombing, remain in place on the floor as testimony. A lesser-known detail: Dietrich Buxtehude served as organist here from 1668 to 1707, and the young Bach traveled from Arnstadt to hear him play.

Lübeckers saved their Altstadt from a wave of demolitions in the 1970s thanks to citizen mobilization—the city was designated UNESCO in 1987, the first in the world in Northern Europe. The ensemble comprises 81 hectares with medieval streets, gabled houses, and the six Salzspeicher (salt barns, built 1579-1745) facing the Holstentor. The marzipan manufactured here since the 15th century is sold by the piece in Niederegger boutiques.

The European Hansemuseum, opened in 2015 in the former convent buildings of the Burgkloster, traces a thousand years of Hanseatic trade across three floors and 5,000 m². It's the largest museum dedicated to the Hansa in Europe. The scenography avoids archive mode—reconstructions of merchant offices in Bruges, Novgorod, and Bergen give flesh to what this network was.

activities.text: Stroll through Fischergrube, the old sailors' street descending toward the Trave. Halfway down, the Buddenbrookhaus recreates the universe of Thomas Mann's novel published in 1901—his family's original house truly stood here. Entry isn't free, but the museum is brief and well done.

The scent of marzipan invades Breite Straße from morning on. The Niederegger confectionery, installed opposite the Rathaus since 1806, sells its almond paste in two hundred forms—in slices, figurines, cakes. The upstairs café offers a view of the pedestrian street without having to order anything expensive.

Crossing the Trave by rowboat from the Holstenbrücke quay takes 20 min and provides a view of the Salzspeicher and Holstentor from the river—an angle street photographers don't capture. Boatmen also offer longer circuits to the outer harbor. Season April-October, departures as demand arises.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen

visits.text: Dubbed the 'Versailles of the North,' Ludwigslust Palace surprises with its French airs dropped into the Mecklenburg plains. Ducal residence from 1765 to 1837, it hides a Goldener Saal whose decorations in papier-mâché mimic stucco. The 127-hectare English-style park is free to enter, with complimentary parking.

activities.text: Walk up Schlossstrasse from the palace: this straight baroque avenue, lined with courtiers' houses, reveals a town designed entirely as the palace's setting. Stop for a craft beer at the Orangerie.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Built in 1271 on a sandbar in the meanders of the Stever river, Burg Vischering is one of the oldest and best-preserved castles in the Münsterland. A 16th-century fire led to a Renaissance rebuild that has remained largely untouched ever since. The museum offers a combined Vorburg + Hauptburg ticket for €7.50.

Defensive and austere on the outside — and yet the park between Lüdinghausen's two castles, open since 2018, reveals a bucolic path shaded by eight-hundred-year-old trees, a stork's nest to the north, and tiny bridges crossing the Stever. Ten minutes on foot separates Burg Vischering from Burg Lüdinghausen.

activities.text: Follow the moat path to Burg Lüdinghausen along the Kapitelweg, lined with busts of churchmen — an atmosphere somewhere between the grotesque and the serene. Allow ten minutes on foot. Burg Lüdinghausen hosts cultural events but its interior is not open to visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: You expect a hedge maze, you get a titanic chaos of granite. The Felsenlabyrinth Luisenburg, classified as a Nationales Naturmonument in January 2026, arrays multi-meter boulders through which you thread on walkways and metal stairs. Goethe visited in 1820 and was astounded. Entry: €4.50 adult, €9 family card.

activities.text: Allow a good two hours for the marked circuit (blue arrows up, red arrows down). Wear real hiking shoes, even in summer: the granite turns slippery the moment it rains. At the summit, the Bundesstein viewpoint rewards the effort.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Lüneburg grew rich on salt for a thousand years, then nearly sank into the ground: the city is built on a pillar of salt that is slowly dissolving beneath its foundations. The result is Gothic façades tilted like exhausted dominoes — and the charm comes precisely from that instability. The salt works closed in 1980, but the houses are still standing.

Built from 1405, Lüneburg's Altes Rathaus is an accumulation of styles spanning six centuries — Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque — stacked together without contradiction. The councillors' chamber with its 16th-century glazed tiles is accessible on a guided tour. Check with the tourist office for opening times.

Head down to the Stintmarkt, the old harbour on the Ilmenau, where the medieval wooden crane from the 14th century still stands on the quay. It was used to load barges with salt bound for Lübeck. Today the terraces have taken over from the cargo — views across half-timbered warehouses included.

activities.text: The Deutsches Salzmuseum is worth two unhurried hours: housed in the former salt works that closed in 1980, it traces the salt industry from antiquity to the 19th century. Giant extraction vats, horse-powered wheels, the long rivalry with Lübeck — all meticulously reconstructed.

The weathered brickwork and tilting houses of the old town are best seen in the evening: the gabled façades of Am Sande and Grapengießerstraße are lit up and the day-trippers are gone. End with a glass of Lüneburger Pils at one of the Kneipen on the Stintmarkt.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the history of the Protestant Reformation at Lutherstadt Wittenberg, cradle of a movement that transformed Europe. It was here, on October 31, 1517, that Martin Luther allegedly nailed his 95 theses to the door of the Castle Church, triggering a spiritual and intellectual revolution. This monumental portal, now in bronze, attracts visitors from around the world who come to pay tribute at this symbolic site.

Visit St. Mary's Church where Martin Luther preached regularly, a Gothic edifice at the heart of the old town listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Castle Church houses the tombs of Luther and his collaborator Philipp Melanchthon. The market square, dominated by the Luther monument and Renaissance facades, forms one of the finest urban ensembles in central Germany.

Explore the many historic buildings of the old town bearing witness to this pivotal era in European history. Luther House, the former Augustinian monastery where the reformer lived, is today the world's largest museum of the Reformation. Crossing the Elbe bridge, you reach the floodplain meadows where pristine nature offers a peaceful contrast to this intense cultural heritage.

activities.text: Walk through the Elbe meadows to enjoy the preserved natural surroundings

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Discover the Green Citadel, an architectural masterpiece designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser. This unique building with its organic shapes, vibrant colors, and green roofs represents a revolutionary vision of urban architecture. Its colorful columns and irregular windows create a fascinating visual experience that defies traditional architectural conventions.

Admire the majestic Magdeburg Cathedral, the first Gothic edifice erected on German soil. This imposing cathedral, whose construction began in 1209, houses the tomb of Emperor Otto I the Great. Its soaring vaults and medieval sculptures bear witness to eight centuries of spiritual and artistic history at the heart of Germany.

Explore the Elbauenpark and its Millennium Tower, an interactive attraction tracing 6,000 years of scientific and technological history. This 60-meter tower offers hands-on experiences across five levels to understand humanity's great discoveries. The park also houses the famous Sandman figure, an iconic character from East German television.

activities.text: Stroll through the Old Market and discover Otto von Guericke's Magdeburg hemispheres
Visit the Johanniskirche, a Gothic church rebuilt after World War II

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Malchow is an 'island town' set between two lakes, linked by a swing bridge (Drehbrücke) that opens every hour to let boats through. The monastery converted into a DDR-Alltag museum—GDR everyday life—is a quick but revealing stop.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Start with St. Elizabeth's Church at the foot of the hill. Built between 1235 and 1283 over the tomb of Saint Elizabeth of Thuringia, it's Germany's oldest purely Gothic church. The stained glass tells her life story. It served as the model for Cologne Cathedral.

The Oberstadt's staircases climb between half-timbered facades pressed together like books on a shelf. Marburg counts over 700 historic houses, most inhabited. Student cafés spill onto the steps whenever sun breaks through.

The Landgrafenschloss overlooks the Lahn valley from its promontory. The building houses the Universitätsmuseum with a 13th-century Gothic knights' hall, one of Germany's largest secular Gothic halls. Park access is free, museum is paid.

activities.text: If you're there on a Saturday morning, the market on Marktplatz animates the square in front of the town hall. Local cheeses, sausages, Apfelwein. The atmosphere mixes cycling students and retirees with shopping bags.

Marburg students call the climb to the castle 'daily exercise.' The Philipps-Universität, founded in 1527, is the world's oldest Protestant university. Its campus is scattered through town, historic buildings alongside bookstores and Kneipen.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: The first stones of Burg Meersburg reportedly date back to the 7th century, under Merovingian King Dagobert I. It's Germany's oldest still-inhabited castle: not a ruin, not a frozen museum, a living fortress. More than 30 rooms to explore, from dungeon to knights' hall.

Poet Annette von Droste-Hülshoff died here on May 24, 1848. Her apartments and death chamber are part of the circuit. Guides offer special Droste tours by reservation, with access to normally closed rooms.

Climb to the Neues Schloss for the view from the baroque terrace: the lake, the vineyards, the Alps in the background. The palace, built in the 18th century for the prince-bishops of Konstanz, houses the Dornier Museum and temporary exhibitions.

activities.text: The terraced vineyards descend to the lake between the two castles. The Winzerverein Meersburg produces dry whites typical of the Bodensee. Buy directly from the cellar, modest prices for the quality.
If you're visiting on a Thursday evening, the Burg hosts a tasting of four Winzerverein wines within its medieval walls. Lake view included. Book at burg-meersburg.de.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: The Albrechtsburg dominates Meissen from a rocky promontory above the Elbe. This late Gothic castle, begun in 1471 by architect Arnold of Westphalia, is considered one of Germany's first residential palaces—designed as a princely residence rather than a fortress. In 1710, Augustus the Strong installed Europe's very first porcelain manufactory here, under the direction of alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger, who had promised to make gold and discovered porcelain instead. The manufacturing secret was so well guarded that workers were not allowed to leave the castle. Production remained here until 1863, when it was transferred to new buildings below. The interior walls are covered with 19th-century frescoes illustrating Saxony's history.

The Cathedral of Saints John and Donatus, adjoining the Albrechtsburg on the same promontory, was built from 1250 on the ruins of a 10th-century Romanesque church. Its construction lasted until the end of the 15th century. It houses 14th-century Gothic sculptures depicting Emperor Otto I and his wife Adelaide of Burgundy, as well as paintings by Lucas Cranach the Elder. The Silbermann organ, installed in 1983, is one of Saxony's largest instruments.

The Meissen Porcelain Manufactory, in its current buildings at the foot of the hill, houses the most comprehensive museum on the history of European porcelain. The demonstration workshops allow you to see the different stages of production live—modeling, turning, glazing, hand painting—carried out according to methods unchanged since the 18th century. The crossed blue swords trademark, created in 1722, is one of the oldest commercial brands still in use in the world.

activities.text: Porcelain painting workshop at the Meissen Manufactory. The workshops offer discovery sessions where visitors paint a design on a porcelain piece themselves, supervised by a manufactory artist.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: The scent of fresh bread drifts across Memmingen's Marktplatz, framed by patrician houses with carved gables. A Free Imperial City since 1268, the town built its fortune on salt and spice trading. The market runs Tuesday and Saturday mornings—perfect for sampling Allgäu cheese.

Seven gates, twenty-six towers: Memmingen's Stadtmauer is the town's most extensive fortified ensemble. The 13th-century ramparts still stand at their original height in the Ulmer Vorstadt district. Walk the Stadtbach at dusk, when lights shimmer on the water.

Small but mighty, the Stadtpfarrkirche St. Martin rises above the rooftops with its refined Gothic proportions, completed in 1499. The choir stalls are worth a look. Free entry, closed during services.

Take the lane along the Stadtbach toward the weavers' quarter. The Frauenkirche, one of Swabia's oldest churches, hides a surprising medieval fresco: a unicorn hunt. Easy to miss—ask the tourist office for a floor plan.

activities.text: If it's Tuesday or Saturday, the Wochenmarkt on Memmingen's Marktplatz lines up stalls of cheese, honey, and local schnapps. The market is compact—you can circle it in thirty minutes. Try the Allgäuer Bergkäse, the local mountain cheese.
Head to the tanners' quarter along the Memminger Ach. Half-timbered houses mirror themselves in the stream. This is the town's most photogenic corner, especially in late afternoon light.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip
