visits.text: Built between 1824 and 1828, Meppen's Koppelschleuse is a double-chamber work of engineering, designed to overcome more than four meters of elevation between the Ems-Hase Canal and the river. It can be visited in its original state, open-air. Free access.

The Renaissance gables of the old Rathaus, built in 1408 from glacial erratics, deserve a look. The baroque red-and-yellow Gymnasialkirche, a stone's throw away, contrasts with the northern Protestant sobriety.

activities.text: Climb up to the Höltingmühle on the island between the Dortmund-Ems Canal and the Hase. The windmill is photogenic, and the Mühle café serves good homemade cakes.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Schloss Mespelbrunn owes its survival to discretion: hidden in a side valley of the Spessart, it was never stormed. Every war spared it. The Renaissance moated castle - begun in 1427, completed in its current form in 1569 - remains inhabited by descendants of the original family. The guided tour lasts about 40 minutes, admission €5.50.

In good weather, the castle's reflection in the pond is the true postcard of the Spessart. In 1958, the film Das Wirtshaus im Spessart starring Liselotte Pulver was shot here - you can still ask the Schlosshof café owner which scene was filmed in which room.

activities.text: Explore the Spessart trails from the castle parking lot. The Gruftkapelle of the Counts of Ingelheim (1875) stands a few hundred meters uphill toward the Echterspfahl. The forest is dense and silent - the Spessart is one of Germany's most extensive forest massifs.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: The small Schloss Mirow, on its island in the lake, is the birthplace of Sophie Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, future Queen of England (wife of George III). The exhibition traces her journey. The palace park overlooks the lake—absolute calm.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Six hundred and fifty meters long, 40 meters high, built entirely of rough stone blocks: the Möhnetalsperre is Westphalia's heaviest listed monument. Inaugurated in 1913, it was then Europe's largest reservoir. On the night of May 16, 1943, a British bouncing bomb blasted a 77-meter breach. The floodwaters claimed over 1,600 lives downstream.

activities.text: Book a guided tour inside the dam through the Ruhrverband (€2.50 per person, 1h30 duration). You'll see a replica of the 1943 bomb. Outside tours, the promenade along the dam crest is free and offers a plunging view over the Ausgleichsweiher.

When the dam overflows, water cascades down 105 arches. The spectacle is rare but impressive. Otherwise, the boat cruise on the Möhnesee (April to October, departing from the Staumauer) lasts about an hour and shows the lake's arms.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Monschau, nicknamed the "Pearl of the Eifel," is nestled at 517 meters altitude in the steep valley of the Rur. First mentioned in 1198, the town today preserves more than 330 listed buildings, including an ensemble of 17th- and 18th-century half-timbered fishermen's and craftsmen's houses stretching along both riverbanks. Its 13th-century medieval castle, built by the Counts of Limburg, overlooks the valley from a rocky spur. During the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944, Monschau marked the northernmost point of the German offensive front and was one of the few towns spared from destruction.

The Rotes Haus, the Red House, is an 18th-century patrician residence that tells the story of Monschau's golden age of cloth industry. In the 17th and 18th centuries, the town was one of Europe's great centers for fine cloth production—its woolen cloth was renowned throughout Europe. The house, converted into a museum, presents remarkably preserved period interiors with their Baroque and Rococo furnishings.

activities.text: Hike in the Rur valley around Monschau: several marked trails follow the river, cross meadows, and climb toward the forested hills of the Eifel. The Eifelsteig trail passes through Monschau and offers loops suitable for all levels. In spring, the valleys are covered with millions of wild daffodils, a spectacular natural phenomenon of the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Moritzburg Castle rises in the middle of a vast artificial pond, its silhouette with four round towers topped by copper roofs reflecting in the water—the image is striking, especially in misty weather. The first hunting lodge was built here between 1542 and 1546 by Duke Moritz of Saxony, whose name the castle bears. Augustus the Strong had it completely rebuilt in Baroque style from 1703, with the help of Pöppelmann, architect of the Zwinger. The interior of its more than 200 rooms is wallpapered with painted leather depicting hunting scenes—unique 18th-century pieces—and houses the world's largest collection of deer trophies. Napoleon stayed here in 1806, on the occasion of the coronation of his ally the King of Saxony. In winter, the castle hosts the Three Hazelnuts for Cinderella exhibition, named after the 1973 Czechoslovak fairy tale film that was shot here and remains cult throughout Central Europe.

The Fasanenschlösschen, the little pheasant palace, is located 2.5 km from the main castle at the end of a long perspective carved through the forest. This bright pink rococo pavilion, built between 1769 and 1782, was used as a secondary residence during hunting parties. Its miniature harbor with jetty and Saxony's only lighthouse—a pink lighthouse, too—served as scenery for the reconstructed naval battles that the princes organized on the ponds.

activities.text: Walk around the pond to see the castle from all angles. The promenade around the lake is about 3 km and takes 45 minutes. The most impressive view of the castle is from the opposite shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: One hundred and seven meters above the Wupper, the Müngstener Brücke is Germany's highest steel railway bridge. Completed in 1897, it measures 465 meters long. Every ten minutes, an S-Bahn rumbles overhead. The Brückensteig offers a guided climb onto the lower parabolic arch for the more daring.

activities.text: Head down to Brückenpark Müngsten for a riverside picnic. Minigolf, lawns, beer garden. A floating ferry (Schwebefähre) crosses the river without an engine, pulled by cable. Free, operates weekends April to October.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: In 1648, inside the Friedenssaal of the historic Rathaus, delegates from across Europe brought thirty years of war to a close — the room is smaller than you'd expect, and that's precisely what catches you off guard. The 37 portraits of sovereigns and envoys, purchased by the council as early as 1649, still gaze down from oak-panelled walls dating to 1577. Entry €3; closed Monday mornings.

The scent of fresh pretzels drifts up from the market at Domplatz, just behind the Prinzipalmarkt. This arcaded street — known to locals as the Gute Stube, the city's front parlour — runs 300 metres between the Rathaus to the south and the Lambertikirche to the north. The Giebelhäuser, destroyed in 1945, were rebuilt in Baumberge sandstone between 1947 and 1958. Come early in the morning, before the delivery vans arrive.

Three iron cages hang from the tower of the Lambertikirche — in these late-Gothic cages, the bodies of executed Anabaptist leaders were put on public display in 1536. The church itself is free to enter, and the organ alone is worth a stop. The contrast between the beauty of the space and the darkness of its history is quietly unsettling.

activities.text: Pick up a bike at the central station: Münster is one of Germany's most cycle-friendly cities. The loop through the Schlossgarten, past Schlaun's princely palace and the botanical garden, takes under two hours. Stay alert on shared paths — local cyclists don't slow down for anyone.

If you're passing through on a Wednesday or Saturday morning, the Domplatz market is unmissable: fruit, Westphalian cheeses, and cured meats right in front of the cathedral. Arrive before 10am for the best picks. Stalls are gone by 1pm.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: The Belchen remains little-known outside Germany despite steady visitor numbers—and that's its greatest asset. From the Ballon d'Alsace, 73 km to the west, the sun rises precisely over this summit at the equinoxes: according to Basel cantonal archaeologist Rolf d'Aujourd'hui, this system of five peaks named Belchen formed a druidic calendar roughly 2,300 years old. The name likely comes from Belenus, the Celtic sun god. At 1,414 m, the Belchenhaus serves hot soups on a south-facing terrace. Arrive early if you want the Vosges-Jura-Alps panorama to yourself.

The monks simply called it 'the devil's stone'—a giant sandstone boulder supposedly brought by Satan to trap Saint Ulrich's monastery. The anecdote is stronger than the site: just a rock in the woods, no dramatization. But it's on the return route from the Belchen, and it takes ten minutes. The detour feeds into the trip's theme without forcing it.

activities.text: Four and a half hours if you hike from Neuenweg—12 km, 700 m elevation gain. Otherwise, the cable car from Multen settles the matter in 8 minutes. Two wheels are recommended if the knees are reluctant, especially with the option to descend on foot. Bring water: the Belchenhaus doesn't always open early. In September, the pastures are still open and the cows are gone.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Neuschwanstein sits on a rocky spur at 970 meters altitude, dominating the valley by 200 meters. Ludwig II had it built from 1869 as a 'temple worthy of the divine friend'—meaning Richard Wagner, whose operas inspired all the interior frescoes: Tannhäuser in the singers' hall, Lohengrin in the bedrooms, Parsifal in the throne room. The castle was never completed. Ludwig II spent only 172 nights of his life there. In June 1886, doctors appointed by the government declared him insane and interned him; three days later, he was found dead in Lake Starnberg. The unfinished rooms were opened to visitors just seven weeks after his death—to repay his debts. Today, 1.4 million people visit the castle annually. Advance booking is absolutely essential.

The Marienbrücke bridge towers 92 meters above the bottom of the Pöllat gorge and spans a 45-meter waterfall. Originally built in wood then remade in iron in 1866, it bears the name of Marie of Prussia, Ludwig II's mother. From this bridge comes the world's most iconic view of the castle. In high season, lines form here—prefer early morning.

Arrive in Munich mid-afternoon. First discovery walk: Isartor → Viktualienmarkt → Marienplatz. The Viktualienmarkt is Munich's permanent open-air market, founded in 1807, selling Bavarian specialties, charcuterie, cheese, and beer at the central Biergarten. Marienplatz has been the city's heart since 1158: the Marian column (Mariensäule), erected in 1638 to celebrate the end of Swedish occupation, stands at the square's center.

The Munich Residence (Residenz) is Germany's largest urban palace—130 rooms, 10 interior courtyards, 40,000 m² of surface area. Seat of the Wittelsbach dynasty from 1508 to 1918, it offers a complete panorama of 16th to 19th century architecture and decorative arts. Its jewel is the Antiquarium, a Renaissance hall 69 meters long built between 1568 and 1571 by Duke Albrecht V to house his collection of ancient sculptures—it's the largest secular Renaissance hall north of the Alps. The barrel vault is pierced by 17 pairs of windows and covered with 102 lunette frescoes depicting Bavarian towns.

The Schatzkammer (Treasury), accessible with the same ticket, houses over 1,200 precious objects from Bavarian ruling dynasties: crowns, scepters, reliquaries, jewels, goldsmith's tableware—spanning two millennia of history. The Cuvilliés Theatre, designed between 1751 and 1755 by architect François de Cuvilliés, is one of Europe's finest rococo theaters. Mozart's opera Idomeneo premiered here in 1781.

Marienplatz and the Neues Rathaus: the Glockenspiel animates its 32 life-size figures at 11am, noon, and 5pm from March to October. The two scenes depicted—the tournament at William V's wedding in 1568 and the coopers' dance (Schäfflertanz) commemorating the end of a plague epidemic—have been performed since 1908.

The Frauenkirche (Cathedral of Our Lady), with its two 99-meter onion-domed towers, is Munich's symbol. Its Gothic construction dates from 1468 to 1488. Inside, look for the mysterious 'devil's footprint' (Teufelstritt) in the entrance floor—a footprint allegedly left by Satan himself, who came to inspect the building.

The Englischer Garten (English Garden), created in 1789, is one of the world's largest urban parks—larger than New York's Central Park with its 370 hectares. In the southern section, the Eisbachwelle is a permanent artificial wave where surfers practice in the heart of the city, an emblematic Munich image.

activities.text: Walk up from the Ticket Center to the castle (30-40 min) for the morning opening slot, before tour buses arrive. Visitor numbers exceed 6,000 people per day in summer—the earliest slots are significantly less crowded.

Walking tour through the Innenstadt: Odeonsplatz → Feldherrnhalle → Theatinerkirche (yellow baroque facade, built from 1663 as an ex-voto for the birth of an heir) → Hofgarten → return toward Marienplatz.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: It was here, in Murnau's Münter-Haus, that Kandinsky and Gabriele Münter lived between 1909 and 1914 and invented abstract art. The small house with blue shutters, nicknamed the "Russenhaus," has remained in its original state: painted furniture, Hinterglasbilder on the walls. Closed Mondays.

Climb to the Schloßmuseum Murnau, whose Blaue Reiter collections have gained international stature since the 2023 KK-Stiftung donation. Kandinsky, Münter, Marc, Jawlensky: the originals are here, in the village that inspired them.

From the shore of the Staffelsee, the light changes hour by hour. Franz Marc called this region the "Blaues Land," the Blue Land. You understand why at day's end, when the mountains turn gray and the lake deep green.

activities.text: If weather permits, rent a Tretboot (pedal boat) on the Staffelsee. The lake is calm, shallow, and reed-lined. An hour is enough to cross and return.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: The Murnauer Moos is Central Europe's largest undrained peat bog. A boardwalk trail crosses this landscape of mosses, dwarf birches, and reeds. Binoculars are useful: curlews, snipes, and harriers nest here.

activities.text: Allow an hour for the boardwalk loop through the Murnauer Moos. The trail is walkable even after rain. Free access, no ticket booth.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: The Zwingerweg runs along Nabburg's intact wall with its towers, gates and viewpoints over the Naab valley. Below, storks nest on the steeples. The complete circuit takes 30 minutes and is worth it for the atmosphere alone.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: The Königsstuhl, a 118-meter chalk cliff, is Rügen's symbol. The Nationalparkzentrum presents the ecosystem of ancient beech forests (UNESCO world heritage). The Königsweg, a suspended walkway, replaces the old cliff-edge access. Plan on the shuttle from Sassnitz or Hagen—cars prohibited.

activities.text: Hike the Hochuferweg, a ridge trail along the chalk cliffs between Sassnitz and Königsstuhl. The path winds through beech forest, with openings onto the Baltic. About 6 km, two hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Kellerwald-Edersee National Park, created in 2004, covers 5,724 hectares in northern Hesse—the state's only national park. Part of its beech forests has been integrated since 2011 into the UNESCO site "Primeval Beech Forests of the Carpathians and Other Regions of Europe." These beech groves are among the best-preserved in Central Europe: some trees exceed 200 years of age and 30 meters in height. The forest is left to natural evolution without human intervention, creating "wilderness of tomorrow." Wildcats (which reappeared in 2007 after 60 years of absence), black storks, and 15 species of bats inhabit the forest.

The park center in Vöhl-Herzhausen offers exhibitions on beech forest ecology and the Eder Dam, which closes off the park's eastern boundary. The dam, 400 meters long, was built in 1914 and holds back the Edersee—Germany's second-largest reservoir at 27 km long. During World War II, it was targeted in May 1943 by Operation Chastise, one of the first uses of the "bouncing bomb" by the RAF. The breach caused catastrophic flooding downstream.

The Edersee's shores offer 50 km of waterfront to explore. The turquoise waters of the lake—fed by Kellerwald streams—are among the clearest in inland Germany. At low water levels, remnants of the village of Asel, submerged when the reservoir was filled in 1914, sometimes reemerge from the former shoreline.

activities.text: Hike through the UNESCO-listed beech forests on the Urwaldsteig Edersee, a 68 km trail that loops around the lake in 4 to 5 stages. The sections on the northern side, in the heart of the national park, are the wildest. The trail is perfectly marked with "U-E" in white on a blue background.
Boat excursion or water activities on the Edersee: sailing, windsurfing, kayaking, and swimming at developed beaches. Pleasure boats tour the lake from the Waldeck pier.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Neubrandenburg possesses northern Germany's best-preserved medieval brick wall. Four Gothic gates (Stargarder, Treptower, Friedländer, Neues Tor) frame 25 Wiekhäuser—small half-timbered houses embedded in the wall. Now workshops, cafés, and galleries.
The Konzertkirche St. Marien is a radical contrast: Gothic brick exterior, interior transformed into a modern concert hall (glass, concrete, wood). The acoustics rival the spectacle.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Stop in Oranienburg, 35 kilometers north of Berlin, to visit the Sachsenhausen Memorial. Built in 1936 by prisoners from other camps, it was designed as the SS "model camp": its triangular architecture allowed a handful of guards to monitor the entire complex from a single central tower. Between 1936 and 1945, more than 200,000 people were interned here and approximately 84,000 died. This is where Himmler established his headquarters and where the first methods of mass extermination, including gas vans, were tested. After 1945, the Soviets reused the site until 1950 to imprison their own political opponents.

Neuruppin, nicknamed Fontanestadt, is the birthplace of two of Prussia's greatest cultural figures: architect Karl Friedrich Schinkel, born March 13, 1781, and writer Theodor Fontane, born December 30, 1819. Fontane's father's pharmacy still stands on the central square. A devastating fire razed nearly the entire town in 1787—Schinkel was six years old, and his father, a superintendent, died of exhaustion shortly after. The town was rebuilt between 1788 and 1796 in a rigorous neoclassical style that makes it one of the best-preserved classicist ensembles in northern Germany. Fontane himself thought the town resembled a suit too large for the wearer.

Walk to the Klosterkirche Sankt Trinitatis by the lakeside—it was the first Dominican monastery east of the Elbe, founded in 1246. Its two tall neo-Gothic towers, built between 1904 and 1907 from plans inspired by Schinkel, are visible from the entire lakeside promenade. Just behind it, the old high school where both Schinkel and Fontane were students bears the inscription on its pediment: CIVIBUS AEVI FUTURI—"To the citizens of the future."

The Tempelgarten was laid out for Crown Prince Frederick during his stay in Neuruppin between 1732 and 1736. Here, Georg Wenzeslaus von Knobelsdorff—who would later build Sanssouci in Potsdam—created his very first work: a round temple completed in 1735.

activities.text: Stroll along the Ruppiner See, Brandenburg's longest lake at 14 kilometers. Excursion boats sail in summer under the name Kronprinz Friedrich, and the view from the water of the Klosterkirche's twin towers is well worth the trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast

visits.text: The Karner of Perschen, a Romanesque rotunda from 1150, preserves original unrestored frescoes whose color intensity astonishes art historians. It adjoins the church of St. Peter und Paul and the Edelmannshof, a 17th-century farmhouse. Free admission.

activities.text: Push on to the Freilandmuseum Oberpfalz: 30 hectares of reconstructed historic farms with heritage breed animals. Five themed villages to explore on foot. Count on 2 hours minimum.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Neustrelitz lost its palace (destroyed in 1945) but kept its baroque star plan. Eight streets radiate from the central square. The Schlosspark, with its orangery and neoclassical temple, remains the lake district's finest park. Logical final stop before Berlin.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Stone giants deposited on the heath: the Lausitzer Findlingspark Nochten lines up more than 6,000 erratic boulders, torn from Scandinavia by glaciers and then unearthed here during coal mining. Opened in 2003 on a former opencast mine, it covers about twenty hectares divided into seven themed gardens. Allow one to two hours. Avoid windy and cold days—the site is wide open.

activities.text: Climb up to the Feldzeichen, the high point of the Findlingspark, for an unobstructed view over Lusatia. Below, children rush toward 'Die Wüste,' an adventure area with boulders to climb, a labyrinth, and water features. The paths are stabilized gravel, so strollers and wheelchairs can get almost everywhere. The bistro at the visitor center covers lunch.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: The Nördlingers call it the Daniel: the 90-meter-high bell tower of St.-Georgskirche, whose watchman still calls out every night between 10 p.m. and midnight - "So, G'sell so!" - a tradition uninterrupted since the Middle Ages. Climbing the 350 steps to see the town inscribed in its meteorite crater is the best way to grasp the site's cosmic origin.

Nördlingen's Stadtmauer makes a 2.7-km circuit: it's entirely walkable and intact, with five gates and eleven towers. One of the rare complete medieval enclosures in Germany still entirely closed and walkable. Nördlingen sits at the center of the Ries, a crater about 14.5 million years old with a diameter near 25 km.

activities.text: Climb onto the ramparts before 9 a.m. and walk the complete circuit. The five Stadttore carry collectible stamps - the Entdeckerpass distributed at the tourist office turns the walk into a game for the whole family. Entry to the towers is free and open.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: You might smile at the nickname 'the Westphalian Versailles' — and then you pull up in front of Schloss Nordkirchen and immediately understand it. This water-encircled Baroque castle, designed by Pictorius and completed by Schlaun, is the largest moated castle in Westphalia. Since 1959 it has housed a finance school of the state of North Rhine-Westphalia. Guided tours run on Sundays and public holidays.

Twenty-three radiating avenues fan out from Schloss Nordkirchen through a formal French-style park. The reconstructed Venus Island, the sculptures punctuating the parterres, the right-angled canals — a geometry that borders on the dizzying. The park is free to enter.

activities.text: Rent a bike at the park entrance: Nordkirchen has marked cycling paths through the Münsterland bocage. A full circuit of the estate takes about an hour. Avoid weekends when open-air concerts bring in the crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the history of Nuremberg, Bavaria's second city and an imperial city of capital importance in the Middle Ages. This powerful city where the Diets of the Holy Roman Empire were held and where the imperial crown jewels were kept retains an authentic medieval atmosphere behind its imposing walls. Its Christmas market, the Christkindlesmarkt, is Germany's most famous.

Visit the Kaiserburg, the imperial fortress dominating the city from its rocky promontory. This complex castle, enlarged over the centuries, was the residence of all the emperors of the Holy Roman Empire. The view from its ramparts encompasses the red roofs of the old town, the church towers and the meanders of the Pegnitz River.

Discover the old town rebuilt after the destruction of 1945, where half-timbered houses stand alongside the Gothic churches of St. Sebaldus and St. Lawrence. The Nuremberg Trials courthouse, Albrecht Dürer's house and the Documentation Center on Nazism bear witness to both the dark and bright pages of this city of art and culture.

activities.text: Visit the Germanic National Museum, the largest museum of German art and culture
Stroll through the medieval lanes of the artisans' quarter

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Visit Linderhof Palace, the most intimate of Ludwig II's palaces and the only one he saw completed in his lifetime. This rococo gem nestled in an Alpine valley enchants with its hilly park dotted with gushing fountains, sculpted groves and exotic pavilions. The Venus Grotto, artificially lit, recreates a scene from the opera Tannhäuser with a navigable underground lake.

Discover Ettal Abbey, a masterpiece of Bavarian Baroque founded in 1330 by Emperor Louis IV. Its monumental dome, one of Germany's largest, houses celestial frescoes of breathtaking magnificence. The Benedictine monks still perpetuate the tradition of monastic brewing and liqueur distillation to this day.

Wander through the lanes of Oberammergau, a village world-famous for its facades painted with biblical frescoes and fairytale scenes. This tradition of Lüftlmalerei, trompe-l'oeil painting on plaster, transforms every house into a work of art. The village also hosts the famous Passion Play every ten years since 1634.

activities.text: Discover the local woodcarving craft tradition

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: The 191-meter antenna stands out for miles: the Ochsenkopf, 1,024 m, second-highest summit of the Fichtelgebirge. The Asenturm at the top houses the region's highest restaurant. On clear days, views stretch all the way to Bayreuth. Arrive before 10 a.m. to beat the weekend family crowds.

Carved into the granite summit, an ox's muzzle gives the mountain its name. The Ochsenkopf-Wahrzeichen, a sculpture hewn from the rock, goes unnoticed if you're not looking for it. Spot it on the southern side, left of the tower.

activities.text: Take the Seilbahn cable car from Bischofsgrün to climb the 250-meter elevation gain without breaking a sweat. In summer, the Ochsenkopf Coaster—a kilometer-long toboggan track on rails—races downhill at 40 km/h. Family thrills guaranteed, individual braking.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Offenburg conducted two waves of witch trials: the first between 1586 and 1608, the second between 1627 and 1631. In total, 89 people were executed according to city council minutes—women and men, from all walks of life, including families of councilors. The first convictions of the second wave fell as early as December 1, 1627: three women burned alive. The Ritterhaus (municipal museum, Ritterstraße 10) has a documented section on these trials. Section primarily in German.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: You don't expect such a castle right in the center of Oldenburg. The Schloss, residence of counts then grand dukes since the 12th century, houses the Landesmuseum für Kunst und Kulturgeschichte. The state rooms and chapel are worth the admission.

The rhododendrons of the Schlossgarten bloom fragrant from May onward. This 16-hectare English park, laid out by Duke Peter Friedrich Ludwig, preserves specimens planted in 1828 among the oldest in Germany. Open daily from 8 AM.
The Lappan, a 15th-century bell tower isolated from its church, stands as the city's symbol. Three minutes' walk from the Schloss, it marks the entrance to the pedestrian shopping zone.

activities.text: Make a detour to the Schlossgarten's Tropenhaus to see tropical plants in the middle of Lower Saxony. Free, open in the afternoon. In the evening, bats take over the park.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Experience Osnabrück, the historic university city where the Peace of Westphalia was signed in 1648, ending the Thirty Years' War. This cultural hub seamlessly blends medieval heritage with youthful energy, featuring impressive Gothic architecture, the magnificent Town Hall, and a vibrant pedestrian zone. The city's 1,200-year history unfolds through its stunning churches and well-preserved old town.

activities.text: Explore the surrounding Osnabrücker Land, ideal for mountain hiking with routes ranging from short day walks to multi-day treks. The rolling hills and ancient forests offer scenic trails through the Teutoburg Forest and peaceful countryside dotted with traditional farms and historic villages.

Cycle through the Braunschweig and Osnabrück regions on well-marked paths suitable for both racing and leisure cycling. The flat sections offer smooth riding through meadows and along rivers, while the surrounding hills provide challenging climbs rewarded with sweeping panoramic views.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: Osnabrück and Münster vie for the title of 'City of Peace.' It was here, in the Friedenssaal of the Rathaus, that the Treaty of Westphalia was signed in 1648. The 42 portraits of sovereigns and ambassadors still hang on the walls. The 1512 building has carried the European Heritage Label since 2015.

The broken angles of Daniel Libeskind's Felix-Nussbaum-Haus (1998) unsettle you before you even enter. The museum preserves more than 200 works by the painter born in Osnabrück in 1904, murdered at Auschwitz in 1944. Architecture and content speak to each other.

Push open the door of the Remarque-Friedenszentrum to discover the life of Erich Maria Remarque, author of All Quiet on the Western Front, born here in 1898. The permanent exhibition is compact but well done. Free admission.
The Dom Sankt Peter blends Romanesque and Gothic across 109 meters. The cloister and treasury are accessible. Cathedral Square hosts a market on Wednesdays and Saturdays.

activities.text: If you're there on a Saturday, the Wochenmarkt on Domplatz is one of the region's best: smoked sausages, rye bread, cheeses from the Osnabrücker Land. Cheerful atmosphere, well-stocked stalls.

Head down to the Altstadt to try Grünkohl (kale cooked long) in season, or a Pinkus Müller, Osnabrück's beer brewed since 1816. The pedestrian district around Heger-Tor-Straße is packed with small restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Explore the German Wadden Sea National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site ranked alongside the Grand Canyon and Great Barrier Reef as a natural wonder of global significance. This unique tidal ecosystem stretches along Lower Saxony's North Sea coast, hosting millions of migratory birds and remarkable marine life. The dramatic interplay of ebb and flow creates ever-changing landscapes of mudflats, sandbanks, and salt marshes.

Discover the seven East Frisian Islands, one of Germany's most beautiful coastal treasures offering pristine beaches and unique island atmospheres. These car-free havens feature sandy shores, towering dunes, and endless skies where the rhythm of life follows the tides. Each island has its own distinct character, from bustling resorts to tranquil nature reserves.

activities.text: Embark on a guided mudflat walking tour across the exposed seabed during low tide, an experience unique to the Wadden Sea. Wade through this lunar-like landscape teeming with shellfish, crabs, and marine worms while expert guides reveal the secrets of this extraordinary ecosystem. The sensation of walking on the ocean floor as seabirds wheel overhead is truly unforgettable.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: From outside, Ottobeuren's Basilika looks like any large country church. Push the door: the interior explodes with stucco, frescoes, and gilding. Built between 1737 and 1766, it's nicknamed the Swabian Escorial. Free visit, organ concerts in summer.

In the Kaisersaal, sixteen Habsburg emperors in marble stare down from their pedestals. The monastery's reception hall gives a sense of the abbey's baroque power and wealth. Accessible by guided tour only.

Founded in 764, Ottobeuren's Benedictine abbey has run uninterrupted through the centuries. The Klostermuseum traces this history through the old conventual rooms. There's also a Klosterladen where the monks sell jams and liqueurs.

activities.text: Attend an organ concert in the Ottobeuren Basilika during summer. The acoustics are remarkable, and concerts are often free or by donation. Check the monastery website for the schedule.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Cruise ships built 30 km from the sea, in a covered hangar perched on a river: Meyer Werft in Papenburg defies logic. The visitor center exhibits full-size cabins and a six-meter-diameter propeller. 90-minute guided tour, reservation required.

Stroll along the Hauptkanal, the most unusual pedestrian promenade in the north. Replicas of historic sailing ships are moored right in the shopping district. No parking meters anywhere in town, free parking everywhere.

activities.text: If you're lucky, an Überführung of a cruise ship on the Ems happens during your stay: a 300-meter giant glides a few meters from the banks, escorted by tugboats. Dates depend on weather and tides, announced at the last minute.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Humboldt called it the "most beautiful basalt cone in Europe." The Hoher Parkstein, a volcanic plug 24 million years old, raises its hexagonal columns 38 m above the village. Classified as a Naturdenkmal since 1937, it's one of Germany's 77 notable geotopes.

Climb the Kreuzweg, five minutes of steep ascent, and you'll find at the top the Bergkirche St. Marien (1852) and a circular panorama over Upper Palatinate. On clear days, Czechia sketches itself on the horizon.

activities.text: At the Vulkanerlebnis, the floor rumbles and red smoke rises from the planks: the museum simulates a volcanic eruption every hour. Fun and well-done, especially with children. Entry: €3.50 adult.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Discover Passau, the Bavarian Venice, a spectacular city built at the confluence of three waterways: the blue Danube, the green Inn and the black Ilz. This unique geographical setting creates a fascinating chromatic spectacle where waters of different colors are slow to mix. The old town on its peninsula offers romantic perspectives at every turn of the lane.

Visit St. Stephen's Cathedral, an Italian Baroque masterpiece housing the world's largest cathedral organ with its 17,974 pipes. Daily organ concerts make this monumental instrument resonate beneath vaults adorned with gilded stucco and celestial frescoes. The exceptional acoustics of this sanctuary make it a high point of European sacred music.

Stroll through the old town with its typically Italian pastel facades, a legacy of master builders from the peninsula after the fire of 1662. The elegant squares, vaulted passages and picturesque staircases bear witness to Passau's historic role as a commercial crossroads between Central Europe and Italy.

activities.text: Walk along the quays at the confluence of the three rivers

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: In 1943, the first V2 rocket was launched from Peenemünde. The Historisch-Technisches Museum traces this research site's history, the ambivalent birthplace of space exploration. The former Soviet submarine U-461, moored in the harbor, can be visited as a complement. Budget two hours minimum.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Pillnitz Castle is actually an ensemble of three palaces set in a 28-hectare park on the banks of the Elbe, 15 km southeast of Dresden. Augustus the Strong had received it from a disgraced former mistress—Countess of Cosel—and decided to make it a pleasure palace "in the Indian taste," meaning exotic. Architect Pöppelmann built between 1720 and 1724 the Wasserpalais and Bergpalais, two symmetrical buildings with characteristic pagoda roofs, framing a Baroque courtyard. A wide staircase descends from the Wasserpalais to the Elbe, where royal gondolas docked directly. In 1791, it was here that the Emperor of Austria and the King of Prussia signed the Declaration of Pillnitz, threatening military intervention in France if the French royal family were mistreated—which precipitated the declaration of war.

The New Palace, built between 1819 and 1826 after the 1818 fire that destroyed the Renaissance wing, closes the east side of the courtyard and now houses the Museum of Decorative Arts of the Dresden State Collections. Its classical dome is the only one of its kind in Dresden. The park also contains a Chinese pavilion from 1804 and a neoclassical English pavilion from 1780. The garden's camellia, imported from Japan around 1780 and planted in 1800, is Europe's oldest: it measures 8.9 meters tall and produces up to 35,000 carmine red flowers between February and April.

activities.text: Stroll in Pillnitz Park between the three palaces, French gardens, English garden, and greenhouses. You can also walk along the Elbe on the landscaped banks below the Wasserpalais.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Pirna is the gateway to Saxon Switzerland—and a medieval town worth a stop in itself. Its lanes around the Marktplatz have remained almost intact since the 16th century, with Renaissance facades with Gothic porticoes and houses with Baroque elements in adjacent streets (Schmiedergasse, Schössergasse). The painter Bernardo Bellotto, known as Canaletto, worked there in the 1750s and left several paintings of the town that show how little it has changed—some are visible at the Residenzschloss in Dresden.

The Marienkirche, whose steeples dominate the Marktplatz, is a Gothic church built from the 14th to 16th centuries. Its interior, modified in the Baroque era, retains Gothic ribbed vaults and a Renaissance altarpiece.

Sonnenstein Castle dominates the town from a rocky spur. Built from the 13th century for the Margraves of Meissen, it became a psychiatric hospital in 1811—the first establishment of this type in Saxony. Between 1940 and 1941, as part of the Nazi euthanasia program Aktion T4, more than 13,700 people with mental and physical disabilities were gassed in its cellars. A memorial on the site documents these crimes and honors the victims. The castle now houses the district administrative headquarters.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Plau am See received its town charter in the 13th century and retains a compact historic center with medieval lanes and half-timbered houses around the river port. The town hall, designed in Dutch Renaissance style, is one of the most striking buildings on the central square.

Climb up to the ruins of Plau Castle, which still dominate the center from their promontory. Two steps away, the Gothic church of Sankt Marien deserves a stop for its distinctive bell tower and austere interiors typical of northern Germany.

The Hubbrücke is the town's liveliest spot: this historic lift bridge spans the Elde River right in the center and regularly raises to let pleasure boats pass. Settle into a terrace table by the quay to watch the maneuver—it's Plau am See's essential ritual.

activities.text: The Plauer See, Mecklenburg's third-largest lake at 38 square kilometers, is surrounded by reeds where cranes and ospreys nest. The bike path around it extends for 55 kilometers—a 10-kilometer section toward Bad Stuer passes the lift bridge and follows wild shorelines.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast

visits.text: Nine degrees all at once, in the middle of summer, when you enter the Teufelshöhle in Pottenstein: the 1,900-meter-long cave - of which 800 meters are accessible to the public - maintains this temperature year-round. The guided tour lasts 45 minutes and passes through the "Barbarossadom," a grand chamber measuring 45 × 18 × 15 meters according to site data, and past a complete cave bear skeleton. Open all year, including winter.

The Burg Pottenstein dominates the village from a limestone outcrop. The thousand-year-old enclosure today houses a restaurant and a small exhibition; the official site indicates seasonal opening. The view across the dolomitic valleys of Franconian Switzerland deserves the ascent.

activities.text: After the cave, take the trail toward Schöngrundsee along the Weihersbachtal stream. The Teufelshöhle café, open April to October, offers a terrace facing the cliff - the route's most spectacular coffee break. Franconian Switzerland counts more than 1,000 caves in total.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: The Darßer Ort lighthouse, at the peninsula's tip, is reached on foot or by bike through the Darß primeval forest. The Natureum, museum of the Boddenlandschaft national park, sits right beside it. The Darß's west beach is one of the Baltic's wildest.

activities.text: Hunt for Darßer Haustüren, painted and carved house doors—a local tradition now protected cultural heritage. Each door tells the owner's story. Prerow's Darß Museum documents them.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Board a ferry from Prien-Stock harbor to reach Herreninsel Island on Chiemsee, Bavaria's largest lake. The crossing takes about 20 minutes. In 1873, Ludwig II purchased the entire island for 350,000 florins from an industrialist who planned to clear-cut all its forests. Construction of the palace began in 1878 under architect Georg von Dollmann.

Tour Herrenchiemsee Palace, Ludwig II's final and most lavish project - and where he spent the least time: just 16 days total. The king spent more money on this single construction than on Neuschwanstein and Linderhof combined. At his death in 1886, 50 rooms remained unfinished. The Great Hall of Mirrors, inspired by Versailles, measures 98 meters - 25 meters longer than its French model. To illuminate it, 70 servants had to light 33 candelabras and 52 chandeliers.

After the palace, take the boat to Fraueninsel (Women's Island), where the Frauenwörth Benedictine convent has existed for 1,200 years - one of Germany's oldest monastic communities still in operation. Walk around the small island (one hour on foot) among fishermen's houses and monastery gardens. The nuns produce a renowned local brandy.

activities.text: Late afternoon from Schönau am Königssee, stroll the Chiemsee shores (Priener Uferweg) or follow the lakeside paths from Prien. Sunset with the Alps as backdrop.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
German Alpine Road: Lake Constance to Königssee
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Prora deserves a stop separate from the pass-through from Binz. The Dokumentationszentrum traces the KdF complex's history, meant to accommodate 20,000 vacationers simultaneously. The contrast between the ideology and the sandy beach just behind is stark.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Putbus is Rügen's only neoclassical town, created in the early 19th century by Prince Malte zu Putbus. Its Circus, a round square ringed with white houses, evokes Bath more than the Baltic. The Rasender Roland, a steam train chugging at 30 km/h, departs from here to Binz and Sellin.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Quedlinburg counts approximately 2,000 half-timbered houses spanning six centuries of construction—from the 14th to the 19th century. UNESCO-listed in 1994, the town owes this distinction to its imperial origins: first mentioned in 922 in a charter by Henry I the Fowler, it became the favorite residence of the first Kings of Germany. Between 922 and 1207, Germanic rulers stayed here 69 times.

The Collegiate Church of St. Servatius dominates the town from the Schlossberg. Built from 1070 to 1129 on 9th-century foundations, it is UNESCO-classified as a masterpiece of Romanesque art. In its crypt lie Henry I and his wife Mathilde—Germany's first rulers. Under the Third Reich, Heinrich Himmler transformed the crypt into a neo-pagan sanctuary dedicated to Henry I, whom he considered "the most German of German leaders."

The Lyonel Feininger Gallery presents works by the American-German master who joined the Bauhaus in 1919 and taught there until the Nazis forced its closure. He maintained a personal connection with Quedlinburg and deposited here a series of works that form this permanent collection.

The Marktplatz is framed by the town hall mentioned as early as 1310 and its Roland statue, a medieval symbol of municipal freedoms. The inner courtyards hidden behind the facades are worth exploring.

activities.text: Climb to the Schlossberg for views over Quedlinburg's rooftops and the Harz plain, then descend through cobbled alleyways. About 15 minutes from the market square.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites
Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: The exterior looks like a real ninth-century Slavic fortress—because it's an exact reconstruction. The Slawenburg Raddusch was excavated from 1984 to 1990 before being rebuilt from 1999 to 2003. The ringwall measured 57 meters in diameter, 10 meters wide, and 9 meters high. Inside the concrete shell: 130,000 years of Lower Lusatian archaeology, and the 'Götze von Raddusch,' an oak idol dated 926—unique in Brandenburg. Check the schedule before coming: the Slawenburg closes intermittently.

activities.text: The 600-meter Zeitsteg runs from the Slawenburg to the shore of the flooding lake: archaeological panels on 130,000 years of human presence in Lower Lusatia. Count on 30 minutes round-trip, much longer if you linger by the Götze's display case.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: The Karl May Museum occupies two buildings: Villa Shatterhand, where the writer lived from 1895 until his death in 1912, and Villa Bärenfett, a log cabin in Far West style built next door. Karl May—who had never set foot in America when he wrote his novels about Winnetou—possessed here a collection of over 800 Native American ethnographic objects from the 18th and 19th centuries. His office, library, and Oriental salon are preserved identically in Villa Shatterhand. May is the most-read German novelist of all time, with 200 million copies sold worldwide.

The vineyards of Radebeul carpet the hillsides above the town, facing south on terraces supported by stone walls. This 507-hectare region is Germany's smallest and one of its northernmost—vines have been cultivated here since at least the 12th century. The dominant grape varieties are Riesling, Müller-Thurgau, and Pinot Blanc. Wackerbarth Castle, a state wine estate founded in 1728, is the valley's best known.

activities.text: Hiking in the Radebeul vineyards with views over the Elbe valley. Marked trails cross the vineyard terraces to the Bismarckturm at the hilltop. Allow about 2 hours for a complete circuit with views of Dresden in the distance.

Saxon wine tasting at a Radebeul winemaker. Local winemakers offer guided tastings in the cellar or on the terrace with views over the vines—Riesling, Pinot Blanc, and Goldriesling are the region's flagship wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Ravensburg has preserved its medieval profile intact: towers, gates, and ramparts frame a merchant town prosperous since the 14th century. The Mehlsack, a 51-meter white tower, dominates the skyline. Climb it for views across Lake Constance to the Alps.
The Veitsburg, a former Guelph fortress above the town, offers 360° panoramas and a Biergarten popular with locals. The Museum Ravensburger, devoted to the board game publisher, appeals to families.

The Museum Humpis-Quartier occupies a complex of 15th-century patrician houses in the center. It traces Ravensburg's history as a member of the Great Ravensburg Trading Society, a commercial network that rivaled the Hanseatic League.

activities.text: Stroll down Marktstraße under the arcades and sample a Seele, an elongated bread roll typical to Swabia, crusty and studded with salt and caraway. They're found almost exclusively in Upper Swabia.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Explore Regensburg, Bavaria's first capital, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This city with 2,000 years of history houses more than 1,500 buildings classified as historic monuments, constituting Germany's best-preserved medieval ensemble. The patrician towers competing in height bear witness to the power of medieval merchant families.

Cross the Stone Bridge, a masterpiece of 12th-century medieval engineering that served as the model for Prague's Charles Bridge. At 310 meters long and supported by 16 arches, this bridge offers a splendid panoramic view of the old town skyline dominated by the spires of the Gothic cathedral. It was once the only stone bridge over the Danube between Ulm and Vienna.

Admire St. Peter's Cathedral, the only example of pure French Gothic in Bavaria, whose medieval stained glass windows rank among Germany's finest. The old town abounds with architectural treasures: the old town hall where the Diet of the Holy Roman Empire sat, the colorful patrician houses and Renaissance interior courtyards.

activities.text: Walk along the Danube quays at sunset

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Founded in 724 by itinerant bishop Pirmin, Reichenau Abbey was one of Carolingian and Ottonian Europe's major cultural centers. UNESCO since 2000, Lake Constance's largest island keeps its three Romanesque churches, its vegetable fields, and its calm.

Benedictine monks returned to the island in 2001, two and a half centuries after the last ones left. The abbey also serves as town hall. The Minster's Schatzkammer houses reliquaries and illuminated manuscripts of considerable value.
Visit Sankt Georg church for its 10th-century murals: the most complete Ottonian cycle preserved north of the Alps. The colors have survived a thousand years. Free entry.

activities.text: Small but erudite, the reconstructed Kräutergarten behind the cloister follows the prescriptions of Walahfrid Strabo, 9th-century abbot and author of the botanical treatise Hortulus. Medicinal herbs, aromatic plants, flowers. Guided tours in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: Schloss Rheinsberg was Frederick the Great's youthful residence before his accession in 1740. The palace on the Grienericksee inspired Tucholsky (Rheinsberg: Ein Bilderbuch für Verliebte, 1912). Rococo rooms, palace theater, English-style park.

activities.text: Circle the Grienericksee by canoe or on foot (5 km loop). The palace reflects in the water in the morning—ideal light for photos. Canoe rental at the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: The Bavarian Forest National Park (Bayerischer Wald) is Germany's oldest national park, created in 1970. It covers 24,000 hectares along the Czech border, continuous with Šumava National Park on the Bohemian side—together they form the largest protected forest area in Central Europe.

Start at the Tierfreigelände in Neuschönau, a 250-hectare natural enclosure where 45 animal species live in conditions close to their wild habitat: wolves, brown bears, lynx, European bison, otters, beavers, deer and large raptors. A 7-kilometer trail winds through the site—allow 3 to 4 hours. Bring binoculars, as some animals keep their distance. Entry is free.

Head to Rachelsee, a mountain lake at 1,071 meters altitude, surrounded by centuries-old beeches and spruces. Local legend attributes its name to the devil's grandmother, and tells that an evil woman was the last to navigate its waters, her coffin accompanied by a flight of black ravens. The trail from Spiegelau is well marked.

Stop at the Hans-Eisenmann-Haus visitor center in Neuschönau, which presents the park's history and wildlife. It's also the starting point for the Baumwipfelpfad, a 1,300-meter aerial walkway that winds through the forest canopy.

activities.text: Hike the marked trails of the park from Riedlhütte or Neuschönau. Over 500 kilometers of paths crisscross the park, some passing through "primeval forest" zones where no human intervention has been permitted for decades. The areas around Lusen and Rachel peaks are the wildest.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Kröpeliner Straße, Rostock's wide thoroughfare converted into a pedestrian zone in 1968—one of East Germany's first—is well-restored and honest, without the commercial staging of other Hanseatic cities. It stretches from Neuer Markt to Kröpeliner Tor for about 600 m, alternating brick Gothic, Renaissance, and some GDR-era concrete buildings. The Universitätsplatz midway is the historic heart.

The Kröpeliner Tor tower rises 54 m above the end of the pedestrian zone, built around 1270 as part of the city fortifications. Of the twenty-two gates Rostock once possessed, four have survived. A small local history museum lodges in the tower. The view from the rampart walk justifies the stairs.

The Marienkirche houses a medieval astronomical clock still in operation. This clock dates from the 14th century and displays the liturgical calendar, moon phases, and solar time with a precision its builders took a century to perfect. Each day at noon, the upper dial's figurines parade. The church itself dates from the 13th century and its 117-m spire is visible from Warnemünde on clear days.

activities.text: A 15-min S-Bahn ride separates Rostock from Warnemünde, the historic seaside district at the Warnow estuary. The white sand beach, aligned Strandkörbe, and old red-brick lighthouse form the most repeated image of the Mecklenburg coast. Less touristy than Binz, the waterfront remains local and calm outside August.

If you're there in August (typically first week), the Hanse Sail brings together each year on the Warnow one to two hundred traditional sailing ships and museum vessels. It's one of northern Germany's major maritime gatherings, open to the public and free from the quays. Boarding excursions are also offered.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast
German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

places.name: Rothenburg Ob Der Tauber

visits.text: Rothenburg ob der Tauber ranks among Europe's best-preserved medieval towns. The town, perched on a plateau above the Tauber River, has remained virtually unchanged since the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648), which bankrupted the city but paradoxically preserved it: too poor to modernize, it kept its medieval face intact. Its 13th and 14th-century ramparts still stand for 4 kilometers, punctuated by 42 towers and gates. The wall walk (Turmweg) is freely accessible and offers a walk of about 90 minutes at 6 meters height, with plunging views over the red-tiled roofs and the valley. After the March 31, 1945 bombings destroyed more than a third of the old town, international aid enabled faithful reconstruction—donors' names are engraved on the restored rampart stones.

The Plönlein—from the Latin planum, "little square"—is Rothenburg's most photographed corner and one of Germany's most reproduced images. At the intersection of two sloping streets, a slightly crooked yellow and red half-timbered house stands between the Siebers Tower (1385) on the left and the Kobolzeller Gate on the right, which descends toward the Tauber valley. This setting inspired the village in Disney's Pinocchio. Come early in the morning to avoid crowds and enjoy the low-angle light. From there, climb the Röder Tower or the town hall belfry for a 360° panorama of the town.

St. James's Church (St. Jakobskirche), built between 1311 and 1484, dominates the Marktplatz with its Gothic silhouette. It houses the Altarpiece of the Holy Blood, a masterpiece by sculptor Tilman Riemenschneider. The neighboring town hall, blending 13th-century Gothic elements with a Renaissance façade added in the 16th century, guards a mechanical secret: each day at set hours, the clock on the façade comes alive and an automaton depicts the legendary scene of the Meistertrunk—the "Master Draught"—where Mayor Nusch supposedly downed 3.25 liters of wine in one go to save the town from destruction in 1631.

The Burggarten, at the western tip of town, occupies the site of the Hohenstaufen castle built in 1142, from which King Conrad III ruled the Holy Roman Empire. Of the double fortress, only St. Blaise's Chapel remains, now converted into a memorial. This terraced garden offers Rothenburg's finest panorama: unobstructed views of the Tauber valley, vineyards, town rooftops, and the double row of ramparts.

The Medieval Crime Museum (Kriminalmuseum), housed in the former commandery of the Order of St. John, displays over a thousand years of legal history across four floors. Public humiliation masks, dunking cages, "gossips' violins" for punishing talkative women: these instruments reveal the brutal realities of medieval law enforcement. In complete contrast, the German Christmas Museum (Deutsches Weihnachtsmuseum) celebrates German holiday traditions year-round through thousands of decorations, nutcrackers, and historic Advent calendars—Rothenburg hosts one of Germany's oldest Christmas markets.

The Imperial City Museum (Reichsstadtmuseum), housed in the former Dominican convent founded in 1258, preserves an intact medieval monastic kitchen, Rothenburg's Stations of the Cross (1494)—a cycle of twelve panels painted by Martinus Schwarz—and Mayor Nusch's tankard, the famous 3.25-liter cup adorned with the emperor surrounded by the seven prince-electors (1616), which illustrates the Meistertrunk legend.

Fifty kilometers south of Rothenburg, Dinkelsbühl is the forgotten "little sister" of the Romantic Road—and that's precisely its charm. This former Free Imperial City, fortified by Emperor Henry V, has kept its ramparts intact thanks to an edict by King Ludwig I of Bavaria and was spared from bombings in both world wars. The walls feature about twenty towers, the oldest dating from 1370. The Wörnitztor, the town's oldest gate, opens onto streets lined with colorful gabled patrician houses, the Wine Market square (Weinmarkt), and St. George's Church, one of the finest Gothic hall churches in southern Germany, built between 1448 and 1499, with its net vaulting and St. Aurelian's reliquary.

activities.text: Join the Night Watchman (Nachtwächter) tour, departing each evening from the Marktplatz. In medieval costume, lantern in hand and halberd on shoulder, the watchman guides visitors through the old town's dark alleys, recounting Rothenburg's legends, scandals, and anecdotes—a tradition dating back to the Middle Ages when watchmen patrolled to prevent fires and disturbances.

Try a Schneeball (snowball), Rothenburg's specialty: puff pastry rolled into a ball, fried, and dusted with powdered sugar or coated in chocolate. This Franconian pastry comes in dozens of variations at bakeries along Schmiedgasse and Herrngasse.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria
Germany's Romantic Road: Fairytale Castles and Medieval Towns of Bavaria
Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends
From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

map_keywords: St. Jakobskirche Rothenburg ob der Tauber

visits.text: Kitsch, certainly, but the Drosselgasse commands its own atmosphere: 144 meters of pedestrian street lined with Weinstuben, live music, outdoor tables. Touristy to the point of caricature in season - and yet it's exactly what you come for. Arrive at opening around 10:30 a.m. to catch it still empty.

The locals call it the Daniel: the 90-meter-high, 75-tonne Niederwald Monument according to official site measurements. Erected between 1871 and 1883 to commemorate German unification, it overlooks the Rhine from the Niederwald forest. The cable car (Seilbahn) climbs from town in minutes - the valley view alone justifies the ticket.

activities.text: Take the cable car up to the monument then walk back down through the Niederwald trails toward Assmannshausen: about 3 km through woods with Rhine panoramas. At the bottom, the Winzerexpress (in service since 1978) brings you back to town through the vineyards for those who'd rather skip the climb.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

places.name: Ruhestein

visits.text: Boring name, impressive content. The Black Forest National Park visitor center at Ruhestein presents the ecology of the primeval forest reclaiming its territory since the park's creation in 2014. A sober exhibition on the forest framework and the species making a comeback. A 30-minute stop before descending to the Mummelsee, accessible 8 km away.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

map_keywords: Ruhestein National Park Center Black Forest

visits.text: Saalfeld's Feengrotten - «fairy grottoes» - are the galleries of a former alum mine operated from 1530. After closing in 1850, the mine was rediscovered in 1910, and the third chamber - the «Fairy Realm» - was discovered in 1913. Opening to the public occurred on May 31, 1914. In 1993, the Guinness Book of World Records designated them «the most colorful grottoes in the world»: the mineral formations - stalactites, stalagmites, concretions - display hues ranging from blue to red through yellow, due to the oxidation of iron phosphate (diadochite), a rare mineral. Since 1914, more than 20 million visitors have traversed the three chambers connected by galleries.

Since 1937, a gallery of Saalfeld's grottoes has housed Germany's first «Emanatorium» - a healing gallery by inhalation of underground air. The gallery's microclimate (constant temperature, mineral-charged air) was used to treat respiratory conditions. This therapeutic gallery is Germany's oldest.

The Grottoneum, an interactive museum adjacent to the grottoes, presents the site's geological and mining history along with exhibitions on the region's minerals and crystals. Children's activities are offered.

activities.text: Forest adventure trail near Saalfeld's Feengrotten, with family-friendly routes through the woods above the grottoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: Built in 1334 under the name Zapfenburg to protect pilgrims on the road to Gottsbüren, the Sababurg was later transformed into a hunting lodge by Landgrave William I from 1490 onward. Between 1571 and 1591, a thorn hedge three metres high and five kilometres long encircled it — the very image said to have inspired the Brothers Grimm for Sleeping Beauty. The state of Hesse has been restoring the castle since 2018.

A rose garden surrounds the ruined towers of the Sababurg, with rare species and climbing plants weaving through the medieval remains. In summer (Friday to Sunday), open-air theatre performances bring Grimm fairy tales to life in the castle courtyard. The atmosphere is oddly enchanting — part building site, part fairy tale.

activities.text: The Tierpark Sababurg, open since 1571 and one of the oldest wildlife parks in Europe, is home to European bison, lynx, and wild horses roaming the Reinhardswald. Directly adjacent to the castle, it's the natural companion to your visit. Allow two hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: Salem Abbey, a former Cistercian monastery founded in 1134, is one of southern Germany's largest monastic complexes. After the 1803 secularization, the Margraves of Baden made it their residence. Salem boarding school, one of Europe's most prestigious, occupies part of the buildings.

The rococo stuccowork by Johann Georg Dirr in the palace salons displays exceptional finesse. The Feuerwehrmuseum, installed in the former stables, traces firefighting history with a collection of vintage vehicles. Combined ticket available.

activities.text: Salem's park and gardens are freely accessible. A craft market is regularly held in the courtyard. Salem lies midway between Ravensburg and Lake Constance.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Savor Salzwedel, the town of Baumkuchen, where this traditional spit cake has been made according to ancestral recipes for centuries. This sweet specialty with its multiple golden layers, slowly cooked on a rotating cylinder, has become the culinary emblem of the Altmark. The old Hanseatic town with its half-timbered houses and Gothic brick churches provides a charming setting for this gourmet discovery.

Cruise on Lake Arendsee aboard the paddle steamer Queen Arendsee, which glides peacefully over the waters of this 5 km² natural lake. This nostalgic cruise evokes the atmosphere of old-time steamboats while offering enchanting panoramas of the wooded shores. The lake, the largest in the Altmark, is a popular destination for relaxation and water activities.

Discover the singular story of Gustav Nagel, legendary itinerant preacher and natural healer of the early 20th century. This eccentric figure lived in a cabin he had built near Lake Arendsee, traveling the region barefoot to spread his messages of peace and communion with nature. His legacy bears witness to the spiritual allure this peaceful region already held.

activities.text: Tasting of the famous Salzwedel Baumkuchen in a traditional pastry shop

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Jasmund National Park, established on September 12, 1990—one of the last acts of the East German government before reunification—is Germany's smallest national park at 3,003 hectares. It occupies the Jasmund peninsula in northeastern Rügen and is home to Germany's highest chalk cliffs: the Königsstuhl rises 118 meters above the Baltic. The chalk that forms these cliffs was deposited about 70 million years ago. Every storm carries off entire sections of cliff into the sea, exposing fossils of sea urchins and sponges. Painter Caspar David Friedrich immortalized these cliffs in 1818 in his painting Chalk Cliffs on Rügen, which made them a symbol of German Romanticism.

The Hochuferweg, a trail that follows the cliff between Sassnitz and Lohme for several kilometers, is one of the most spectacular on the Baltic coast. It passes through the Stubnitz beech forest—493 hectares of old-growth beech woods listed as a UNESCO World Natural Heritage site—and offers overhanging views of the sea. At the Königsstuhl, since 2023, an elliptical 185-meter walkway floats above the cliff from a cable-stayed mast, offering a vertiginous perspective on the chalk white and Baltic blue.

activities.text: Hike from Sassnitz to the Königsstuhl along the coastal Hochuferweg: about 10 km round trip through the beech forest and cliff viewpoints. The trail alternates between deep forest passages and windows open to the sea. Allow 3 to 4 hours. The park is also accessible by bus from Sassnitz to the Königsstuhl visitor center.

The harbor of Sassnitz, former ferry port to Sweden and Poland, now hosts fishing and pleasure boats. Walk along the quays in late afternoon to watch fishermen return and the fish halls. Several terraces serve freshly smoked herring, a local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea

visits.text: Schierke is the last village before the summit—a handful of inns, a church, fir trees. Ideal for observing the atmosphere on Walpurgis Night eve (April 30th) before the Brocken ascent the next morning. Outside of April 30th, it's simply quiet and well positioned.

activities.text: Goethe climbed on December 10, 1777 through snow and fog—today you can reach the Brocken (1,141 m) from Schierke via the Goetheweg in 3 hours, or take the Brockenbahn steam train from Wernigerode (round-trip around €49). At the summit: optical phenomenon of the Brocken specter (your shadow projected with a halo on the mist), museum on alpine flora and the history of the East-West German border—the Brocken was a closed Soviet military zone until December 3, 1989. Check weather at brocken.de before departing.

days_plan.itin_title: Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: Founded in 1985 in an 1818 farmhouse that's been a listed monument since 1960, the Bauernmuseum Schlepzig is the only museum in the Unterspreewald. Across 5,000 square meters you'll find farming tools from the Kaiser's era through the GDR, a reconstructed living room, and the clay oven where cake is still baked during demonstrations. Open April through October, Tuesday to Sunday from 10 a.m.

activities.text: Take the towpath along the Spree to reach the heart of Schlepzig village, one of the rare Unterspreewald settlements to have kept its traditional farmhouse fabric intact. Twenty minutes is enough for a complete loop through the lanes.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: The Nydam Boat, a 4th-century rowing vessel excavated in 1863, is displayed in the Nydamhalle at Schloss Gottorf: 23 metres long, 28 oars, built from clinker-laid oak with no keel. It was probably a Germanic war vessel. No other archaeological find of this size and age is as well preserved anywhere in Northern Europe.

Dom St. Petri dominates the Schleswig skyline from every angle — its 112-metre spire towers over the Schlei fjord. Inside stands the Brüggemann Altarpiece, a masterwork of early 16th-century wood carving composed of 392 individual figures.

Schloss Gottorf looks like a pleasure palace but is in fact a transformed medieval fortress — the Dukes of Schleswig-Gottorf ruled from here between the 12th and 18th centuries. Now the state museum of Schleswig-Holstein, it also houses a reconstruction of the Gottorfer Globus, a Baroque globe-planetarium 3 metres in diameter that rotated slowly for its 17th-century visitors.

activities.text: Walk through the reconstructed Baroque garden of Gottorf, a terraced garden descending to the Schlei: clipped hedges, formal parterres and a water view reproduce the 17th-century original. Free and open year-round, it is often empty off-season.

The banks of the Schlei smell of seaweed and the tar of old wooden boats — the brackish-water scent unique to Baltic fjords. The Lollfuß promenade follows the waterfront from the old town harbour to the cathedral: twenty minutes on foot, with sailing boats in view.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Around the bend of the forest path, Schloss Neuschwanstein's white towers surge above the fir trees. Ludwig II launched construction in 1869 on the site of two medieval ruins. The castle mixes Romanesque Revival and Gothic Revival, inspired by Wartburg and Pierrefonds. He lived there just 172 days.

The Singers' Hall, on Schloss Neuschwanstein's fourth floor, reproduces Wartburg's. Ludwig II designed it as a "worthy temple" to Richard Wagner. The castle was never finished, and this interrupted construction is part of its legend.

activities.text: Book your tickets online the day before at hohenschwangau.de. Morning slots sell out first—aim for 9 a.m. The walk up from the Ticket Center takes 30 to 40 minutes. Large backpacks are forbidden inside.

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: Closed to the public during the Cold War (military zone until 1996), the Schneeberg is Franconia's highest summit at 1,052 m. The Bundeswehr's former telecommunications relay station still crowns the top. No refreshments up there: bring your own picnic.

activities.text: Climb up to the Backöfele, the wooden observation tower offering a complete panorama over the horseshoe of twelve Fichtelgebirge peaks. According to local legend, bread was baked here in wartime. Allow 1.5 hours from the Bischofsgrün car park.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Board one of the electric boats on Königssee from Schönau - the only craft allowed on this protected lake since 1978 within Berchtesgaden National Park. The lake, 8 km long and 190 meters deep, lies trapped between vertical limestone walls. Midway across, the pilot plays trumpet against the rock face to demonstrate the extraordinary echo.

Disembark at St. Bartholomä, a peninsula accessible only by boat. The baroque pilgrimage church, first built in 1134 and remodeled in the early 18th century, is recognizable from afar by its red onion domes. The ensemble of church and former royal hunting lodge, silhouetted against the 1,800-meter vertical east face of the Watzmann, is one of Germany's most photographed scenes.

Continue by boat to Salet to walk to the Obersee - a flat 15-minute walk from the dock. This smaller lake, with no boats whatsoever, displays absolutely transparent water and perfect reflections of the surrounding rock walls. Since commercial fishing ended in 1978, the lake has returned to wilderness.

Ride the Jennerbahn to reach the Jenner summit at 1,800 meters. The view embraces the entire Königssee, the east face of the Watzmann, and the Berchtesgaden Alps on the Austrian side. Salzburg is 30 km away - this is where the circuit naturally ends for those returning rental cars in Austria.

activities.text: Hike the Malerwinkel-Rundweg, a panoramic forest trail above the Königssee offering the classic elevated view of the lake. In 2015, this spot ranked as Germany's 12th most popular tourist attraction. Allow 1 to 2 hours depending on your pace.

days_plan.itin_title: German Alpine Road: Lake Constance to Königssee

visits.text: Explore Schönebeck, the salt town, whose millennial history is intimately linked to the extraction of white gold. The Salt Museum (Salzlandmuseum) takes you on a fascinating journey through the centuries to understand the economic and social importance of this precious resource. The picturesque old town with its colorful half-timbered houses invites leisurely strolls through streets steeped in history.

Relax in the spa district of Bad Salzelmen, where the majestic Gradierwerk (graduation tower) diffuses therapeutic salt spray into the air. This monumental wooden structure, where salt water trickles over blackthorn bundles, creates a unique iodine-rich microclimate. The salt bath offers an authentic wellness experience inherited from German spa traditions.

Admire Europe's longest inhabited half-timbered house on the Elbe, a spectacular testimony to traditional German architecture. The Salt Flower, a contemporary art installation on the river, dialogues with this historical heritage. St. Johannis Church completes this architectural panorama with its tower dominating the town.

activities.text: Visit the Pömmelte sanctuary, a cultic site over 4,000 years old with its reconstructed circular ditch

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Schotten is the gateway to the Naturpark Vulkanregion Vogelsberg, Germany's oldest nature park. The Vogelsberg is Europe's largest contiguous volcanic massif: it formed approximately 20 million years ago during a period of intense Tertiary volcanic activity. Basaltic lavas created a topography of rounded domes, peat bogs, and springs. The Vulkaneum in Schotten, a museum-interpretation center renovated in 2018, presents the geology and ecology of the massif with interactive stations.

The Vogelsberg landscapes are characterized by their mixed beech and spruce forests, flowering meadows, and dry-stone hedges. The central plateau—the Oberwald—is covered with forests traversed by springs and small streams. The slopes are dotted with basaltic formations with distinctive silhouettes. The region forms a water recharge zone for much of Hesse.

The Hoherodskopf (764 m) is the most iconic accessible summit in the Vogelsberg alongside the Taufstein (773 m). The Naturpark information center is located here, with a permanent exhibition on volcanic geology and the massif's biodiversity. In winter, cross-country ski trails are groomed on the plateau. In summer, a 7 km trail loops around the summit, passing through peat bog, streams, and several viewpoints.
