activities.text: Hike a section of the Vulkanring Vogelsberg, a circular long-distance trail that crosses the massif along its most remarkable geological formations: basalt flows, volcanic columns, and "Bilstein" cliffs. Nature guides offer guided excursions from Hoherodskopf.
Cycling or e-biking in the Vogelsberg on the marked trail network. Elevation changes remain moderate on the plateau. Bicycles can be rented in several park villages.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks

visits.text: Discover Schwäbisch Hall, a beautiful and charming town that was once a major center of salt production, the precious 'white gold' that created its wealth. The old town center winds through streets of magnificent half-timbered buildings to a gorgeous central market square dominated by the imposing St. Michael's Church. The covered wooden bridge spanning the Kocher River creates one of Germany's most photographed scenes.

Visit the local museum to learn about the fascinating history of salt making that gave the town its name and fortune. Salt has been extracted here since Celtic times, and the industry shaped every aspect of local life, architecture and culture. The Hällisch-Fränkisches Museum brings this heritage to life through engaging exhibits about the salt workers and merchants who built the town.

Explore the Großcomburg monastery complex perched on a hill just above the town, where 16th-century defensive walls still stand protecting Romanesque chapel and cloister within. The monastery's peaceful courtyards and historic architecture offer a contemplative contrast to the bustling town below. Check the city's festival calendar, as Schwäbisch Hall hosts excellent cultural events throughout the year.

activities.text: Relax in the riverside park along the Kocher and admire views of the picturesque covered bridge

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Above ground, the town has lost most of its charm: a bombing in April 1945 destroyed 70% of the buildings. But underground, Schwandorf hides Bavaria's largest labyrinth of rock cellars. More than 130 rooms carved from sandstone follow one another over nearly a kilometer.

Book the guided tour of the Felsenkeller-Labyrinth to understand how these cellars were used to brew bottom-fermented beer starting in the late 15th century. During World War II, thousands of civilians took refuge here.

activities.text: The temperature drops to 8°C when you enter the galleries. Bring a jacket even in midsummer. The tour lasts around 45 minutes, by reservation at the tourist office (09431-45-550). Warning: limited seating.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: 'Come to Schwarzkollm, to the Black Mill': that's the summons that opens the Sorbian legend of Krabat, the boy who became apprentice to a sorcerer miller. The Krabat-Mühle recreates this 'Black Mill' as a four-sided Lusatian farmstead—six buildings on a large site. You discover Sorbian traditions and the story behind the tale, via guided tour or audio guide. Ideal for families; check the dates for 'Krabat-Saga' performances.

activities.text: Bake your own bread in the Krabat-Mühle's historic wood-fired oven: 'family baking days' must be reserved in advance. Around it, a discovery trail dotted with panels tells the Krabat saga to children. The site comes alive mainly around its events, so everything depends on the day's calendar. Outside special activities, the visit stays short—an hour suffices.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Schwerin Castle floats on its lake—and we're hardly exaggerating; it sits on an island connected to the mainland by a narrow bridge. The current castle was rebuilt in neo-Gothic historicist style between 1847 and 1857 by Friedrich Franz II. It houses today both a museum and the Landtag (regional parliament), making it one of the few historic residences still in political use in Germany.

In Schwerin Cathedral (Dom zu Schwerin), silence is absorbed by the Gothic brick walls. The main construction dates from the 14th century, but older parts date from the 13th. The nave contrasts with the lightness of its columns—a classic of Mecklenburg Backsteingotik. The tower is accessible and provides views over the lake and rooftops.

Schwerinians call it the 'Neuschwanstein of the north'—this isn't flattering hyperbole but sincere architectural comparison, both castles being contemporaneous and sharing the aesthetic of 19th-century romantic historicism. The Schlossgarten designed by Peter Joseph Lenné in the 1840s surrounds the castle with French-style gardens and an orangery.

activities.text: Rent an electric boat on Schwerin Lake: the lake surrounds the castle on three sides and offers a perspective that tourists who stay on the lawns never see. The south shore is uncrowded on weekdays. Rental at the marina 10 min walk from the castle.

If you pass on a Saturday, the market on Alter Garten, the main square facing the castle, features local producers, flowers, and grilled Mecklenburg sausages. The square is framed by the Staatliches Museum Schwerin, which houses a Flemish and Dutch collection assembled by the dukes in the 17th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: Lakes, Castles, and the Baltic Coast
German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Lose yourself in the magnificent gardens of Schloss Schwetzingen, a stunning summer palace with acres of meticulously landscaped grounds that rank among Germany's finest baroque gardens. The salmon-pink palace provides an elegant backdrop to formal parterres, romantic temples and winding woodland paths designed to delight at every turn. This princely retreat was created for the Electors Palatine as an escape from court life in Heidelberg.

Discover the garden's extraordinary follies, including a mosque, Roman temple and bath house that reflect 18th-century fascination with exotic cultures. The circular parterre and grand water features demonstrate the height of baroque garden design and horticultural artistry. If visiting in May, the town's famous white asparagus festival celebrates this prized local delicacy with culinary events throughout the region.

activities.text: Enjoy lunch at one of the charming restaurants along the road leading to the palace gardens

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Locals call it 'der rostige Nagel,' the rusty nail—and it truly is. Sedlitz's Landmarke Lausitzer Seenland is a 30-meter Corten steel tower built in 2008 at the mouth of the Sorno canal into the Sedlitzer See. The rust color is intentional: it recalls the machinery of the former tagebau. From the platform you can make out the Sedlitzer See and three other lakes (Geierswalder, Partwitzer, and Großräschener). Free access, year-round.

activities.text: Just north of the tower, the Schwimmender Steg reaches out over the Sedlitzer See: a floating walkway a few dozen meters long to see the lake at water level, without the 162 steps. Short but effective before heading out.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Black water, 18 meters deep, no one knows the color of the bottom. The Mummelsee is a glacial lake at 1,029 m altitude, 800 m around, the largest and deepest of the seven Karseen in the Black Forest. Eduard Mörike set his ballad 'Die Geister am Mummelsee' here in 1829, a procession of ghosts descending toward the lake. Grimmelshausen had already sent Simplicissimus here to the center of the earth. The lake circuit takes 20 minutes. Don't throw stones: legend says it provokes a storm.

1,163 m, the highest point in the northern Black Forest. Hornisgrinde is reached via 3 km from the Mummelsee, 135 m elevation gain, 45 minutes. At the summit: Bismarck Tower, views over the Rhine plain to Strasbourg on clear days. The complete Mummelsee-Hornisgrinde loop is 6 km, about 2 hours. In October, fog rises from the valley and envelops the Mummelsee on the return.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Seiffen is the iconic village of the Ore Mountains (Erzgebirge), nestled at 700 meters altitude on the Czech border. When the region's mines were exhausted in the 18th century, inhabitants converted to woodworking. In the 19th century, the village established itself as the world center for miniature toys and Christmas decorations. There are more than a hundred craftsmen and forty shops. The wood-turning technique called "Reifendrehen"—a log slice is turned then cut like a pie to obtain dozens of identical animal silhouettes—is unique to Seiffen and inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

The Seiffen Toy Museum traces the village's history and its craftsmanship since the 18th century. Christmas pyramids—those wooden candle carousels that rotate by thermal convection—are exhibited in period versions. The nutcrackers in Saxon soldier form, whose first version was created in the region to ridicule authority figures, are the other local specialty. The tradition of the mining angel and the Schwibbogen, the luminous wooden arch placed in windows, also finds its explanation here: these objects recall the candles that miners placed at the mine entrance on Christmas Eve.

activities.text: Hiking in the Ore Mountains from the village. Marked trails cross conifer forests and ridges with views over the Czech border. In winter, the cross-country ski trail network is one of Germany's densest.

Historic steam train ride on the Fichtelbergbahn network, the narrow-gauge line connecting Cranzahl to Oberwiesenthal since 1897. Period steam locomotives cross the villages and forests of the Ore Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony Road Trip: Dresden, Leipzig & Saxon Switzerland

visits.text: Leuchtenburg dominates the Saale Valley from 395 meters altitude, nicknamed «the Queen of the Saale Valley». Its first documented mention dates to 1221, when it was a military base of the Lords of Lobdeburg. It changed hands frequently and became in 1396 the administrative center of the Wettin villages. In 1553, an 80-meter-deep well was dug into the rock - Thuringia's second-deepest at the time. After its administration closed in 1705, it served variously as a prison, hospice, and asylum. Between 1920 and 1997, Thuringia's first youth hostel operated there. Purchased at auction in 2007 by the Leuchtenburg Foundation, the castle was restored and a new permanent exhibition opened in 2014.

The Porzellanwelten Leuchtenburg traces 800 years of Thuringian porcelain history in the castle's halls. The exhibition explores the art, technique, and culture of this craft of which the town of Kahla, at the castle's foot, is a historic center. Modern installations deliberately contrast with the medieval walls.

The Mythos Burg exhibition presents the castle's medieval history and legends linked to Leuchtenburg. The keep, accessible by a steep staircase, offers a 360-degree panorama over the Harz, the Thuringian Forest, and the Saale's steep valley.

activities.text: Panoramic view from Leuchtenburg's keep: on clear days, the vista extends to the Harz in the north and the Thuringian Forest in the south. The ascent is via a path or stairs from parking areas below.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: From the outside, the former Rosenthal factory looks like a medieval fortress with its massive chimneys. Inside, the Porzellanikon unfolds several thousand square meters on the making of "white gold." You'll find pieces from the Rosenthal collection, bought by the Oberfrankenstiftung in 2009. Selb remains the heart of Germany's porcelain industry.

Walk through the Porzellanikon's six halls to understand each step: kaolin grinding, molding, firing, glazing, decoration. The "Keramik Technique" space shows surprising applications: medical prosthetics, electrical insulators, aerospace components. Closed Mondays.

activities.text: The smell of damp kaolin still drifts through the Porzellanikon workshops where you can paint your own plate. Porcelain decoration courses offered regularly, including for children. Check the calendar at porzellanikon.org.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Sellin Pier measures 394 m and is Rügen island's longest pier. Its first version, inaugurated in 1906, measured 508 m before being reduced by storms and ice—the last destruction dates from winter 1941/42. The current pier, rebuilt in 1998 following models from 1906 and 1927, descends from the top of the cliff to the sea in a spectacular staircase, with a diving gondola at the end.

Destroyed multiple times by ice and storms, then rebuilt in 1998 exactly identical to the 1927 model: this is the Seebrücke's story, but also a metaphor for Sellin itself. The villas of Wilhelmstraße, in carved white wood and open terraces, passed through the GDR era in a state of disrepair then were restored after 1990 one by one. The result is strangely intact.

activities.text: Walk down Wilhelmstraße lined with Bäderarchitektur villas from the Rasender Roland station to the sea in 10 min on foot. The street is steeply sloped—which is why the pier also descends via a monumental staircase to beach level. Below, the restaurants over the water serve fresh fish and pretzels at sunset.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: A Renaissance fortress serving as a mining museum: few places combine that. Senftenberg's Schloss und Festung dates from the sixteenth century and now houses the Museum des Landkreises Oberspreewald-Lausitz. The showpiece is in the cellars: a full-scale reconstructed mine gallery shows coal extraction as it was practiced last century. Closed Mondays.

The Senftenberger See deserves a stop: a former tagebau converted into a lake in the late 1960s, it now offers 18 kilometers of shoreline with fine sandy beaches and a stadthafen with an 80-meter seebrücke over the water. In summer, sailboats and paddleboarders share the water with rental electric boats.

activities.text: You'll embark from the Stadthafen to sail to the neighboring Geierswalder See via the Koschener canal. Electric boat rentals are on-site; count on 2–3 hours for a leisurely cruise and €15–25 depending on boat size.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Lusatia: Spreewald and Mining Lakes in 6 Days

visits.text: Rubens was probably born here in 1577, under inglorious circumstances: his father was under house arrest for adultery. The Oberes Schloss, perched on the Siegberg at 307 meters, houses the Siegerlandmuseum with nine original canvases by the Flemish master. The basement conceals a reconstructed 14-meter-deep mine shaft.

From the castle park, spring tulips frame a panorama over the city. The Nikolaikirche below wears a gilded 'Krönchen' atop its spire, the city's emblem. Siegen claims 86% green space across its municipal area.

activities.text: If you enjoy contemporary art, the Museum für Gegenwartskunst occupies the old postal building, a 10-minute walk from the castle. Rotating collections, modest admission. Otherwise, a café at Marktplatz suffices to observe the easygoing rhythm of this university town.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: From the Danube bridge, the mass of Sigmaringen Castle crushes the limestone cliff. Seat of the Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen line since 1535, it's Germany's second-largest city palace. The Waffenhalle displays some 3,000 pieces, one of Europe's largest private arms collections. Audioguides available in multiple languages.

In 1944–1945, the castle briefly served as seat of the Vichy government in exile. Louis-Ferdinand Céline described the atmosphere in 'Castle to Castle.' The Marstallmuseum, in the stables, presents 17 princely carriages and sleighs.

Stroll downstream along the Danube from the castle: the limestone cliffs of the Upper Danube Valley begin here, offering striking panoramas. The Naturpark Obere Donau ranks among Germany's most beautiful.

activities.text: In the evening, the Biergarten terrace facing the illuminated castle is one of the town's finest spots. The castle still belongs to the Hohenzollern family, who maintain partial residence.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Seen from the air, Schloss Clemenswerth resembles a star set in the forest. Eight pavilions arranged in an octagon around the central pavilion: it's the last preserved Parforce-Sternanlage in Europe. Designed by Johann Conrad Schlaun, built between 1737 and 1747 for Prince-Bishop Clemens August.

The silence of the Klostergarten, broken only by birdsong, contrasts with the baroque pomp of the Festsaal. The painted chapels and the hunting pavilion with its trophies are worth the visit. Count on 75 minutes with the guided tour.

activities.text: Eight pavilions in a star around a central castle, eight lime-tree avenues, a pond with five islands: walk through the park to grasp the baroque geometry at human height. The Marstall, at the west end, houses an exhibition on court life.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: Take the cable car (Seilbahn) up to Schloss Burg rather than climbing on foot. This 12th-century fortress, the largest restored burg in North Rhine-Westphalia, towers 110 meters above the Wupper. The Bergisches Museum traces the lives of the Counts of Berg. Admission €10, closed Mondays.

In the lanes of Gräfrath's old town, the scent of bakeries mingles with slate-grey walls. This historic Solingen quarter clusters half-timbered houses and cobbled courtyards around the Klosterkirche. Less touristy than Freudenberg, more authentic too. Park at Gräfrather Marktplatz.

activities.text: If weather permits, hike the Klingenpfad, a 7-km 'blade trail' linking Gräfrath to Schloss Burg through the forest. Solingen has been the city of knives since the Middle Ages. The path passes old forges and grinding mills.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: Experience the breathtaking Lüneburg Heath, one of Germany's most enchanting natural landscapes. This vast expanse of purple heather moorland, dotted with ancient juniper bushes and grazing Heidschnucken sheep, creates a magical atmosphere especially during the August-September flowering season. The heath's peaceful beauty has inspired poets and painters for centuries.

Get your adrenaline pumping at Heide-Park Soltau, home to the largest wooden roller coaster in the world. This premier theme park combines heart-stopping rides with themed lands, live entertainment, and family attractions spread across a beautifully landscaped setting. Thrill-seekers and families alike find endless excitement in Germany's second-largest amusement park.

activities.text: Marvel at exotic species from around the globe at Walsrode Bird Park, one of the world's largest bird parks. Over 4,000 birds from 650 species inhabit this spectacular 24-hectare garden featuring stunning aviaries, tropical houses, and interactive feeding experiences. From tiny hummingbirds to majestic eagles, the diversity of birdlife creates a colorful paradise.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: The winemakers simply call it the Weininsel - the wine island. Sommerach and Nordheim occupy a meander of the Altmain across about 750 hectares of vineyards. The Pfarrkirche St. Eucherius, whose late Gothic silhouette has dominated the village since the 16th century, is one of the rare sanctuaries in Europe dedicated to this saint.

Sommerach's Pfarrkirche, whose steep tower structures the village skyline, took its current form in 1560, with the choir enlarged in 1756-1757. The Kirchplatz surrounding it is the village's natural center - benches, fountain, views across the vineyards.

activities.text: Step through the door of the InfoVinothek on the Kirchplatz for a guided tasting of the Weininsel's wines. The Winzerkeller Sommerach, founded in 1901, is Franconia's oldest wine cooperative. On Friday evenings in season, the terrace atmosphere often surpasses that of big-city bars.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: The Bärenhöhle at Sonnenbühl takes its name from the cave bear skeletons discovered inside. Limestone formations sculpt spectacular shapes in this Swabian Alb cave. Guided tour about 30 minutes.
The Swabian Alb plateau around Sonnenbühl offers easy hikes with panoramas over valleys below. The UNESCO-recognized Swabian Alb Biosphere Reserve begins here.

activities.text: Walk the 'Hochgehträumt' trail linking Lichtenstein Castle to the Nebelhöhle over 10.9 km, with viewpoints overlooking the Albtrauf escarpment. Easy difficulty, moderate elevation gain.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Lichtenstein Castle stands on a rocky spur at 817 meters altitude, above the Echaz valley in the Swabian Jura. In 1826, writer Wilhelm Hauff published a historical novel featuring the medieval castle of Lichtenstein as its setting. The novel was a resounding success, and Count Wilhelm von Urach, cousin of the King of Württemberg, was so enthused he purchased the site and had the current neo-Gothic castle built between 1840 and 1842—a few years before Ludwig II of Bavaria did the same at Neuschwanstein. The interior houses a collection of medieval weapons and wall frescoes. From the ramparts, the view over the valley and Swabian Jura plateaus is remarkable.

Hohenzollern Castle perches at 855 meters atop Mount Zoller, an isolated outlying hill above the Swabian plain. It's the ancestral seat of the Hohenzollern family, from which Prussia's kings and Germany's emperors descended. The first fortress was erected in the 11th century; destroyed in 1423 after a ten-month siege, rebuilt from 1454 to 1461. The current castle, third version, was commissioned in 1846 by King Frederick William IV of Prussia and designed by architect Friedrich August Stüler in neo-Gothic style. Among the collection's pieces: the Prussian royal crown, a letter from President George Washington thanking Baron von Steuben for his role in the American War of Independence, and personal effects of Frederick the Great. On clear days, views from the ramparts extend to the Black Forest.

activities.text: Sunset viewpoint from the overlooks below Hohenzollern Castle parking lot. The fortress silhouette cutting against the sunset over the Swabian hills is one of Baden-Württemberg's most photographed panoramas.

days_plan.itin_title: Germany Road Trip: Castles, Medieval Towns & Alpine Legends

visits.text: Stand in awe before the Speyer Cathedral (Speyer Dom), a monumental Romanesque masterpiece that served as the principal church of the Holy Roman Empire. This UNESCO World Heritage site, one of the largest Romanesque churches in the world, houses the crypts of eight German emperors and kings beneath its soaring vaulted ceilings. The cathedral's twin towers and red sandstone facade have dominated the Rhine plain for nearly a thousand years.

Descend into the imperial crypt to pay respects at the final resting place of medieval rulers who once shaped the destiny of Europe. The atmospheric underground chambers feature simple yet powerful stone sarcophagi that connect visitors directly to the empire's medieval glory. An excellent audio guide available in multiple languages brings the cathedral's fascinating history vividly to life.

Visit the museum across the cathedral square to deepen your understanding of Speyer's pivotal role in European history. The collection traces the city's development from Roman times through its prominence in the Holy Roman Empire. In summer, the lively Brezelfest fills the streets with carnival rides and traditional festival tents, while December brings a charming small-scale Christmas market.

activities.text: Stroll along the historic Maximilianstrasse, Germany's oldest and longest pedestrian street

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: The Augustinian abbey of St. Märgen, founded in 1118, houses a museum of folk traditions: wood carvings, Fasnet masks (the local Alemannic carnival). This carnival is the closest thing the Black Forest has to an openly pre-Christian procession—animal-headed masks, demon costumes. The baroque church is worth a quick look. Free access to the exterior, museum admission charged.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Experience Stade, a jewel among Germany's Hanseatic cities boasting remarkable maritime charm on the banks of the Schwinge River. This 1,000-year-old trading port features a beautifully preserved old town with half-timbered houses, historic merchant buildings, and the iconic wooden harbor crane. The atmospheric harbor quarter and winding lanes transport visitors back to the glory days of Hanseatic trade.

activities.text: Cycle along the North Sea Cycle Path that winds along the embankments between grazing sheep and the vast coastal landscape. This scenic route offers peaceful riding through traditional fishing villages, past historic lighthouses, and along dikes with panoramic views of the North Sea and Elbe estuary.

Explore Stade's maritime heritage at the harbor where traditional sailing vessels still moor alongside the historic quays. The town's museum in the Swedish Storage House tells the story of centuries of trade, fishing, and seafaring that shaped this Hanseatic gem.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands

visits.text: In 1539, an alchemist was found in pieces in his room after an explosion. Room 5 of the Gasthaus zum Löwen still exists—the inn has been documented since 1407. The fresco on the façade shows Mephistopheles snapping Faust's neck. Take a moment to admire the cracks splitting through the medieval center, the result of a failed geothermal drilling project in 2007: locals say the devil came back to claim what was owed.

The climb smells of resin and ripening Gutedel grapes—the Schlossberg vineyards cling to the hillside all the way up to the ruins. Twenty minutes on foot from the market square to reach the remnants of the castle of the Lords of Staufen, who died out in 1602. On clear days, the view stretches as far as the Vosges. Don't expect reconstructions: just dry stones, turtledoves, and a panorama you'll have mostly to yourself.

activities.text: If the dates align, the Mephisto Tour is the highlight of the stay: a theatrical tour through Staufen's alleyways, performed by local actors. Pricing and dates must be verified directly on stadtgeschichten-staufen.de before departure—sessions aren't permanent. Allow 90 minutes. October, with low fog rolling over the rooftops, is the perfect time for this format.

days_plan.itin_title: Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

places.name: Steinhausen An Der Rottum

visits.text: Steinhausen claims the title of 'world's most beautiful village church.' The Wallfahrtskirche, built between 1728 and 1733 by Dominikus Zimmermann (the same architect as Bavaria's Wieskirche), concentrates a rare density of decoration into an oval space. Johann Baptist Zimmermann's frescoes cover the entire ceiling.

The village itself comprises just a handful of houses around the church. The contrast between the place's modesty and the interior's exuberance creates the entire effect. Free admission, donation box available.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

map_keywords: Steinhausen an der Rottum Pilgrimage Church

visits.text: Discover Stendal, proud Roland town of the Altmark, whose monumental statue in the market square symbolizes medieval civic liberties. This Hanseatic city prosperous in the Middle Ages preserves a remarkably homogeneous Gothic brick architectural heritage. Narrow alleyways lead to picturesque squares and hidden treasures revealing the historical importance of this former merchant town.

Marvel at St. Mary's Church which majestically towers above the town. This Gothic brick hall church impresses with its soaring architecture and refined artistic details. St. Jacobi Church, another Gothic jewel, completes this exceptional religious heritage with its stellar vaults and medieval stained glass windows.

Pass through the monumental Uenglingen and Tangermünde gates, impressive witnesses to the town's former fortifications. These Gothic brick gates, among the finest in northern Germany, tell of Stendal's strategic importance in medieval history. The Powder Tower and Renaissance Town Hall complete this remarkable architectural panorama.

activities.text: Visit the Altmark Museum tracing the history of this historic region

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Explore Stolberg, a picturesque town in the southern Harz whose medieval center is miraculously preserved. Historic half-timbered houses lining the steep alleyways create a setting from another time at the foot of the castle overlooking the valley. Stolberg Castle, residence of the eponymous counts, and St. Martin's Church constitute the emblematic symbols of this romantic town.

Discover the birthplace of Thomas Müntzer, radical theologian and leader of the Peasants' War born here in 1489. A statue commemorates this controversial figure who defied the established order in the name of social justice. His revolutionary legacy makes Stolberg an important memorial site for the history of social movements in Europe.

Climb the 200 steps of Joseph's Cross, the world's largest iron double cross erected on the Großer Auerberg. This impressive 38-meter-high metal structure, built in 1896, offers a magnificent view of the Harz massif from its observation platform. The 360-degree panorama rewards the climbing effort with a grandiose natural spectacle.

activities.text: Climb to Joseph's Cross for the panorama over the southern Harz

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: The old town of Stralsund has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2002, jointly with Wismar. It has preserved its medieval urban plan intact, with narrow streets, six large Gothic brick churches, and merchants' houses on parcels typical of the Hanseatic era. Stralsund was one of the most powerful cities of the Hanseatic League in the 14th and 15th centuries, before becoming a Swedish administrative center in the 17th and 18th centuries—Swedish occupation lasted until 1815 and left traces in the architecture and coat of arms.

The Alter Markt, the old market square, is dominated by the Gothic Rathaus, whose origins date back to around 1250. Built in four wings forming a 30-by-60-meter rectangle, it's one of the most remarkable Gothic secular buildings in northern Germany. Its northern façade, adorned with the coats of arms of Hanseatic cities—Stralsund, Hamburg, Lübeck, Rostock, Greifswald—recalls the commercial power of the Hanse. Just beside it, St. Nicholas Church, consecrated in 1276, is the city's oldest church: its baroque lantern spire, added in 1667 after a fire, and its medieval astronomical clock make it an essential stop.

The Ozeaneum, opened in 2008 in Stralsund's harbor, is Germany's largest maritime museum. Its giant aquariums recreate the ecosystems of the North Sea and the Baltic—the largest tank holds millions of liters of seawater. Outside in the harbor, the Gorch Fock I, a training ship launched in 1933, is now a museum vessel moored at the quay.

activities.text: Evening walk in Stralsund harbor: the quays overlook the Strelasund, an arm of the Baltic that separates the city from the island of Rügen. The view across the water with the Gothic spires in the background is characteristic of Pomerania's maritime atmosphere. In summer, short cruises to Rügen or Hiddensee depart from the harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Germany Road Trip: From Dresden to the Baltic Sea
German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: The entire town sits on a limestone spur so steep the houses look ready to slide. Sulzbach-Rosenberg mixes half-timbering, a Gothic Rathaus and a Schlossanlage whose foundations go back to the 8th century. It's the heart of the Bayerische Eisenstraße, the old iron road.
The Schloss Sulzbach dominates the rock and offers a panorama over the Oberpfälzien Jura countryside. The Romanesque Bergfried is one of Bavaria's oldest. Exterior visit free.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Wander through Tangermünde, considered one of the most beautiful small towns in Germany. This picturesque town at the confluence of the Elbe and Tanger rivers captivates with its cobblestone streets lined with historic half-timbered houses and impressive red brick buildings. The preserved medieval atmosphere instantly transports you several centuries back in time.

Contemplate the imposing medieval Tangermünde Castle, former imperial residence of Charles IV in the 14th century. This fortress overlooking the Elbe offers spectacular views of the town and river banks. The massive ramparts and watchtowers testify to the strategic importance of this town in the history of the Holy Roman Empire.

Admire the Gothic brick Town Hall, an architectural gem of northern Germany with its characteristic stepped gables. St. Stephen's Church, an impressive 14th-century brick building, dominates the town's skyline with its slender bell tower. The Owl Tower (Eulenturm) is among the remarkably preserved remains of the medieval fortifications.

activities.text: Walk along the medieval ramparts with panoramic views over the Elbe
Discover the statue of Grete Minde, a legendary figure of the town

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: Westphalians call it 'the balcony of Münsterland': perched on the Teutoburger Wald, Tecklenburg offers a clear panorama over the plain. The medieval town is compact, all cobbled lanes, 16th-18th century half-timbered houses and stiegen (staircases) carved into the rock.

Nestled in the ruins of Burg Tecklenburg, founded in the 12th century, sits Germany's largest Freilichtbühne: more than 2,300 seats for summer musicals. The acoustics of the old ivy-covered walls are remarkable.

The uneven cobblestones and half-timbered facades of the Altstadt lead to the Puppenmuseum and the Wasserschloss Haus Marck, a moated manor hidden in the woods below. This is where the first negotiations of the Peace of Westphalia took place in 1643.

activities.text: Book a seat for a musical at the Freilichtbühne if you're there in summer (June-September). Performances are in the evening, 1,800 seats are covered. Tickets at freilichtspiele-tecklenburg.de.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles
Westphalia and the Weser Valley — Münster to Kassel in 9 Days

visits.text: The former Kloster Tegernsee now houses a grammar school, a restaurant, and the Wittelsbachs' ducal brewery. The mix is improbable but works. The Bräustüberl serves beer brewed on-site, within the walls of an abbey founded in the 8th century.
The Pfarrkirche St. Quirinus merits a stop: baroque interior, and the Wittelsbach crypt where Sissi's mother, Duchess Ludovika, rests. Not visitable, but mentioned on a panel at the entrance.

The lake shimmers blue-green beneath the Wallberg mountains. Six kilometers long, a kilometer and a half wide, and water that's become among Europe's cleanest thanks to efforts by the lakeside communes.

activities.text: Circle the Tegernsee by boat: shuttles link Tegernsee, Rottach-Egern, Bad Wiessee, and Gmund. An hour's cruise, cappuccino on deck, views of the peaks. Departures every hour in season.

Founded in the 8th century, the Benedictine monastery was secularized in 1803 then converted into a royal residence. The Wittelsbachs' brewery still operates in the north wing. Order a Tegernseer Spezial and a Brezn at the Bräustüberl—it's a local ritual.

days_plan.itin_title: Bavaria's Fairy-Tale Route: Castles, Alpine Lakes & Medieval Towns
Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: The smell of hops floats over Tettnang in summer. The small town is one of Europe's great hop terroirs: Tettnanger Hopfen goes into the composition of beers worldwide. The Hopfenmuseum explains everything, from cultivation to harvest.

Less known than the lake castles, the Neues Schloss of Tettnang is visited in peace. Baroque residence of the Counts of Montfort, 18th century, state rooms with stucco and frescoes. Open April to October.

activities.text: Visit the Hopfenmuseum to understand why this small town feeds breweries around the planet. Models, tools, tasting possible. In September, the Hopfenernte (harvest) transforms the surrounding fields into a green, fragrant spectacle.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: Ascend to the Hexentanzplatz, the legendary witches' dance floor, accessible by a panoramic cable car soaring over the Bode Valley. This rocky plateau at 454 meters elevation is the scene of numerous legends surrounding the Harz witches who allegedly gather here every Walpurgis Night. The plunging view over the wild Bode Gorge and surrounding forests creates a panorama of striking beauty.

Climb the Roßtrappe, a 400-meter granite rock offering a fantastic view of the Harz Mountains. According to legend, the horseshoe-shaped imprint visible in the rock comes from Princess Brunhilde's horse, which leapt across the gorge to escape the knight Bodo. The latter, having fallen into the abyss, allegedly gave his name to the Bode River that winds 200 meters below.

Hike on the Harzer Hexenstieg, the famous hiking trail following the Bode River through a spectacular gorge. The Devil's Bridge spans the tumultuous waters in an enchanted forest setting worthy of the Brothers Grimm fairy tales. St. Peter's Church and Goethe's Rock, which the writer visited during his travels in the Harz, complete this immersion in a German romantic landscape.

activities.text: Hike along the Bode on the Hexenstieg trail through the wild gorge

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: In the morning, junipers rise from the mist in dark clumps across this bowl-shaped hollow — a scene that draws nature photographers from across Germany. Totengrund owes its brooding name to old local legends whose meaning no one quite agrees on. What is certain: the hollow, likely formed at the end of the last ice age, looks unlike anything else in the region.

This 30-hectare hollow was bought in 1906 by Pastor Wilhelm Bode using donor funds — a founding gesture that gave rise to Germany's first major nature reserve, established in 1921. A memorial stone to investor Andreas Thomsen marks the viewpoint at the rim of the hollow.

activities.text: Walk the loop around Totengrund on the marked paths that trace the rim of the hollow — descending into the bowl itself is prohibited to protect the vegetation. The full circuit takes about 45 minutes. Keep an eye out for snipe and northern lapwings in the wetland areas.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Not touristy in the facade-seaside sense, but authentically Baltic: Travemünde still lives with its ferries to Helsinki and Stockholm, its fishermen, and its 19th-century resort villas. The waterfront combines an active port, a pedestrian promenade, and the museum sailing ship Passat (1911) moored at the quay, accessible for visits.

The Leuchtturm Travemünde, commissioned in 1827 and deactivated in 1972 when a hotel obscured it, is today a maritime museum 31 m tall. Its 142 steps lead to an observation gallery with plunging views over the Baltic and the ferries maneuvering in the channel. It's the second-oldest lighthouse on the German Baltic coast after the one at Kap Arkona.

activities.text: Rent a Strandkorb—the woven wicker beach chair, symbol of the Baltic—and watch the ferries on the Helsinki-Travemünde line pass a few hundred meters from shore. These orange-hulled Finnlines vessels are impossible to miss. The white sand beach is pleasant even off-season; parking near Strandbahnhof remains affordable.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen

visits.text: En route to Triberg, morning stop at Unteruhldingen for the Pfahlbaumuseum (Stilt House Museum), Germany's oldest open-air museum, founded in 1922. Twenty-three Neolithic and Bronze Age dwellings (from 4000 to 850 BC) have been reconstructed on stilts above Lake Constance, based on underwater excavations. The site is part of the 111 prehistoric pile dwelling sites around the Alps, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011. Walkways cross the lake village; the reconstructed houses contain life scenes with mannequins, tools, and period domestic animals.

The Triberg Waterfalls are Germany's highest waterfalls outside the Alpine zone, with a total height of 163 meters across seven distinct tiers. The Gutach River cascades over granite rocks amid a conifer forest. Three marked trails allow the ascent (between 45 minutes and 1.5 hours). The site is illuminated every evening until 10pm.

The Schwarzwaldmuseum in Triberg, built in 1873, presents Black Forest traditional life across 1,600 m²: cuckoo clock manufacturing, regional costumes (including the Bollenhut with red pompoms for married women or black for unmarried), mining life, and one of Europe's largest collections of barrel organs. Triberg has claimed its position as the historic capital of Black Forest clockmaking since the 18th century—the first mechanical cuckoo clocks were manufactured here.

activities.text: Hike on one of the marked trails around the Triberg Waterfalls. The cascade path (Kaskadenweg, ~45 min) follows the successive tiers from bottom to top. Squirrels and uncommon forest bird species are frequently observed along the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns
Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Black Forest & Alpine Lakes
Occult Black Forest - 7 days on Faust's trail

visits.text: Begin with the Porta Nigra, built from 169-170 AD as the northern gate of the Roman city Augusta Treverorum. Its 7,200 sandstone blocks, weighing up to 6 tons each, are assembled without mortar, held by iron clamps sealed with lead. It owes its name to the dark color the stone has taken on over the centuries. If it survived, it's thanks to the hermit Simeon who settled there in the 11th century: Bishop Poppo transformed the gate into a church in his honor in 1040, thus sparing it from demolition. Napoleon had the medieval additions removed during his visit in 1804.

Head to the Basilica of Constantine (Konstantinbasilika), the former imperial throne room—the largest intact building from antiquity: 67 meters long, 27 wide, 33 high. Trier was one of the largest cities in the Roman Empire, called the "Second Rome" in the late 3rd century. Constantine the Great resided here for six years in the early 4th century. The basilica's acoustics, with a 7-second echo, make it a concert venue today.

Continue to St. Peter's Cathedral, Germany's oldest, built in the 4th century on the site of a palace from Constantine's era. The cathedral-Liebfrauenkirche ensemble has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986. The Liebfrauenkirche, completed around 1260, is the first entirely Gothic church in Germany.

Visit the Imperial Baths (Kaiserthermen), begun under Constantine in the early 4th century. They were meant to be the largest baths in the Roman Empire but were never completed—the building was converted into military barracks, then used as a quarry. Descend into the underground passages where slaves fed the hypocausts (underfloor heating systems).

Trier's amphitheater, built in the 2nd century, could accommodate 20,000 spectators—one of the best-preserved Roman amphitheaters north of the Alps. Descend into the underground cellars where gladiators and animals awaited their arena entrance.

Finish with a walk along the Moselle riverbanks and through the old town. Karl Marx was born in Trier in 1818—his birthplace is now a museum. To leave the city, Frankfurt is about 2 hours by train, Luxembourg 50 kilometers away.

activities.text: The Moselle Valley around Trier is one of Germany's most renowned wine regions, specializing in Riesling grown on steep slate slopes. Several estates offer tastings in the surrounding villages.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Germany Road Trip: Castles, Forests & Medieval Towns

visits.text: Fall in love with Tübingen, an enchanting university town that many consider Germany's most beautiful city. The charming old town features a magnificent castle, cobblestone streets lined with half-timbered houses and a picturesque waterfront along the Neckar River. The centuries-old university gives the town a youthful, intellectual energy that fills the winding lanes and historic squares.

Climb to Hohentübingen Castle, perched on a ridge overlooking the town, where university students attend classes in buildings that have educated scholars for over 500 years. The castle courtyard offers panoramic views over the red-tiled roofs and church spires of the old town below. The surrounding vineyards produce local wines that feature on menus throughout the town.

Experience a traditional Stocherkahn boat ride, Tübingen's version of Venetian gondolas, where flat-bottomed punts glide along the Neckar past willow-draped banks. The annual Stocherkahnrennen boat race is a beloved local tradition featuring costumed students and much hilarity. The Holzmarkt square and surrounding streets come alive on summer evenings with locals enjoying the outdoor café culture.

activities.text: Explore the old town's atmospheric squares and imagine yourself in a Harry Potter film set

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Germany Explorer: Black Forest Castles and Baden-Baden Spas

visits.text: Tüchersfeld resembles an urban planning error: half-timbered houses wedged between dolomite needles that tower twenty meters above them. The limestone towers are former coral reefs from the age of dinosaurs. The village is an ortsteil of Pottenstein and remains inhabited, which heightens the strangeness - not an open-air museum, a real life among the stones.

The Fränkische-Schweiz-Museum in the former Judenhof - a complex of half-timbered buildings financed in 1750 by Jews from Bamberg - presents across 43 rooms the archaeology, geology, and traditions of Franconian Switzerland. The interior setting is as remarkable as the content.

activities.text: Climb to the Fahnensteig in 10 minutes from the village parking: the viewpoint offers a vertical plunge onto Tüchersfeld and its rock needles. The trail continues to the Kühloch (a natural cave) and the Zeckenstein - count one hour round-trip for the whole route.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Überlingen closes the journey on the shores of Lake Constance. The medieval old town slopes gently toward the lake. The Münster St. Nikolaus houses a limewood altarpiece carved by Jörg Zürn in 1613, considered among the finest late Gothic works in Germany.
Überlingen's Stadtgärten, terraced gardens facing the lake, hosted the 2021 Landesgartenschau. The cactus collections and rampart gardens merit a stroll. Modest admission fee.
The Städtisches Museum, housed in the 1462 Reichlin-von-Meldegg-Haus, presents a Renaissance ballroom with painted ceiling and regional art collections. The building itself is a monument.

activities.text: Finish with a swim in Lake Constance from the Therme Überlingen or the public beaches below the promenade. The view across the lake to the Swiss Alps provides the perfect endpoint for the road trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days
Lake Constance Loop: 6 Days Around the Bodensee

visits.text: Birnau Basilica overlooks Lake Constance from a vine-planted hillside. Completed in 1750, it concentrates the finest of Swabian rococo: Joseph Anton Feuchtmayer's stuccowork and Gottfried Bernhard Göz's frescoes. The Honigschlecker, a putto licking honey, is the most photographed figure of the entire Baroque Route.
From Birnau's forecourt, the view plunges to Lake Constance and the Swiss Alps beyond. The vineyard terraces around the church produce wine sold at the monastery shop. Free admission.

activities.text: The Pfahlbauten at Uhldingen, a reconstructed lake-dwelling village with UNESCO status, is a few minutes' drive away. The Bronze Age stilt houses offer family-friendly visits.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: Kochberg Castle is a moated castle (Wasserschloss) built around 1600 in Renaissance style, surrounded by a moat. It's known worldwide for its connection to Goethe and Charlotte von Stein. The von Stein family acquired the estate in 1733. Goethe was a regular guest of Charlotte von Stein from 1775 to 1788 - it was at Kochberg that he met Friedrich Schiller for the first time on September 7, 1788, before inviting him to Jena. He wrote his visit dates in ink on the castle's secretary desk, still visible today. More than 1,700 letters from Goethe to Charlotte von Stein testify to this thirteen-year relationship. The museum reconstructs the period's apartments with original furniture, portraits, and manuscript documents.

The six-hectare landscape park was transformed by Carl von Stein (Charlotte's son) between 1800 and 1840, from a Baroque garden into a Romantic English park. Under imposing old trees, winding paths lead to a canvas hut, a tower ruin, a grotto with burial site, a bathing lake, and the flower garden with its floral staircase.

The Liebhabertheater is a 75-seat private theater built around 1800 by Carl von Stein after the model of Weimar's court theater. It's a unique jewel worldwide: the classical columned portico, the hall with its hand-marbled wallpapers and floral friezes, the stage and pit are fully restored. It belongs to the «European Route of Historic Theatres» and hosts every weekend from May to October operas, chamber concerts, and theatrical performances.

activities.text: Performance at Kochberg's Liebhabertheater: Baroque opera or chamber concert in this 75-seat theater, in the authentic atmosphere of the 18th century. Productions are conceived specifically for this space and employ historically informed performance practices.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

visits.text: 768 spiral steps wind to the top of Ulm Minster, Germany's tallest church spire at 161.53 m. The viewing platform at 143 m commands views stretching to the Alps on clear days. Founded as a burgher church in 1377 and converted to Protestant worship in 1530, it survived the 1944 bombings unscathed. Church admission free, tower climb ticketed.

The Fischerviertel, the old fishermen's and tanners' quarter, snakes along the Blau just before it meets the Danube. Half-timbered houses lean at improbable angles over cobbled lanes, and the Schiefes Haus ranks among the world's most tilted buildings. Five-minute walk from the Minster.

Ulm's Stadtbibliothek, a glass pyramid designed by Gottfried Böhm, deliberately contrasts with the Gothic Minster it adjoins. Bold contemporary architecture in a city rebuilt after wartime destruction. Free admission.

activities.text: Sample Maultaschen at a city-center Gaststätte: these hefty Swabian dumplings filled with meat and spinach are served in broth or pan-fried. Legend has it monks invented them to hide meat during Lent.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: The terrace of the Vogelsburg: beneath your feet, the Main River forms its famous Schleife - the only loop along the river's 527 kilometers. This viewpoint belongs to a 10th-century former monastery-restaurant, nestled in the vineyards a few hundred meters above the river. The panorama embraces the vineyards of Escherndorf, Nordheim, and on the horizon, the Steigerwald.

Volkach's Altstadt concentrates the Echterhof, the old Zehnthof, and the Schelfenhaus around a Marktplatz with fountain and 16th-century Rathaus. The Wallfahrtskirche Maria im Weingarten possesses an altarpiece by Tilman Riemenschneider - if time is limited, this detail alone justifies entry.

activities.text: Tasting Silvaner facing the Mainschleife on the Vogelsburg terrace is one of Franconia's rituals. The restaurant charges reasonable prices for such a view. Otherwise, head down to Astheim and rent a canoe at the river station - paddling up the loop is the best way to understand why the river stopped there.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: Waren is the gateway to Müritz National Park. The Müritzeum, an interactive museum on lakes and forests, features a freshwater aquarium where a school of whitefish circles across two floors. Immersive effect guaranteed. Lake Müritz itself (117 km²) is Germany's second-largest.

activities.text: In autumn, head to Rederangsee at dusk: up to 10,000 common cranes gather before migration. Guided observation by national park rangers. In summer, canoe trips on the canals between lakes.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: On the Warnemünde side, the morning beach is empty before 9am. The Alter Strom lines up fishermen's houses converted to fish restaurants. Buy Räucherfisch—smoked fish—straight from the boat for a few euros.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

places.name: Weiden In Der Oberpfalz

visits.text: Weiden isn't the most picturesque town in the Palatinate, but its pedestrian center around the Oberer Markt has character. Good base for restocking, finding a decent restaurant and fueling up before the rural stages that follow.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

map_keywords: Weiden in der Oberpfalz old town

visits.text: Weilburg Palace occupies half the old town, set on a rocky spur above the Lahn. The baroque residence of the Nassau-Weilburg dynasty preserves its terraced gardens with orangery, among Hesse's finest. The Renaissance Hochschloss (1530-1572) is one of the state's best preserved.

Inaugurated in 1847, Weilburg's ship tunnel is Germany's oldest and longest still-navigable tunnel: 195 meters beneath the Mühlberg. Duke Adolph of Nassau had it bored to bypass the Lahnschleife rapids. Ten years later, the railway made it obsolete.

activities.text: Rent a canoe to pass through the ship tunnel by river. The Lahn flows gently through the vaulted tunnel, lit by a few bulbs. The experience lasts five minutes but stamps the memory. Rental available at Weilburger Bootsanleger, at the tunnel's north entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

places.name: Weimar

visits.text: Tiefurt Palace is a small summer residence on the banks of the Ilm east of Weimar. Originally a modest manor from the mid-16th century enlarged in 1765, it became Duchess Anna Amalia's favorite retreat from 1781 until her death in 1807. Despite its seven rooms per floor, it hosted literary and musical soirées bringing together Goethe, Wieland, and Corona Schröter, making Tiefurt a pastoral «Museion». The music room and the duchess's apartments are restored to their late 18th-century state with original furniture. Tiefurt is part of the UNESCO site «Classical Weimar».

The Bauhaus was founded in Weimar on April 12, 1919, by architect Walter Gropius, who merged the city's Academy of Fine Arts and School of Applied Arts. His manifesto proclaimed that «the ultimate goal of all creative activity is building». Among the recruited masters: Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, László Moholy-Nagy, and Oskar Schlemmer. The school left Weimar in 1925 under pressure from a conservative regional government and moved to Dessau, before being closed by the Nazis in 1933. The Bauhaus Museum Weimar, opened in 2019 for the centenary, holds the world's oldest Bauhaus collection - started by Gropius himself - with 13,000 objects spread across three floors. The Bauhaus sites in Weimar have been UNESCO World Heritage Sites since 1996.

Goethe's House at Frauenplan is the residence the poet occupied for 50 years, from 1782 to his death in 1832. It is preserved as he left it: his study with the mineral collection carefully arranged on shelves, his 5,000 volumes, the bed in which he died. It's the world's most important museum dedicated to Goethe. The house is part of the UNESCO site «Classical Weimar» inscribed in 1998.

Schiller's House is the dwelling the poet occupied during his last three years of life, from 1802 to his death in 1805. It's here that «The Bride of Messina» and «William Tell» were written. The study is preserved with the inclined desk at which Schiller worked standing, his cane, the manuscripts. The modest rooms contrast with the grandeur of the works born there.

The Duchess Anna Amalia Library is housed in the Renaissance «Green Palace» (1562-1565) converted into a library in 1761. Goethe was its director from 1797 to 1832 and doubled the holdings to reach 80,000 volumes. The three-story oval Rococo hall, in wood, is one of Germany's most photographed interiors. On September 2, 2004, a fire destroyed 50,000 volumes and damaged 118,000 - a national tragedy. After restoration, the library reopened in 2007. It preserves the world's largest collection of Goethe's Faust.

The House of the Weimar Republic (Haus der Weimarer Republik), opened in 2019, traces the history of Germany's first democracy. In 1919, the National Constituent Assembly met in Weimar's German National Theater - Berlin was deemed too dangerous amid revolution - and adopted the Constitution of August 11, 1919, instituting universal suffrage and social rights pioneering for the era. The permanent exhibition illuminates the birth, crises, and fall of this Republic.

activities.text: Stroll through Tiefurt's English landscape park along the Ilm River. Open meadows, century-old trees, and small monuments punctuate the main avenue: an artificial grotto, a temple of the muses, a Japanese-style wooden bridge. The park was designed in the spirit of 18th-century «natural» English gardens.

Bauhaus walking tour through Weimar's streets to discover buildings linked to the school: the Haus am Horn (1923), a Bauhaus housing prototype built for an exhibition, and Henry van de Velde's buildings where the school had its first workshops. Specialized guides offer commented tours.

days_plan.itin_title: Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders
Thuringia Road Trip: Cultural Heritage & Natural Wonders

map_keywords: Goethe Gartenhaus Park an der Ilm Weimar

visits.text: With 6,666 pipes, the Gabler organ in Weingarten Basilica ranks among Germany's largest baroque instruments. Built in 1750, it resonates through a 102-meter-long nave locals call the 'Swabian St. Peter's.' The ceiling frescoes are by Cosmas Damian Asam.

Every year on the Friday after Ascension, Weingarten's Blutritt gathers horsemen from across Swabia for an equestrian procession honoring a relic of Christ's Blood. It's Europe's largest mounted procession.

activities.text: Weingarten is 3 km from Ravensburg. The two towns complement each other well in a single day. The Alamannenmuseum, dedicated to the Alemanni of the early Middle Ages, displays remarkable funerary finds.

days_plan.itin_title: Castles and Baroque Splendor of Swabia in 8 Days

visits.text: The Weißenstädter See, 50 hectares of water ringed by forest, surprises with its calm. No motors allowed: all you hear is the windsurfers and grebes. The 4-km paved loop around the lake works equally well for strollers and rollerblades.

The Felsenkeller carved into the granite near the cemetery create a fantastical film set. Overgrown with ivy and moss, these former cellars stored local beer. Photographers, come at dusk: the light filtering through the vaults is striking.

activities.text: If the sun's out, lap the lake by SUP or pedal boat. The Siebenquell Therme, right on the shore, offers hot baths with views of the Schneeberg. Day pass around €20.

days_plan.itin_title: Fichtelgebirge and Oberpfalz: Granite, Beer and Castles

visits.text: Weißwasser was long a glass capital, and the Glasmuseum Weißwasser tells this industrial and local story. You follow the glassmaking process and the daily lives of the glassworkers who built the town. The nearby Waldeisenbahn once transported raw materials to the glassworks, hence the tight connection between the two. Plan an hour, and combine the visit with a ride on the train for coherence.

activities.text: Board the Waldeisenbahn Muskau, the last forest railway of its kind preserved in Germany, with its tiny 600-millimeter gauge. Steam and diesel locomotives from the 1950s take you toward Kromlau or Bad Muskau, departing from Weißwasser's Teichstraße station. It runs mainly April through October, on weekends and public holidays. Buy your ticket at the station or directly from the conductor.

If you're traveling with children, Tierpark Weißwasser breaks the museum pace without breaking the budget. This small zoo stays human-scale, ideal for an hour or two between stops. It complements the town's glass-and-train day nicely. Check the opening hours on-site before going—they vary by season.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Lusatia: Bad Muskau, Sorbian Country & Bautzen in 6 Days

visits.text: Contemplate the impressive half-timbered Town Hall of Wernigerode, one of the most photographed in Germany with its pointed turrets and medieval sculptures. This pearl of the Harz captivates with its ensemble of perfectly preserved half-timbered houses, including the town's smallest house that attracts the curious. The neo-Gothic castle enthroned on its hill offers a fairytale silhouette overlooking the colorful rooftops of the old town.

Visit Wernigerode Castle, residence of the Counts of Stolberg-Wernigerode transformed into a museum tracing 19th-century aristocratic life. The sumptuous apartments and decorative art collections testify to the refinement of German nobility. The panoramic view from the terraces embraces the Harz forests and surrounding peaks.

Discover the Steinerne Renne, a spectacular gorge of the Holtemme River where water cascades between enormous moss-covered granite boulders. This natural wonder at the gates of Wernigerode offers a refreshing hike through preserved primeval forest. At the Baumkuchenhaus, this traditional spit-roasted cake perpetuates a unique pastry-making tradition.

activities.text: Excursion to the Schierke district, home of the Schierker Feuerstein herbal liqueur
Visit Pullman City Harz theme park in Hasselfelde, Germany's largest western town

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends
Phantom Harz - 7 Days from Hamelin to the Brocken

visits.text: Built between 1906 and 1908 on an artificial mound four metres above the flats, the Leuchtturm Westerheversand stands 40 metres tall and its beam carries 39 kilometres — as far as Helgoland on a clear day. Since the 1970s it has appeared on Jever Beer bottles, earning it a fame its solitary setting would never suggest.

Sheep graze around the red-and-white tower in complete indifference — an absurd and entirely real scene. The Salzwiesen salt marshes at the foot of the lighthouse are a protected biotope within the Schleswig-Holsteinisches Wattenmeer National Park.

activities.text: Climb the 157 steps to survey the Eiderstedt peninsula from the platform at 37 metres: on a clear day you can count twenty church spires across the peninsula, along with the halligen islands to the north. The tower is only accessible on a guided tour, Wednesday to Monday, 11am–4pm, from Easter to late October.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Westerland is not pretty — it is functional: Sylt's 'capital' concentrates hotels, shops and the Hindenburgdamm rail terminal. It is a logistical base, not a destination in itself. The Weststrand that stretches for kilometres north and south, however, is genuine wilderness: wind, waves, and few crowds outside high season.

The Hindenburgdamm, the 11-km railway causeway that has connected Sylt to the mainland since 1927, is the island's only land link. Cars are loaded onto flat wagons — the car-train. Watching one pass from the beach at Morsum or the port at List gives a vivid sense of Sylt's deliberate isolation.

St. Severin in Keitum is the real Romanesque church of Sylt — built in the 12th century in local sandstone, flanked by a mariners' churchyard with granite crosses. Keitum is also the best-preserved village on the island, with its thatched houses. It is 5 km east of Westerland by bike or bus line 1.

activities.text: Cycle the Weststrand from Westerland towards Wenningstedt or Kampen — the cycling paths follow either the Wattenmeer on the east side or the sea on the west. Hire a bike in town: €15–20 a day. The eastern route through the Braderup heath is less known and wilder.

The North Frisian wind tears words from your mouth and snaps the flags — a constant on Sylt. Use it as an excuse to watch kitesurfers at Brandenburger Strand, 3 km north of Westerland: conditions are consistent there and the riders are spectacular.

Book a car-train ticket on the Hindenburgdamm to cross to Niebüll on the mainland — a 40-minute passage at rail level with the dunes and the Wattenmeer, perfect at sunset. Online booking is mandatory via DB Sylt Shuttle, especially at weekends in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Wetzlar Cathedral is famous for what it isn't: finished. The second Gothic tower never rose beyond its base, for lack of money after plague and 15th-century wars. The result is a strange asymmetric façade mixing Romanesque and Gothic. Free admission.

In 1772, young Goethe arrived in Wetzlar for an internship at the Reichskammergericht. He fell for Charlotte Buff, engaged to another. The Lottehaus on Pfaffengasse tells this story that became 'The Sorrows of Young Werther.' Charming small museum, 30-minute visit.

Walk down to the Lahn via Pfaffengasse and Kornmarkt. Goethe lived at Kornmarkt during his stay. The half-timbered houses, small squares, and walls of the former imperial city can be covered in an hour. Six museums cluster in the old town.

activities.text: At Leitz-Park, the Leica world displays photography history since the invention of the Leica I in 1925. Galleries, workshops, boutique. The contemporary building contrasts with the Fachwerk downtown. Bus from Domplatz or 15 min by car.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: The Kurhaus of Wiesbaden is the city's emblem. Inaugurated in 1907 under the impetus of Wilhelm II—who made Wiesbaden his spring residence and actively contributed to its development—this neoclassical building with its colonnade houses a casino (one of Germany's oldest, said to have inspired Dostoevsky's novel The Gambler), twelve salons, and a grand concert hall. It opens onto the Kurpark, landscaped in the 19th century in English style with a lake and fountains.

The Hessisches Staatstheater of Wiesbaden, inaugurated in 1894 in neo-Baroque style, is considered one of Germany's most beautiful theaters. It hosts opera, ballet, and drama. The Maifestspiele festival—founded in 1896 for Wilhelm II's visits—attracts internationally renowned opera productions each year.

Ascend the Neroberg summit (245 m) via the Nerobergbahn, a water-powered funicular inaugurated in 1888—one of the oldest still operating in Germany. At the summit, the Russian Orthodox Church of Saint Elizabeth hides among the trees with its five golden domes. It was built between 1897 and 1899 by Duke Adolf of Nassau in memory of his wife Elisabeth Romanova, daughter of Grand Duke Michael of Russia, who died in childbirth at age 19 on January 27, 1845. The princess's coffin still rests there. The view from the Monopteros—a temple-shaped belvedere—extends across the entire city and Rhine valley.

The Museum Wiesbaden, founded in 1819, brings together modern and contemporary art collections under one roof—including one of the world's largest collections of works by Alexej von Jawlensky, the Expressionist painter who lived in Wiesbaden—along with natural history collections featuring pieces from the Duchy of Nassau.

The Rheingau vineyards stretch across approximately 3,100 hectares between the Taunus and the Rhine's northern bank, around Rüdesheim am Rhein (35 km west of Wiesbaden). Riesling has been cultivated here since the Middle Ages—the first Johannisberg vineyard dates to 817. The south-facing slopes overlooking the Rhine and the thermal shadow of the Taunus create an exceptional microclimate. In Rüdesheim, the Niederwald cable car leads to the Germania statue, erected in 1883 to commemorate the founding of the German Empire.

The Upper Middle Rhine Valley, between Bingen (opposite Rüdesheim) and Koblenz, has been a World Heritage Site since 2002 for its cultural landscapes of vineyards, castles, and villages. Along 65 km, the river corridor is dotted with rocky outcrops, medieval ruins, and terraced slopes—a classic route that can be explored on foot, by bicycle, or by boat.

activities.text: Wine tasting of Rheingau wines at one of the wine bars in Wiesbaden's center—the city itself lies within the appellation. Riesling represents 80% of production; Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) occupies most of the remaining parcels.

The Rheingauer Weinwoche (Rheingau Wine Week) takes place every year in early August on Wiesbaden's Schlossplatz. Over 100 winemakers present their wines on the castle forecourt. It's one of Hesse's oldest and most popular wine festivals.

days_plan.itin_title: Hesse Germany Tour: Cities and Natural Parks
From the Rheingau to Bayreuth - 12 Days in Germany

visits.text: From outside, a simple country church with pastel walls set in a meadow. Inside, the Wieskirche is a rococo explosion: stucco, gilding, ceiling frescoes in shades of pink and blue. A UNESCO site since 1983, it draws visitors from around the world. Free entry.

In 1738, a peasant woman claimed to see tears flowing from a statue of Christ's eyes. This "miracle of tears" triggered a pilgrimage, and the Zimmermann brothers built the Wieskirche between 1745 and 1754. Dominikus Zimmermann loved his work so much he settled next door until his death.

activities.text: Take the time to sit for ten minutes on a bench in the Wieskirche. The light streaming through the windows changes with the hour. Early morning, before the tour groups, the silence is total. On-site parking (paid).

days_plan.itin_title: Allgäu & Wieskirche: 9 days baroque road trip

visits.text: More than 530 burial mounds across 30 hectares of heathland: the Pestruper Gräberfeld is the largest Bronze Age necropolis in north-central Europe. The funerary urns date from approximately 800 to 200 BC. The site has free access, parking on the K248.

Continue to the Alexanderkirche in Wildeshausen's historic center, the only basilica in the Oldenburger Land. The medieval Stadtwall that surrounds it can be walked in twenty minutes. Have a look at the Gothic-gabled Rathaus while you're passing by.

activities.text: If you come between mid-August and early September, the flowering heather transforms the Gräberfeld into a purple carpet. In the morning, fog clings to dew-covered cobwebs: guaranteed spectacle for photographers.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles

visits.text: The locals simply call it 'the summit' — at 169 metres, Wilseder Berg is the highest point on the north-west German plain. On a clear day, Hamburg is visible 40 km away. You arrive on foot from Niederhaverbeck (about 4.5 km) or from Wilsede: cars are banned throughout the nature reserve.

Wilsede, a car-free village off-limits to tour coaches, looks like a fairy-tale set: half-timbered houses, sandstone walls, chickens roaming freely on cobbled paths. The Heidemuseum Dat ole Huus preserves 19th-century moorland peasant life — the attached Milchhalle serves a decent buckwheat cake.

activities.text: Walk the marked heathland trails around Wilseder Berg along the Heidschnuckenweg, the long-distance path that crosses the entire reserve. The curly-fleeced Heidschnucken sheep have been the moorland's gardeners for centuries: without them, the heather would revert to forest. Best time: August, when everything turns violet.

days_plan.itin_title: Schleswig-Holstein: Heathland, North Frisia and Sylt

visits.text: Ski resort in winter, heath trail in summer: Winterberg changes face with the seasons. The Kahler Asten, at 841 meters, is North Rhine-Westphalia's second-highest summit. An Astenturm with weather station has crowned the peak since 1895. The 36-hectare Hochheide surrounding it is a protected peat bog remnant dating from 1965.

activities.text: Climb the Kahler Asten via the Rothaarsteig from Winterberg (5.5 km, 1h30 walk). The trail cuts through spruce forest before emerging onto the bare summit heath. Parking at the summit if you prefer to drive.

In clear weather, from the Astenturm, the gaze extends to the Wasserkuppe in the Rhön and the Großer Feldberg in the Taunus. In winter, snow holds an average of 120 days per year. The Berggasthof at the summit serves regional dishes.

days_plan.itin_title: From the Bergisches Land to the Lahn: 11 days road trip

visits.text: The Wasserkunst stands in the center of Wismar's Marktplatz like a Dutch pavilion dropped there by mistake. This Renaissance bronze pavilion was built between 1579 and 1602 by Philipp Brandin and supplied the city with drinking water until 1897. Wismarians make it their emblem. The Marktplatz is one of northern Germany's largest, bordered by the Alter Schwede dating from around 1380.

St.-Nikolai-Kirche, consecrated in 1459, impresses with its central nave 37 m high. Built starting in 1381 to honor sailors and fishermen, it survived World War II almost intact, making it one of the region's best-preserved medieval buildings. Free entry.

The Alter Schwede leans slightly—it's intended, or rather time intended it. This Gothic brick facade dates from around 1380, and the name (the 'Old Swede') comes from a 19th-century tavern installed there in memory of Swedish occupation. Wismar was under Swedish rule from 1648 to 1803, a period of which the Zeughaus (Karl XII's baroque arsenal) bears witness.

activities.text: Walk along the Grube, a medieval canal dug in the 13th century to supply the city with fresh water, for its two kilometers through the old town. This canal is the last inner medieval moat preserved in a northern German old town. The walk passes former mills and brick facades.

If you have a stomach for smoked fish, try the Bückling—whole hot-smoked herring—in one of the Old Port stalls. Wismar's port has been active since the Middle Ages and still hosts reconstructed Kogge vessels. The Poeler Kogge Wissemara offers sailing excursions in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: German Baltic Coast in 12 days: Hanseatic Towns and Rügen
Baltic Palaces & Coastal Gems: 13 Days in Mecklenburg

visits.text: Autostadt is a 69-acre theme park opened in 2000, built along the Mittelland Canal for the Hanover World Expo. It showcases the Volkswagen Group's brands in carefully designed pavilions. Its most spectacular attraction: two 48-meter glass towers, fully automated, storing up to 800 new vehicles connected to the factory by a 700-meter tunnel. Nearly 175,000 customers come here each year to collect their cars. The ZeitHaus museum traces automotive history from the Ford Model T to the Beetle.
The Kunstmuseum Wolfsburg, inaugurated in 1994, is one of northern Germany's most active contemporary art museums, featuring major temporary exhibitions in a building designed by Peter Schweger.

Wolfsburg Castle is first mentioned in 1302—a Wasserburg, or moated castle, former residence of the noble Bartensleben family. When the British renamed the town in May 1945, they chose this Weser Renaissance castle to erase the name "Stadt des KdF-Wagens" imposed by Hitler in 1938.

activities.text: Walk along the Mittelland Canal with an overview of the Volkswagen facilities: the chimneys of the factory founded in 1938 and the two glass delivery towers form a recognizable industrial skyline.

days_plan.itin_title: Lower Saxony, Germany: Harz Mountains, Heathland and North Sea Islands
Road Trip Around Hanover: Lower Saxony's Most Beautiful Sites

visits.text: Marvel at Wörlitz Park, one of Germany's most beautiful landscape gardens and jewel of the Dessau-Wörlitz Garden Realm, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This 18th-century gardening masterpiece spans 112 hectares of canals, artificial islands, and romantic vistas. Neoclassical pavilions, picturesque bridges, and Greek temples create an idealized landscape inspired by Prince Leopold III's travels to Italy and England.

Visit Wörlitz Palace, Germany's first neoclassical building, whose refined interiors house remarkable art and antiquities collections. St. Peter's Church, harmoniously integrated into the park landscape, completes this architectural ensemble of exceptional aesthetic coherence. Gondolas gliding along the canals offer a unique perspective on this earthly paradise designed according to Enlightenment ideals.

activities.text: Boat ride along the park canals for a romantic perspective

days_plan.itin_title: Saxony-Anhalt's Heritage Trail: Bauhaus, Luther & Harz Mountain Legends

visits.text: A farming village turned artists' colony in 1889: Worpswede owes everything to Fritz Mackensen, captivated by the light of the Teufelsmoor. In 2026, the four museums celebrate Paula Modersohn-Becker's 150th birthday with interconnected exhibitions. Plan on spending the morning.

The low light of the Teufelsmoor filters through the windows of the Barkenhoff, a former farmhouse transformed into a Gesamtkunstwerk by Heinrich Vogeler as early as 1895. The Art Nouveau garden is the most photographed spot in the village. The Heinrich Vogeler Museum occupies the interior.

Hoetger's Niedersachsenstein divides visitors: an expressionist brick sculpture perched on the Weyerberg, some find it brilliant, others hideous. Climb up for the view over the peat bogs. Free access, five minutes' walk from the center.

activities.text: Rent a bike and pedal through the Teufelsmoor on dirt paths between drainage canals. The flat, misty landscape explains why the painters stayed. Count on 90 minutes for the classic loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Bremen to Münster: Peat Bogs, Canals and Castles
